events.title: Inauguration
events.description: George Washington inaugurated as the first President of the United States in New York City, the nation's capital.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Inauguration

events.title: Congress enacts tariff
events.description: Congress, led by Representative James Madison, enacts the first protective tariff. Madison consulted with President Washington about the need for the measure.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress enacts tariff

events.title: First naturalization law
events.description: Congress passes the United States' first naturalization law, establishing terms of citizenship.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First naturalization law

events.title: Ratifying the Constitution
events.description: Rhode Island ratifies the Constitution, becoming the last of the original thirteen states under the Articles of Confederation to join the newly formed Union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ratifying the Constitution

events.title: Copyright law
events.description: President Washington signs the first United States copyright law.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Copyright law

events.title: Establishing the capital
events.description: President Washington signs a bill into law that permanently places the nation's capital along the Potomac River, in an area to be called the District of Columbia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Establishing the capital

events.title: Revolutionary War debts
events.description: President Washington signs a bill into law that directed the federal government to assume the Revolutionary War debts of the states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Revolutionary War debts

events.title: Moving the capital
events.description: The United States Capital officially moves from New York to Philadelphia, where it remains until the completion of the District of Columbia in 1800. Capital Moves to Philadelphia On December 6, 1790, the United States Capital officially moved from New York City to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The capital remained in Philadelphia until 1800 when it permanently settled in Washington, D.C. The United States government spent its first year (1789-1790) under the Constitution in the city of New York. During much of the preceding confederation period (1776-1787), however, Congress had resided in Philadelphia. Upon the formation of a new national government under the Constitution, the city campaigned vigorously for the federal government to return. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Moving the capital

events.title: Creating a national bank
events.description: Secretary of the Treasury Alexander Hamilton, with President Washington's support, sends Congress a controversial message (The Report on a National Bank) calling for the creation of an official Bank of the United States. After a hard-won approval by Congress, Washington signs the bill on February 25, 1791.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Creating a national bank

events.title: First revenue law
events.description: Congress approves its first internal revenue law, creating fourteen revenue districts and placing a tax on all distilled spirits.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First revenue law

events.title: Naming the capital
events.description: Commissioners name the territory within the District of Columbia (and the future seat of the Federal Government) the city of Washington in honor of the nation's first President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Naming the capital

events.title: Fighting in Miami
events.description: The Miami Indians soundly defeat an American military force of 1400 men led by General Arthur St. Clair at the cost of 900 American lives. The Washington Administration had sent St. Clair to the Ohio country with the hope that his presence would clear the way for American settlers.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Fighting in Miami

events.title: Ratifying the Bill of Rights
events.description: The states officially ratify the first ten amendments to the Constitution, also known as the Bill of Rights. President Washington had called for their ratification in his first inaugural address.
events.impact: Guaranteed fundamental individual freedoms

events.title: Appointing Thomas Pinckney
events.description: President Washington appoints Thomas Pinckney as the first United States minister to England. Washington instructs him to convey a spirit of “sincere friendship” and to seek the liberation of American commerce from British regulations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Appointing Thomas Pinckney

events.title: The President's mansion
events.description: The cornerstone for the President's mansion is laid in Washington D.C.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The President's mansion

events.title: Washington reelected
events.description: George Washington is unanimously re-elected President of the United States by the Electoral College. John Adams is elected for a second term as Vice President.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Proclaiming neutrality
events.description: President Washington issues a proclamation of neutrality, warning Americans to avoid aiding either side in the emerging conflict between Britain and revolutionary France.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Proclaiming neutrality

events.title: Receiving french envoy
events.description: President Washington cautiously receives France's envoy to the United States, Edmond Charles Genet. Washington fears that Genet wants to enlist U.S. aid in the conflict between Britain and France. Washington Receives “Citizen Genet” On May 18, 1793, President George Washington received the French minister to the United States, Edmond Charles Genet. Known as “Citizen Genet,” the minister had come to the United States to try to gain U.S. support for France. He arrived in the country in April 1793 and journeyed to Philadelphia, stopping to celebrate along the way with adoring, supportive crowds. France and the United States had maintained friendly relations since signing an alliance in 1778. When the French Revolution turned violent in 1792, however, many Americans re-evaluated that friendship. Republicans, including Thomas Jefferson, sympathized with the revolution, seeing it as an emulation of America's own freedom struggle. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Receiving french envoy

events.title: Straining relations with Britain
events.description: American relations with Britain begin to deteriorate rapidly after the British government issues secret orders for the Royal Navy to confiscate any vessels trading with French possessions in the Caribbean. The Royal Navy seizes more than 200 American ships.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Straining relations with Britain

events.title: Jefferson resignation
events.description: Thomas Jefferson resigns as secretary of state. President Washington appoints Edmund Randolph as his successor.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson resignation

events.title: Warships
events.description: Congress responds to British aggression by authorizing the production of six warships (March 11) and announcing a sixty-day embargo on American shipping (March 26). The Washington administration supports both measures.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Warships

events.title: Special envoy to Britain
events.description: In the hopes of quelling mounting tensions between the United States and Britain, Washington selects Chief Justice of the Supreme Court John Jay to serve as a special envoy to Britain, in the hope that he can broker a deal with the British government to improve relations and address American grievances.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special envoy to Britain

events.title: Farmers' rebellion
events.description: Farmers in western Pennsylvania rebel over the strict enforcement of an excise tax on whiskey passed in 1791. President Washington issues a proclamation on August 7 ordering the insurgents to return home. When this fails, he calls up more than 12,000 militiamen and dispatches them to Pennsylvania, whereupon the insurrection dissolves.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Farmers' rebellion

events.title: Battle of Fallen Timbers
events.description: General Anthony Wayne defeats an Indian force numbering more than 1,000 at the Battle of Fallen Timbers. The victory helps open the Ohio territory for American settlement and is a defeat for Britain, which had allied with the Native Americans in the region.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Battle of Fallen Timbers

events.title: Jay Treaty
events.description: On November 19, 1794, American statesman John Jay signed the Amity, Commerce, and Navigation Treaty with Britain. The treaty, now known as Jay's Treaty, was designed to resolve issues between the United States and Britain. Although the treaty left some important issues unresolved and its ratification divided politicians in the young federal government, it successfully allowed the United States to avoid war with its more powerful adversary, Britain. In the 1790s, the United States was struggling to assert both its political and economic independence, and the new nation encountered difficulties in foreign relations when its two primary trading partners, Britain and France, went to war yet again. President George Washington sought to follow a policy of strict neutrality, allowing American merchants and ships to trade with both countries while aiding neither in their war efforts. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jay Treaty

events.title: Hamilton resignation
events.description: Alexander Hamilton resigns from his post as secretary of the treasury. Washington appoints Oliver Wolcott to replace Hamilton.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hamilton resignation

events.title: Ratifying the Jay Treaty
events.description: After fierce public debate, the Senate ratifies the Jay Treaty. President Washington signs the treaty on August 14.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ratifying the Jay Treaty

events.title: Treaty of San Lorenzo
events.description: The United States signs the Treaty of San Lorenzo with Spain, granting Americans the right to ship goods through the port of New Orleans without having to pay duties to the Spanish Government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of San Lorenzo

events.title: Paying the Dey
events.description: The American Government, represented by David Humphreys, agrees to pay a sum of nearly a million dollars to the Dey of Algiers for protection of American shipping in the Mediterranean and for the ransom of sailors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Paying the Dey

events.title: Tension concerning Jay Treaty
events.description: A heated dispute erupts between President Washington and his Federalist allies and Democrat-Republicans in the House of Representatives after the latter demand that the President provide Congress with all papers relating to the Jay Treaty. Washington refuses their demands. America's forgotten founding father
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tension concerning Jay Treaty

events.title: Tennessee becomes a state
events.description: Congress grants Tennessee's application for statehood, allowing it to become the 16th state in the Union.
events.impact: Expanded the Union

events.title: Diplomatic crisis
events.description: France informs James Monroe, America's leading diplomat in Paris, that the Jay Treaty violates, and therefore suspends, certain provisions of the 1778 Treaty of Amity and Commerce between the two nations. This begins a serious diplomatic crisis between France and the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Diplomatic crisis

events.title: Washington says farewell
events.description: President Washington releases what has become known as his “Farewell Address,” in which he advises future American leaders to minimize “political connection” with foreign powers. Washington's Farewell Address On September 19, 1796, newspapers around the country published President George Washington’s Farewell Address. In his address, Washington summarized his presidential tenure, cautioned against political divisions, and advised future American leaders to minimize connections with foreign powers. President Washington, after nearly eight years as the nation’s first President, determined that he would not accept a third term in office. By this time, political divisions between Alexander Hamilton on one side and James Madison and Thomas Jefferson on the other had hardened into proto-political parties. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington says farewell

events.title: Treaty with Tripoli
events.description: In an agreement resembling the one signed with the Dey of Algiers in 1795, the American government signs a treaty with Tripoli, agreeing to pay a yearly tribute to the Pasha of Tripoli in exchange for the peaceful treatment of U.S. shipping in the Mediterranean region.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Treaty with Tripoli

events.title: John Adams elected
events.description: John Adams is elected President of the United States. Thomas Jefferson, the candidate with the second highest electoral vote, becomes vice president.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: France rejects envoy
events.description: France refuses to accept Monroe's replacement, Charles Cotesworth Pinckney, as American envoy to France, worsening relations between the two nations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: France rejects envoy

events.title: John Adams inaugurated
events.description: John Adams is inaugurated as the second President of the United States, thereby officially ending the presidency of George Washington. Washington retires to his home at Mount Vernon.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Inauguration
events.description: John Adams is inaugurated as the second President of the United States in Philadelphia. Thomas Jefferson will serve as Vice President.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Special session
events.description: Adams calls the first special session of Congress to debate the mounting crisis in French-American relations. Charles Cotesworth Pinckney, the American envoy in France, had left France after being insulted by the French foreign minister.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Negotiating with France
events.description: Adams appoints a three man commission, composed of Charles C. Pinckney, Elbridge Gerry, and John Marshall, to negotiate a settlement with France.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Making a militia
events.description: President Adams is authorized by Congress to raise a militia of 80,000 men for defensive purposes in case of war with France.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: XYZ Affair
events.description: The three man American peace commission is received coolly and then asked to pay a bribe in order to speak with French Foreign Minister Charles Maurice Talleyrand. This episode becomes known as the “XYZ Affair.” John Adams - The XYZ Affair On October 18, 1797, three Americans who were sent to France by President John Adams to represent a U.S. peace commission, were received coolly and then asked to pay a bribe in order to speak with French Foreign Minister Charles Maurice Talleyrand. This episode became known as the “XYZ Affair,” after the French agents who met with the American delegation. The incident affected U.S. relations with France and damaged the Democratic-Republican Party because of its traditional pro-French stance. When France broke diplomatic ties with the United States in 1796, incoming President John Adams organized a delegation to negotiate with the French government. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: The Eleventh Amendment
events.description: The Eleventh Amendment to the Constitution of the United States is declared in full force by President Adams. It stipulates that federal courts shall not have the jurisdiction over litigation between individuals from one state against individuals from another state.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: XYZ Affair exposed
events.description: President Adams exposes the XYZ affair, providing Congress with letters from the peace commission indicating French efforts to bribe and intimidate U.S. officials seeking to speak with French diplomat, Charles Maurice Talleyrand. The reaction was one of outrage and intimidation.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Mississippi Territory
events.description: Congress establishes the government for the new Mississippi Territory. The Spanish had ceded the territory to the United States in the 1795 Treaty of San Lorenzo. President Adams appoints native Winthrop Sergeant as governor and selects the town of Natchez to serve as its first capital.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Appointing Benjamin Stoddert
events.description: Adams appoints Benjamin Stoddert to serve as the first secretary of the Navy for the newly formed Department of the Navy. Congress had established the department four days earlier in preparation for war with France.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Preparing for war
events.description: Congress empowers Adams to enlist 10,000 men for service in case of a declaration of war or invasion of the country's domain. It also authorizes Adams to instruct commanders of ships-of-war to seize armed French vessels praying upon or attacking American merchantmen about the coast.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Alien and Sedition Acts
events.description: The first of four acts known collectively as the Alien and Sedition Acts is adopted. The Alien and Sedition acts aimed to curb criticism of administration policies and prevent internal subversion. The first act, stipulating requirements for naturalized citizenship, demanded residence in the United States for period of fourteen years and a declaration of intention for five years. Congress Approves the First Alien and Sedition Act On June 18, 1798, Congress approved the first of four acts that collectively became known as the Alien and Sedition Acts. These four acts became the most bitterly contested domestic issue during the presidency of John Adams. The Alien and Sedition Acts consisted of four different pieces of legislation. [truncated]
events.impact: Restricted free speech and immigration

events.title: Passing the Alien Act
events.description: Congress passes the Alien Act, granting President Adams the power to deport any alien he deemed potentially dangerous to the country's safety.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Alien Enemies Act
events.description: Congress passes the third of the Alien and Sedition acts, the Alien Enemies Act. The act provides for the apprehension and deportation of male aliens who were subjects or citizens of a hostile country.
events.impact: Restricted free speech and immigration

events.title: Washington appointed commander in chief
events.description: Adams appoints George Washington to serve as commander in chief of the United States Army. All French treaties between the United States and France are declared null and void by vote in Congress, most notably the 1778 Treaty of Alliance.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Sedition Act
events.description: Congress adopts the Sedition Act, the fourth and last of the Alien and Sedition acts. The bill subjects any American citizen to a fine and/or imprisonment for obstructing the implementation of federal law, or for publishing malicious or false writings against Congress, the President, or the government.
events.impact: Restricted free speech and immigration

events.title: Franklin's grandson arrested
events.description: Philadelphia newspaper editor Benjamin Franklin Bache, grandson of Benjamin Franklin, is arrested under Sedition Act for “libeling” President Adams.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Kentucky Resolutions
events.description: The Kentucky State Legislature adopts the Kentucky Resolutions, reserving states' right to override federal powers not enumerated in the U.S. Constitution. Thomas Jefferson, angry at the Adams administration for the Alien and Sedition acts, authors the resolution.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Victory against France
events.description: The United States Navy scores its first clear victory against France when the frigate Constellation captures the French ship L'Insurgente near the island of St
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Victory against France

events.title: New envoys
events.description: President Adams selects Van Murray, Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth, and North Carolina Governor Davie to serve as U.S. envoys to France upon assurance from the French that they will be received with the respect owed to their nation.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Treaty of Amity
events.description: diplomats conclude a Treaty of Amity between the United States and Prussia in Berlin.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Amity

events.title: Convicting Thomas Cooper
events.description: Thomas Cooper, a resident of Northumberland, Pennsylvania, is tried and convicted of libel against President Adams and his administration under the newly adopted Sedition Act.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Treaty with Tunis
events.description: Congress finally passes a treaty with Tunis, negotiated originally in 1797.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty with Tunis

events.title: The Constellation and La Vengeance
events.description: The United States frigate Constellation defeats the French ship La Vengeance on the high seas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Constellation and La Vengeance

events.title: Federal Bankruptcy Act
events.description: Congress passes and Adams signs into law the Federal Bankruptcy Act, providing merchants and traders protection from debtors.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Establishing the Library of Congress
events.description: A resolution is passed and eventually signed by President Adams calling for the establishment of a Library of Congress.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Dividing the Northwest Territory
events.description: Congress passes an act dividing the Northwest Territory into two parts, with the border between them running north from the junction of the Ohio and Kentucky Rivers. The western part of the territory will be known as the Indiana Territory while the eastern half will retain the name Northwest Territory.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dividing the Northwest Territory

events.title: Washington becomes capitol
events.description: The new city of Washington in the District of Columbia becomes the official capital of the United States, succeeding Philadelphia. It would not be until November that Congress convened in the new capital and Adams moved into the new Executive Mansion. John Adams - Washington Becomes U.S. Capital On June 11, 1800, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, ceased to be the capital of the United States, as the new city of Washington in the District of Columbia became the country's official capital. The federal government moved its offices to Washington, D.C., in June. In November, President John Adams first slept in the unfinished Executive Mansion (now known as the White House) and Congress met for the first time in the U.S. Capitol building. In 1790, Congress passed “An Act for Establishing the Temporary and Permanent Seat of the Government of the United States,” commonly known as the Residence Act. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Treaty of Mortfontaine
events.description: The “quasi”-naval war with France effectively ends with the signing of the Treaty of Mortfontaine in Paris. France agrees to lift its embargos on American ships, cancel all letters of marque, and respect neutral ships and property. The United States agrees to return captured warships but not captured privateers.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Treaty of Mortfontaine

events.title: Treaty of San Idlefonso
events.description: Spain cedes the Louisiana territory to France with the signing of the secret Treaty of San Idlefonso. Leaders express alarm because the French could be a potentially dangerous enemy in the region.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of San Idlefonso

events.title: Adams loses reelection
events.description: The fourth presidential election is held. Adams, the Federalist Party candidate, loses his bid for reelection. A tie in electoral votes between Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr throws the election into the House of Representatives, with Jefferson emerging the winner.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Jefferson becomes President
events.description: Thomas Jefferson is inaugurated as the third President of the United States, becoming the first President to be inaugurated in Washington, D.C. John Adams's term as President officially ends.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Inauguration
events.description: Thomas Jefferson is inaugurated as the third president of the United States, becoming the first president inaugurated in Washington, D.C. Aaron Burr, who had tied Jefferson in electoral votes before losing the election in the House of Representatives, is inaugurated Vice President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Inauguration

events.title: Tripoli declares war
events.description: Yusuf Karamini, pasha of Tripoli, declares war on the United States by symbolically cutting down the flagpole at the U.S. This action came after the United States refused to pay more tribute to the Tripolitans in exchange for protection from piracy against American ships.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tripoli declares war

events.title: William C.C. Claiborne appointed governor
events.description: William C.C. Claiborne is appointed the new territorial governor of Mississippi.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William C.C. Claiborne appointed governor

events.title: Jefferson addresses Congress
events.description: President Jefferson delivers his first address to the newly convened seventh Congress of the United States in writing and is read aloud by the House clerk. Expressing his dislike for ceremony, Jefferson establishes the precedent, not broken until the presidency of Woodrow Wilson, of not delivering the State of the Union address in person.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson addresses Congress

events.title: The United States and Britain convene
events.description: The United States and Britain conclude a convention regarding Jay's Treaty of 1794 to resolve some outstanding issues about details in the treaty. A commission rules that the United States owes £600,000 to British citizens in settlement of Revolutionary War claims.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The United States and Britain convene

events.title: War with Tripoli
events.description: Congress recognizes the War with Tripoli, authorizing the arming of merchant ships to ward off attacks.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: War with Tripoli

events.title: Reducing army and establishing West Point
events.description: Congress reduces the size of the U.S. army to its 1796 limits. It also passes an act, which is signed into law by Jefferson, establishing an official United States Military Academy at West Point.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Reducing army and establishing West Point

events.title: Excise taxes repealed
events.description: Infamous excise taxes on commodities such as whiskey are repealed.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Excise taxes repealed

events.title: Naturalization laws repealed
events.description: The notorious naturalization laws of 1798 are repealed. The required length of residency reverts from fourteen years to five years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Naturalization laws repealed

events.title: Georgia's western territory ceded
events.description: The Georgia legislature cedes to the United States its western territory, notorious for the Yazoo land fraud of 1795.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Georgia's western territory ceded

events.title: Enabling Act
events.description: President Jefferson signs the Enabling Act, establishing procedures under which territories organized under the Ordinance of 1787 can become a state. The law effectively authorizes people of the Ohio territory to hold a convention and frame a constitution.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Enabling Act

events.title: Washington incorporated
events.description: Congress officially incorporates Washington as a city, empowering Jefferson to appoint the mayor.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington incorporated

events.title: The United States and Spain resolve to refer all d…
events.description: The United States and Spain resolve to refer all disputes between the two countries to a special convention at Madrid.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The United States and Spain resolve to refer all d…

events.title: Monroe appointed minister to France and Spain
events.description: Jefferson appoints James Monroe minister to France and Spain, instructing him to purchase New Orleans and East and West Florida. Napoleon informs U.S. minister in Paris Robert Livingston that France will be willing to sell the entire Louisiana territory, much to his surprise.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Monroe appointed minister to France and Spain

events.title: Ohio becomes a state
events.description: Ohio officially becomes the seventeenth state of the Union. It is the first state to prohibit slavery by law at its inception.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ohio becomes a state

events.title: Marbury v. Madison Decided
events.description: On February 24, 1803, the Supreme Court of the United States issued its unanimous ruling in Marbury v. Madison , one of the most important Supreme Court cases in early American history. This ruling established for the first time the principle that the Supreme Court can declare an act of Congress void if it is inconsistent with the Constitution. This landmark case established the basis for judicial review of congressional and executive actions on the grounds of their constitutionality. Thomas Jefferson's election as President in 1800 came after a bitter partisan struggle between Federalists and Republicans. Republicans won both the presidency and a majority in Congress. Before leaving early in 1801, the Federalist Congress passed a new Judiciary Act that created new judgeships, which enabled outgoing President John Adams to appoint numerous additional Federalists to the judiciary. On his last day in office, Adams worked late into the night signing commissions for new judgeships. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: New Orleans opened
events.description: Spain reopens New Orleans to American merchants.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New Orleans opened

events.title: Louisiana Purchase
events.description: Livingston and Monroe are sent to conclude a treaty for the acquisition of New Orleans, but instead conclude a treaty for the purchase of the entire Louisiana Territory. This day marks the official signing of a peace treaty with France and the purchase of Louisiana. The addition of 828,000 square miles of land between the Mississippi and the Rocky Mountains is purchased from France for approximately $15 million, increasing the national territory by 140 percent. Louisiana Purchase Treaty Signed On April 30, 1803, representatives from the United States and France signed the Louisiana Purchase Treaty. The terms of the agreement gave all of the Louisiana territory from the Mississippi River to the Rocky Mountains to the United States. The Louisiana Purchase doubled the size of the United States and is considered one of President Thomas Jefferson's greatest presidential accomplishments. [truncated]
events.impact: Doubled the size of the nation peacefully

events.title: Jefferson commissions Commodore Edward Preble as c…
events.description: Jefferson commissions Commodore Edward Preble as commander of a U.S. Navy squadron sent to battle Tripoli.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Jefferson commissions Commodore Edward Preble as c…

events.title: Lewis and Clark
events.description: Captain Meriwether Lewis, formerly Jefferson's personal secretary, sets out from Pittsburgh to begin an expedition of the newly acquired western territory of the Louisiana Purchase. Lewis will pick up Captain William Clark to serve as co-leader of the trip early in the next year. Jefferson sponsored the journey out of personal scientific curiosity and concern for the economic and political security of the western United States.
events.impact: Doubled the size of the nation peacefully

events.title: The Twelfth Amendment
events.description: Motivated by the infamous election of 1800, Congress passes the Twelfth Amendment to the Constitution, requiring electors to vote for President and vice president separately. This ends the tradition of the runner up in a presidential race becoming vice president and prevents chances for a deadlock tie.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Twelfth Amendment

events.title: Raising the American flag
events.description: The French flag is lowered in New Orleans and the U.S. flag raised, symbolizing the transfer of the Louisiana territory from France to the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Raising the American flag

events.title: Philadelphia burned
events.description: Stephen Decatur burns the captured U.S. frigate Philadelphia while docked in Tripoli harbor. Tripolitan gunboats had captured the frigate during the previous October. No one is killed.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Philadelphia burned

events.title: Louisiana Territory Act
events.description: Congress passes the Louisiana Territory Act, dividing the Louisiana Purchase into the Territory of Orleans in the south and the district of Louisiana in the north.
events.impact: Doubled the size of the nation peacefully

events.title: Burr Kills Hamilton in Duel
events.description: On July 11, 1804, Aaron Burr and Alexander Hamilton met for duel, and Burr shot and fatally wounded Hamilton, who died the next day. The Burr-Hamilton duel stands as a vivid example that in the early republic partisan politics were also highly personal politics. Hamilton and Burr had been political adversaries long before their famous duel. Hamilton, an arch-Federalist and President George Washington's secretary of the Treasury strongly distrusted his fellow New Yorker, and he worked against Federalist efforts to elect Burr over Thomas Jefferson, both Republicans, when they tied in the presidential election of 1800. Burr had served various positions in New York politics and then became Jefferson's vice president in 1801. Republicans felt he was unreliable, however, and dropped him in the next election. In 1804, Burr was running for governor of New York, and Hamilton was leading the opposition to Burr's candidacy; he spoke out against him and questioned his integrity in public. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Burr Kills Hamilton in Duel

events.title: Ratifying the Twelfth Amendment
events.description: The Twelfth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution is officially ratified, allowing for the presidential election of 1804 to be conducted under new rules.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ratifying the Twelfth Amendment

events.title: Election of 1804
events.description: The fifth presidential election is held under the auspices of the newly ratified Twelfth Amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Election of 1804

events.title: Jefferson reelected
events.description: Thomas Jefferson is officially reelected President of the United States. Fellow Democratic-Republican and first governor of New York George Clinton will be the vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson reelected

events.title: Inauguration
events.description: President Jefferson is inaugurated for his second term. George Clinton officially succeeds Aaron Burr as vice president. In his inaugural address, Jefferson proposes that Federalist-inspired internal taxes be completely eliminated.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Inauguration

events.title: Lewis and Clark reach the Yellowstone River
events.description: Lewis and Clark reach the mouth of the Yellowstone River.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lewis and Clark reach the Yellowstone River

events.title: Capturing Derna
events.description: Marines and Arab mercenaries capture the Tripolitan port city of Derna, achieving a major victory for the United States in the Tripolitan War. Eaton's ultimate plan, approved by President Jefferson, entailed replacing the ruling pasha of Tripoli with the rightful ruler. This is aborted with the forthcoming peace treaty in June.
events.impact: Ended armed conflict

events.title: Treaty of Peace and Amity in Tripoli
events.description: The United States and Tripoli sign a Treaty of Peace and Amity in Tripoli, effectively ending the Tripolitan War.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Peace and Amity in Tripoli

events.title: Aaron Burr arrives in New Orleans
events.description: Rumors circulate about the subversive activities of Aaron Burr as he arrives in New Orleans. These include plans to establish a separate country with New Orleans as its capital and a plot to invade Mexico.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Aaron Burr arrives in New Orleans

events.title: British seize ships
events.description: The British justify seizure of American ships in neutral ports with the invocation of the Rule of 1756.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: British seize ships

events.title: Lewis and Clark reach the Pacific
events.description: Lewis and Clark reach the Pacific after a perilous journey of nearly eighteen months and 4,000 miles.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lewis and Clark reach the Pacific

events.title: Jefferson addresses Congress regarding Florida
events.description: Jefferson makes two addresses, one public and one before Congress, regarding land in Florida. In the public address, Jefferson cites the need to prepare for war with Spain. Privately, Jefferson informs Congress of secret negotiations with France in order to buy the territory from them and asks for five million dollars to be appropriated. The request receives a controversial response from Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson addresses Congress regarding Florida

events.title: Michigan is formed
events.description: Michigan is formed from the territory of Indiana.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Michigan is formed

events.title: Building a national road
events.description: Congress authorizes a commission to build a national road from Cumberland, Maryland, to the Ohio River.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Building a national road

events.title: Prohibiting British imports
events.description: In protest against the seizure of American ships and the impressment of American sailors by Britain, Congress passes a law prohibiting the importation of many British products into the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Prohibiting British imports

events.title: Pike explores the Southwest
events.description: Zebulon Pike begins his exploration of what is now the southwestern United States. On November 15, Pike explores the famous 18,000-foot peak that still retains his name in what is now Colorado.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Pike explores the Southwest

events.title: Envoys commence talks with Britain
events.description: American envoys James Monroe and William Pinckney commence talks with British official Lord Holland on the current naval hostilities.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Envoys commence talks with Britain

events.title: Lewis and Clark Expedition Arrives Back in St. Louis
events.description: On September 23, 1806, Meriwether Lewis and William Clark arrived back in St. Louis two-and-a-half years after they began their expedition to explore the Louisiana Territory and the Pacific Northwest. President Thomas Jefferson commissioned the expedition, which is often considered one of the greatest exploratory quests in U.S. Well before President Jefferson purchased the Louisiana Territory from France, Americans had been curious about the lands west of the Mississippi River. Jefferson saw the West as a great collection of scientific specimens and a vast expanse that enhanced American security, but he also shared the commercial interest of American traders looking for a viable route to the Pacific Ocean. Even before negotiations to purchase New Orleans had commenced, Jefferson planned an expedition to the Pacific Northwest. In January 1803, the President asked Congress for a secret appropriation of $2,500 for the secretly planned exploration. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lewis and Clark Expedition Arrives Back in St. Louis

events.title: Congress passes military organization
events.description: Congress passes legislation providing for a military organizational structure.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress passes military organization

events.title: Jefferson warns Americans
events.description: In Washington, D.C., President Jefferson publicly warns citizens not to take part in a plot to invade Spanish territory. Jefferson issues this warning after having been told of Aaron Burr's subversive activities with respect to annexing Spanish territory.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson warns Americans

events.title: Jefferson asks for ban on slave trade
events.description: Jefferson appeals to Congress asking for a ban on the slave trade.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jefferson asks for ban on slave trade

events.title: Aaron Burr arrested
events.description: Aaron Burr is arrested near Fort Stoddart, Alabama, in connection with his alleged conspiracy against the government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Aaron Burr arrested

events.title: Importation of slaves prohibited
events.description: At Jefferson's behest, Congress passes a law prohibiting the importation of slaves into any place within the jurisdiction of the United States after January 1, 1808.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Importation of slaves prohibited

events.title: The Embargo Act
events.description: The Embargo Act, modified and authorized by President Jefferson, now permits vessels to transport American goods from foreign ports.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Embargo Act

events.title: The Leopard incident
events.description: The infamous Leopard incident occurs. The British ship Leopard fires upon the United States frigate Chesapeake in Chesapeake Bay after the latter's commander, James Barron, refuses to surrender four British deserters on board. Many on the U.S. frigate are killed and wounded.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: The Leopard incident

events.title: Aaron Burr acquitted
events.description: Circuit court in Richmond acquits Aaron Burr of treason.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Aaron Burr acquitted

events.title: British sea aggression
events.description: In spite of Thomas Jefferson's vehement protest, the British government announces it will continue to impress seamen on American ships thought to be British.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: British sea aggression

events.title: Britain issues "Order in Council"
events.description: Britain issues its “Order in Council,” forbidding neutral nations and her allies from trading with France except under tribute to England.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Britain issues "Order in Council"

events.title: The Milan Decree
events.description: Napoleon issues the Milan Decree, forbidding trade with England or her colonies under penalty of confiscation and impressments of any vessel paying tribute to Britain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Milan Decree

events.title: President Jefferson signs the Embargo Act
events.description: President Jefferson signs the Embargo Act, putting a halt to all trading with any country in the entire world. The act serves as a retaliatory measure to the increasingly coercive trade policies of the British and the French.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Jefferson signs the Embargo Act

events.title: Slave trade ban official
events.description: The law officially banning the slave trade goes into effect.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Slave trade ban official

events.title: The Second Embargo Act
events.description: The Second Embargo Act comes into force. It is more stringent than the first and is commonly known as the “O grab me Act.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Second Embargo Act

events.title: The Bayonne Decree
events.description: Napoleon issues the Bayonne Decree, authorizing the French seizure of all U.S. vessels entering French and Italian ports and all ports of the Hanseatic League. Napoleon conveniently argues that his action helps the United States enforce its new policy prohibiting trade with other nations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Bayonne Decree

events.title: Sixth Presidential election
events.description: The sixth presidential election for President of the United States is held.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sixth Presidential election

events.title: James Madison elected
events.description: James Madison is elected president of the United States, with George Clinton as vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: James Madison elected

events.title: Congress repeals the Embargo Act
events.description: After the U.S. economy suffers at the hands of the embargo, Congress repeals the Embargo Act. Jefferson signs the Non-Intercourse Act the same day, closing U.S. ports only to France and England. Trade with the two countries is to be resumed when they agreed to respect the rights of U.S. citizens and vessels.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Congress repeals the Embargo Act

events.title: James Madison inaugurated
events.description: James Madison is inaugurated as the fourth President of the United States, thereby ending Jefferson's presidency. Jefferson retires to his home at Monticello outside Charlottesville, Virginia, to assume a private life.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: James Madison inaugurated

events.title: The 1808 presidential election
events.description: Congress announces the results of the 1808 presidential election. Jefferson's secretary of state, Republican-Democrat James Madison, emerges victorious. Madison swamps Federalist opponent Charles C. Pinckney in electoral votes, 122 to 47. Pinckney carries only five states -- all of them in New England -- to Madison's twelve.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The 1808 presidential election

events.title: Refurbish White House
events.description: Congress authorizes $12,000 to refurbish the White House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Refurbish White House

events.title: Inauguration
events.description: James Madison is inaugurated as the fourth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Inauguration

events.title: Erskine Agreement
events.description: After negotiations with British minister Erskine, Madison issues a proclamation -- known as the Erskine Agreement -- revoking the embargo on Britain, effective June 10. For his part, Erskine leads Madison to believe that Britain will revoke its Orders in Council. On March 25, however, the American envoy in Britain learns that British foreign secretary Canning has canceled the Erskine Agreement; news reaches Madison six weeks later. On August 9, Madison rescinds his proclamation establishing trade with Britain and resumes a policy of nonintercourse.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Erskine Agreement

events.title: Secretary of Treasury
events.description: Madison persuades Albert Gallatin to remain secretary of treasury in the face of strong congressional opposition and discord within Madison's cabinet.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of Treasury

events.title: West Florida tensions
events.description: Prompted by tensions with Spain over West Florida, Madison calls for renewal of an act authorizing the President to call out 100,000 militiamen, fill up the regular army to its authorized strength, establish a force of 20,000 volunteers for immediate emergencies, and reactivate idle components of the naval fleet.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: West Florida tensions

events.title: Fletcher v. Peck
events.description: John Marshall overrules state legislation in Fletcher v. Peck , finding attempts to rectify the Yazoo land fraud scheme a violation of contract rights. Madison determines to add Republicans to the court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Fletcher v. Peck

events.title: Macon's Bill Number 2
events.description: To replace the Nonintercourse Act, Congress passes Macon's Bill Number 2, which allows American ships to carry French or English goods while barring belligerent powers from American ports. The bill further promises to renew nonintercourse with one of the two belligerent nations if the other withdraws its decrees. Trade with France and Britain is restored so long as the European nations respect American trade rights.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Macon's Bill Number 2

events.title: Cadore letter
events.description: The Cadore letter notifies the American minister in France that the Decrees of Berlin and Milan will be repealed, effective November 1, if Britain revokes its Orders in Council or if the United States bars trade with Britain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cadore letter

events.title: Occupation of West Florida
events.description: Madison issues a proclamation authorizing occupation of West Florida, also claimed by Spain, as part of the Louisiana Purchase. Proclamation to Occupy West Florida On October 27, 1810, President James Madison issued a proclamation that authorized the U.S. occupation of West Florida, which included land from the Perdido River west along the Gulf Coast to the Mississippi River. Although the President issued the proclamation in October, he did not inform Congress until his annual message in December. Many Americans, including former President Thomas Jefferson and Madison himself, thought that West Florida was included as part of the Louisiana Purchase. The Louisiana Purchase itself implied that West Florida might in fact be a part of the deal, and Jefferson pressed his claim against Spain. Much to his chagrin, in 1804 France insisted West Florida had not been part of the purchase. [truncated]
events.impact: Doubled the size of the nation peacefully

events.title: Trade wars
events.description: Under the terms of Macon's Bill Number 2, Madison accepts a French offer to stop confiscation of American supplies and ships. In February 1811, he declares a halt in trade with Britain unless the Orders are repealed. Undeterred, Britain vows to continue to seize American ships until France ends its trade restrictions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Trade wars

events.title: Nonintercourse with Britain
events.description: Madison reestablishes nonintercourse with Britain. Meanwhile, the French continue their seizure of American ships.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nonintercourse with Britain

events.title: Presidential vetoes
events.description: In February, Madison vetoes two bills of Congress, one on the 21st incorporating an Episcopal church in Washington, D.C. and the other on the 28th granting land in the Mississippi Territory to a Baptist congregation. Madison argues that both bills violate the non-establishment clause of the First Amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Presidential vetoes

events.title: Bank of the United States
events.description: The Bank of the United States closes. Treasury Secretary Gallatin urges Congress to extend its charter but fails to convince members concerned with the large British interest in the Bank.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bank of the United States

events.title: Secretary of State
events.description: After Madison dismisses Secretary of State Robert Smith, James Monroe accepts Madison's offer of the cabinet position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of State

events.title: Attitudes toward Britain
events.description: Editor Gales, of the National Intelligencer , prints a summary of his discussion with Madison. The talks indicate that Madison has hardened his attitude toward Britain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Attitudes toward Britain

events.title: Battleship President
events.description: After it is attacked, the U.S. battleship President fires on the British ship HMS Little Belt .
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Battleship President

events.title: Address to the People of the United States
events.description: Former secretary of state Robert Smith publishes an Address to the People of the United States , attacking Madison's administration and revealing the disagreements within the cabinet.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Address to the People of the United States

events.title: New British foreign minister
events.description: The new British foreign minister, Foster, arrives in Washington and warns Madison that if nonintercourse remains the policy of the United States, Britain will retaliate against American commerce.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New British foreign minister

events.title: Special session of Congress
events.description: Madison calls a special session of Congress to convene November 4 in preparation for war against Britain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session of Congress

events.title: War message
events.description: Madison delivers a tentative war message to Congress, indicating his shift in policy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: War message

events.title: Battle of Tippecanoe
events.description: After acknowledging the danger posed by Shawnee leader Tecumseh, who hopes to assemble a confederation of tribes, General William Henry Harrison, the governor of the Indian Territory, carriers out a pre-emptive strike on Tecumseh. Harrison's militia is barely successful at the Battle of Tippecanoe, an engagement that serves as a prelude to the War of 1812. Tecumseh flees to Canada and British protection. On December 18, Madison proclaims the Battle of Tippecanoe a victory that will restore peace to the northwestern frontier.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: James Monroe
events.description: The Senate confirms James Monroe as secretary of state, replacing Robert Smith.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: James Monroe

events.title: Growing the armed forces
events.description: The House Committee on Foreign Relations recommends legislation to bring the Army up to full strength, establish a second regular army of 10,000, enable the President to organize 50,000 volunteers, strengthen the Navy, incorporate militia units into national service, and arm merchantmen.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Growing the armed forces

events.title: Army bill
events.description: Congress passes an Army bill to enlarge the second regular army to 25,000. The increase in manpower is far greater than Madison's request -- he had asked for a force of 10,000 -- but the bill provides less flexibility than Madison had requested. Amidst disagreements between Madison's administration and Congress, modifications are made to the legislation over the summer.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Army bill

events.title: U.S. Navy
events.description: The House refuses to enlarge the Navy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. Navy

events.title: Relations with Canada
events.description: Madison shares the letters of John Henry, agent for governor of Canada, with Congress, having purchased the letters the previous month for $50,000. The documents indicate that the governor general of Canada is inciting rebellion in New England.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Orders in Council continued
events.description: British minister Foster tells Madison's administration that the Orders in Council will be continued.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Orders in Council continued

events.title: France sinks U.S. ships
events.description: News arrives that France has sunk American ships carrying flour to British troops in Spain, leading many in Congress to call for war against France. The incident is seen by many as “proof” that France has not repealed edicts against American neutral commerce.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: France sinks U.S. ships

events.title: Congressional embargo
events.description: Congress passes an embargo, effective through July 4, on all shipping to give shippers the opportunity to get their vessels to safe haven.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congressional embargo

events.title: Louisiana
events.description: Louisiana is admitted to the nation as the eighteenth state of the Union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Louisiana

events.title: Nominee
events.description: Amidst fierce intra-party competition, Madison is nominated by the Democratic-Republicans for a second term as President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nominee

events.title: Drafting War Message
events.description: Madison sees the letter from Lord Castlereagh to British minister Foster confirming the continuance of the Orders in Council, and the President begins drafting his war message to Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Drafting War Message

events.title: War message
events.description: Madison delivers a message to Congress, justifying war against Britain and asking for a declaration of war. On June 4, the House of Representatives votes 79-49 for war against Britain. On June 16, Britain revokes its Orders in Council in an attempt to avoid war with the United States, but news of the British decision will reach the United States too late. On June 17, Senate votes 19-13 for a declaration of war.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: War message

events.title: Declaration of war
events.description: Madison issues a declaration of war against Britain. In addition to concern over British actions with regard to international trade, some proponents of war also endorse territorial expansion into British Canada and Spanish Florida; they also hope to end suspected British support of Indian attacks. Without the Bank of America and with an Army of only 6,700, the United States faces dire economic and military straits at the war's outset. Navy, with a fleet of only sixteen vessels, delivers the nation's only victories in the first year of war.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Size of militias
events.description: General Henry Dearborn, commanding American forces into Canada, requests that all New England governors cap the size of militias guarding their respective coasts and frontiers; Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island refuse.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: End of Orders in Council
events.description: The British foreign secretary announces an end to the Orders in Council. The announcement comes too late, however, as Madison requested a declaration of war against Britain on June 1.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: End of Orders in Council

events.title: Armistice with Lower Canada
events.description: Dearborn signs an armistice with the governor of Lower Canada. Madison repudiates it the following week, and Dearborn terminates the armistice on August 25.
events.impact: Ended hostilities

events.title: Surrender of Detroit
events.description: Michigan governor and general William Hull, in charge of the American offensive from Detroit into Upper Canada, gives up his attack on Fort Malden and surrenders Detroit to British forces on August 16 without firing a shot.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Madison reelected
events.description: Despite fierce competition and conflict within the Democrat-Republican party, Madison wins reelection, securing 128 electoral votes to Federalist DeWitt Clinton's 89. The electoral results indicate a divide within the nation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Madison reelected

events.title: Secretary of the Navy
events.description: William Jones of Pennsylvania replaces Paul Hamilton as secretary of the Navy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of the Navy

events.title: Secretary of War
events.description: John Armstrong of New York replaces William Eustis as secretary of war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of War

events.title: Winchester's battle
events.description: Americans throughout the northwest are outraged by Winchester's battle and surrender at Frenchtown, and the Wyandotte murder of sixty Kentucky prisoners of war. The northwest ceases to play a role in war strategy.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Winchester's battle

events.title: Peace negotiations
events.description: Albert Gallatin and James A. Bayard leave to join John Quincy Adams in St. Petersburg for peace negotiations sponsored by Russia.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Albert Gallatin
events.description: John Quincy Adams, James A. Bayard, and Albert Gallatin are nominated as representatives to the peace negotiations, but the Senate rejects Gallatin on July 19. Gallatin is eventually confirmed after the Senate forces Madison to declare Gallatin's treasury cabinet post vacant.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Battle of Put-In-Bay
events.description: In an impressive display of valor, Captain Oliver Perry wins control of Lake Erie in the Battle of Put-In-Bay. For four hours, Perry's flagship, the Lawrence , receives heavy attacks from two British warships, leaving most of his crew dead or wounded. Instead of surrendering, however, Perry rows to another ship and launches an attack on the British, finally accepting surrender of the entire British fleet. Perry sends word to General Harrison, stating, “We have met the enemy and they are ours.”
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of Put-In-Bay

events.title: Battle of Thames
events.description: The United States under General Harrison emerges victorious at the Battle of the Thames -- the most important American victory to date -- as it ends British and Indian control in Northwest and Upper Canada. Tecumseh dies in the battle.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Total embargo
events.description: Madison calls for a total embargo on exports and a ban on all imports of British origin, believing that Britain depends on trade with the United States. Congress passes the embargo just days later.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Total embargo

events.title: Amendment for national bank
events.description: James Jackson of Virginia introduces a constitutional amendment in the House authorizing the establishment of a national bank, but Congress postpones consideration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Amendment for national bank

events.title: Negotiators to Britain
events.description: Madison appoints Henry Clay, Albert Gallatin, James Bayard, Jonathan Russell, and John Quincy Adams as commissioners to negotiate directly with Britain in Gothenburg, Sweden. These negotiations are later transferred to Ghent, Belgium.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Campbell appointed Secretary of Treasury
events.description: George Washington Campbell of Tennessee replaces Gallatin as Secretary of the Treasury.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Campbell appointed Secretary of Treasury

events.title: War costs
events.description: Congress authorizes the borrowing of $25 million to finance war costs from the War of 1812.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: War costs

events.title: Battle of Horseshoe Bend
events.description: Under the command of Andrew Jackson, 2,000 troops defeat the Creek Confederation at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend in the Tallapoosa River, eliminating the Confederation as an obstacle to American expansion toward the Gulf Coast. The engagement is one of the most significant American victories in the War of 1812, providing the United States with two-thirds of Creek land in the Treaty of Fort Jackson.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of Horseshoe Bend

events.title: Napoleon's European empire collapses
events.description: Napoleon's European empire collapses. Learning of Napoleon's defeat, Madison calls for an immediate repeal of the trade embargo with neutral nations, signaling a major reassessment of American war aims and strategy. He signs the bill into law on April 14. The British, meanwhile, can now turn their complete attention to war with the United States.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Napoleon's European empire collapses

events.title: Andrew Jackson becomes Major General
events.description: William Henry Harrison resigns as Major General and is replaced by Andrew Jackson, against Madison's orders.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Andrew Jackson becomes Major General

events.title: Invasion of Canada
events.description: Madison and his cabinet decide to continue with the attempted invasion of Canada.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Special military district established
events.description: Madison convenes his cabinet to establish a special military district for the protection of Washington and Baltimore, placing it under the command of Brigadier General William Winder.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Special military district established

events.title: The Battle of the Burnt Corn
events.description: The Battle of the Burnt Corn in the Mississippi Territory brings the Creek Indians into the war against the United States.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: The Battle of the Burnt Corn

events.title: British burn Washington
events.description: With momentum on their side, and in retaliation for the torching of Canadian Parliament buildings, British forces attack and burn Washington, D.C., setting the White House, the Capitol, and other federal buildings ablaze. British Troops Set Fire to White House On August 24, 1814, during the War of 1812, British troops invaded Washington, D.C., and set the White House, the Capitol, and other federal buildings on fire. The city had been evacuated before the British arrived with President James Madison and his administration leaving the capital city to flee the invading soldiers. During the war, British and American troops clashed up and down the East Coast, from Canada down to New Orleans. The war began after President Madison requested a declaration of war from Congress to protect American ships on the open seas and to try to stop the British practice of impressments, the seizure of U.S. sailors for service in Britain's Navy. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Following the sack of Washington, U.S. General Sam…
events.description: Following the sack of Washington, U.S. General Samuel Smith turns back a British attack on Fort Henry at Baltimore. After the unsuccessful British offensive, Francis Scott Key pens “The Star-Spangled Banner.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Following the sack of Washington, U.S. General Sam…

events.title: Monroe appointed Secretary of War
events.description: Madison nominates James Monroe as Secretary of War to replace John Armstrong. Monroe will serve as secretary of both war and state until the end of the war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Monroe appointed Secretary of War

events.title: Alexander J. Dallas appointed Secretary of Treasury
events.description: Alexander J. Dallas is appointed secretary of treasury, replacing the inept George W. On October 17, Dallas calls for Congress to establish a national bank to finance the war and to increase taxes. The Senate passes a new bank bill on December 9.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alexander J. Dallas appointed Secretary of Treasury

events.title: Convention of New England states
events.description: The Massachusetts General Court calls a convention of New England states, whose livelihood depends largely on international trade, to coordinate regional grievances against the federal government. From December 15 through January 5, delegates from some New England states meet in Hartford, Connecticut, to discuss grievances against the federal government and to provide alternative solutions to talk of secession among New England radicals.
events.impact: States breaking from the Union

events.title: Jackson takes Pensacola, FL
events.description: Without authorization, Andrew Jackson takes Spanish-held and British-occupied Pensacola, Florida, in pursuit of Creek warriors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jackson takes Pensacola, FL

events.title: Hartford Convention issues condemnation
events.description: Twenty-two delegates at the Hartford Convention issue a report condemning the federal government for failing to defend New England. The report recommends that states negotiate arrangements with the federal government for their defense, and proposes constitutional amendments to protect the region's increasingly minority status in the Union. Following news of Jackson's victory at New Orleans, the U.S. public condemns the Hartford Convention as anti-American. The Federalist Party suffers as a result.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hartford Convention issues condemnation

events.title: Treaty of Ghent
events.description: In Europe, the United States and Britain sign the Treaty of Ghent. News of the Treaty will reach the United States in February 1815.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Ghent

events.title: Bank bill vetoed
events.description: The House of Representatives passes an amended bank bill as a compromise between Federalists and anti-bank Republicans. The bill is nevertheless unsatisfactory to Madison and Secretary of the Treasury Dallas. Madison vetoes the bank bill on January 30.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bank bill vetoed

events.title: Battle of New Orleans
events.description: Jackson, leading 4,000 militiamen, citizens, and regular soldiers, wins a resounding victory over 6,000 British forces in the Battle of New Orleans. Many of Jackson's troops are volunteers, among them free blacks and slaves. There are just a dozen American casualties to 2,000 British casualties. Jackson's victory, along with his success against the Creeks, makes him a national hero.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of New Orleans

events.title: Calling up state troops
events.description: Madison signs a bill allowing the President to call up 40,000 state troops. Congress has limited the bill, however, by authorizing troops to serve only in their home states with the consent of state governors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Calling up state troops

events.title: End of the War of 1812
events.description: News arrives of the December 1814 Treaty of Ghent that ends the War of 1812. On February 15, Congress appropriates $500,000 for the reconstruction of federal buildings. The Senate ratifies the Treaty of Ghent on February 16.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: End of the War of 1812

events.title: War on Algiers
events.description: With Madison having secured a declaration of war on Algiers, Captain Stephen Decatur leads a flotilla from New York against the Mediterranean pirates, who attack American ships during the War of 1812. Algiers surrenders on June 30.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: War on Algiers

events.title: Convention with Britain
events.description: Gallatin negotiates a commercial convention with Britain, further signifying the potential for the United States to play an important role in international trade and industrialization.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Convention with Britain

events.title: Madison's seventh message to Congress
events.description: Madison presents his seventh annual message to Congress, advocating military streamlining, a new national bank, protective tariffs to promote industry, and internal improvements.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Madison's seventh message to Congress

events.title: Re-chartering a national bank
events.description: Madison signs a bill re-chartering a new national bank in Philadelphia. The charter is set for a twenty-one year term.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Re-chartering a national bank

events.title: Indiana becomes a state
events.description: Madison signs a bill admitting Indiana to statehood.
events.impact: Expanded the Union

events.title: Monroe elected President
events.description: Secretary of State James Monroe is elected President, easily defeating Federalist Rufus King of New York. Monroe receives 183 electoral votes to King's 34.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Monroe elected President

events.title: Madison's eighth message to Congress
events.description: Madison delivers his eighth annual address to Congress, calling for vigilance in foreign affairs, internal improvements, and the restructuring of the judiciary and executive offices.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Madison's eighth message to Congress

events.title: "Bonus Bill" vetoes
events.description: On his last day in office, President James Madison vetoes Representative Henry Clay's “Bonus Bill” for internal improvements.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Bonus Bill" vetoes

events.title: Rush-Bagot Agreement
events.description: Britain's minister to the United States, Charles Bagot, agrees to the conditions of the Rush-Bagot Agreement. Following negotiations, acting Secretary of State Richard Rush sends the document to Britain in August 1816. This is the final version of a treaty that Monroe, while secretary of state under Madison, negotiated with British foreign minister Robert Stewart Castlereagh. The agreement limits naval capacity on the Great Lakes; in doing so, it alleviates possible tension between the two nations following the War of 1812. Each country is held to one ship on Lakes Champlain and Ontario, and two ships on all the other lakes. Limits are also placed on ship tonnage and armaments.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Rush-Bagot Agreement

events.title: Monroe tours New England
events.description: Monroe embarks on a lengthy, sixteen-week tour of New England. In the absence of his major cabinet appointees, Monroe uses the tour to foster a sense of national unity through local political contact, public appearances, and private meetings with opposing Federalists. The tour gives birth to the designation of Monroe's administration as the “Era of Good Feelings.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Monroe tours New England

events.title: Aguirre Mission
events.description: Monroe enunciates a policy of neutrality towards the Latin American colonies seeking independence. He also advocates a controversial fact-finding mission, the Aguirre Mission, to Buenos Aires that could be construed as recognition for the colony's sovereignty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Aguirre Mission

events.title: Mississippi becomes a state
events.description: Mississippi becomes the twentieth state in the Union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mississippi becomes a state

events.title: Seminole Indian uprisings
events.description: Secretary of War John C. Calhoun orders General Andrew Jackson to quell Seminole Indian uprisings in the Floridas and southern Georgia; Jackson also receives a private letter from Monroe urging such action. In March 1818, Jackson pursues the Seminoles into Spanish Florida -- where he suspects they are receiving assistance -- takes the fort of St. Marks on March 6, forces the surrender of Fort Carlos de Barrancas, and executes, among others, a Scot Indian trader and a British lieutenant. After capturing the Spanish capital in May, Jackson returns to Tennessee.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Seminole Indian uprisings

events.title: Jackson, Florida and Spain
events.description: Monroe learns of Jackson's exploits and, along with his cabinet (except John Quincy Adams), disapproves of Jackson's actions. Following protests from the ministers of Britain, Spain, and France, Monroe concedes that Jackson's behavior in Pensacola amounted to acts of war. The President repudiates Jackson and orders that Pensacola be handed back to Spain. Meanwhile, Adams, in a July letter, supports Jackson's tactics, blaming Spain for its inability to control the Indians. Despite his concession, Monroe recognizes that Jackson's activities in Florida provide the United States with a favorable strategic position for negotiations with Spain.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Anglo-American Convention
events.description: British and American diplomats meet at the Anglo-American Convention and conclude a treaty resolving some, but not all, of the outstanding issues from the War of 1812. The nations agree on a northern border of the Louisiana Purchase, fixed at the 49th parallel, from the Lake of the Woods to the Rocky Mountains. Britain also acknowledges U.S. fishing rights off Newfoundland and provides compensation for slaves who fled to British lines. The Rush-Bagot Agreement is formally signed.
events.impact: Doubled the size of the nation peacefully

events.title: Illinois becomes a state
events.description: Illinois is admitted as the twenty-first state of the Union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Illinois becomes a state

events.title: Alabama becomes a state
events.description: Alabama becomes the twenty-second state of the Union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alabama becomes a state

events.title: The Panic of 1819
events.description: The Panic of 1819 begins to take shape. A sharp decline in real estate values and a severe credit contraction (an inability to secure bank loans) inflates the currency and causes imports and prices to fall. In March, the price of cotton collapses in the English market. The conservative policies of the Second Bank of the United States, founded in 1816, accelerates the crisis, which ends around 1823.
events.impact: Economic crisis causing widespread suffering

events.title: Debates over Missouri's statehood
