events.description: The Senate acquits Secretary of War Belknap. While few Senators doubt his guilt, Belknap has already resigned, and therefore most Senators believe he is not constitutionally impeachable as a result.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Belknap acquitted

events.title: Disputed election results
events.description: The presidential election results are inconclusive. Tilden appears to have a 250,000 edge in the popular vote as well as 184 electoral votes to Hayes's 165. But the returns from Florida, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Oregon, representing 20 electoral votes, are disputed. The Disputed Election of 1876
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Disputed election results

events.title: Electoral commission established
events.description: Both parties in Congress agree to establish a commission to determine results of the contested presidential election. The commission will be composed of five members of each house of Congress and five members of the Supreme Court. The commission contains eight Republicans and seven Democrats.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Electoral commission established

events.title: Senate accepts Compromise of 1877
events.description: The Senate accepts the decision of the electoral commission, which awards the contested electoral votes and the presidency to Rutherford B. Hayes in an 8-7 vote along party lines. Though the House disagrees, the Senate's approval proves sufficient to justify the findings of the electoral commission; the House votes to accept the report.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Senate accepts Compromise of 1877

events.title: Hayes privately sworn in
events.description: Rutherford B. Hayes is privately sworn in as the nineteenth President of the United States. Because March 4 falls on a Sunday, he will be publicly sworn in on March 5.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes privately sworn in

events.title: Rutherford B. Hayes inaugurated
events.description: Republican Rutherford B. Hayes, previously a Union soldier, as well as a representative and governor of Ohio, is publicly sworn in as the nineteenth President of the United States. He takes the oath privately on March 3rd. In the race against Democrat Samuel Tilden, Hayes secures only 48 percent of the popular vote and 164 electoral votes to Tilden's 184. However, voter fraud and unclear results are reported in several states. A Special Electoral Commission is formed with members of the US Senate, House of Representatives, and the Supreme Court. It issues a controversial decision that proclaims Hayes as President, with some Democrats referring to Hayes as “Rutherfraud.” The Disputed Election of 1876
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Rutherford B. Hayes inaugurated

events.title: North and South relations
events.description: At a cabinet meeting, Hayes agrees to send a commission to Louisiana to report on the conditions in the southern state. In reality, he plans to use the action to sanction his decision to allow Democrat Francis T. Nicholls to take over the states by removing federal aid from federally appointed governor Stephen B. Meanwhile, Hayes's cabinet includes staunch liberal Republican William Evarts as Secretary of State and a former Confederate as postmaster general; the nomination of the latter appeases Southern Democrats as part of the Compromise of 1877.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North and South relations

events.title: Withdrawing from South Carolina
events.description: Troops depart the statehouse in South Carolina following a meeting at the White House with Daniel H. Chamberlain and Wade Hampton; without support, Chamberlain gives in, and Hampton becomes governor.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Withdrawing from South Carolina

events.title: Withdrawing from Louisiana
events.description: As in South Carolina, Hayes officially withdraws soldiers from Louisiana. Governor Packard has no choice but to submit, declaring, “One by one, the Republican state governments of the South have been forced to succumb to force, fraud or policy.” Hayes's withdrawal of troops from the South marks the end of Reconstruction. At the same time, Hayes will also oversee the appropriation of federal funds for internal improvements in the South.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Withdrawing from Louisiana

events.title: Patrolling Mexican border
events.description: With Mexican-Texas border incursions continuing, Hayes sends troops to patrol the nearly lawless Mexican border and cross it if necessary to pursue bandits. Mexican president Diaz protests and sends troops to the border as well. Ultimately, economic concerns motivate both parties to work towards a settlement.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Patrolling Mexican border

events.title: Civil service reform
events.description: Following John Jay's investigation of the New York Customhouse, Hayes issues an Executive Order that forbids the involvement of federal employees in political activities. The President takes such action in the hope that it will curtail corruption; the Executive Order stipulates that those in office can no longer be dismissed for political reasons. Congress rejects additional proposals. These events testify to Hayes's interest in civil service reform.
events.impact: Government corruption

events.title: Great Railroad Strike of 1877
events.description: Following pay cuts, the first major interstate strike -- the Great Railroad Strike of 1877 -- begins on the Baltimore and Ohio (B&O) line at Camden Junction, Maryland; additional strikes will follow, lasting a month. Lacking organization, the strikes frequently degenerate into mob activity. Hayes sends federal troops to protect mail and quell the riots that take place in numerous cities, angering many workers. The strike will lead to anti-Chinese attacks in San Francisco during the fall.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Great Railroad Strike of 1877

events.title: Hayes tours the South
events.description: Hayes goes on a tour of the South, pledging reconciliation and solidarity through a policy of pacification.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes tours the South

events.title: Hayes versus Conkling
events.description: Hayes challenges the political power of New York Senator Roscoe Conkling when the President announces he will replace Collector of the Port of New York Chester A. Arthur, as well as naval officer Alonzo Cornell, during the reorganization of the New York Customhouse. Although Arthur has overseen improvement in the Customhouse, he also uses it to further Conkling's political interests. Incensed by Hayes's decision, Conkling, Hayes's opponent for the Republican presidential ticket in 1876, blocks Hayes's nomination of Theodore Roosevelt, Sr., for the position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes versus Conkling

events.title: Knights of Labor
events.description: Founded in 1869 by Uriah S. Stephens, a tailor in Philadelphia, the Knights of Labor is established as a national organization. It is the first labor union to attempt to organize all workers and hopes to establish a worker-owned factory system. With rapid growth in the 1880s, the Knight membership peaks in 1886 and then goes into rapid decline.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Knights of Labor

events.title: U.S.-Samoa Treaty
events.description: A U.S.-Samoan treaty is signed in Washington which gives the United States the right to establish a naval and coaling station at the port of Pago Pago; it also pledges American assistance to Samoa if a third country interferes with Samoan chiefs. The Senate ratifies the treaty on January 30.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S.-Samoa Treaty

events.title: Hayes vetoes Bland-Allison Act, overridden
events.description: Hayes vetoes the Bland-Allison Act, advocated by farmers and debtors, but Congress passes the measure over his veto. The act calls for the resumption of silver coinage at a rate between $2 and $4 million per month.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes Bland-Allison Act, overridden

events.title: Hayes limits Chinese immigration
events.description: Hayes vetoes a bill which bans incoming vessels from carrying more than fifteen Chinese passengers. Hayes then works to negotiate changes to the Burlingame Treaty with China in order to set limits on Chinese immigration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes limits Chinese immigration

events.title: Diaz regime in Mexico
events.description: America recognizes the Diaz regime in Mexico in an effort to avoid greater conflict.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Diaz regime in Mexico

events.title: Investigating the election
events.description: House Democrats begin an investigation of the controversial presidential election of 1876, much to the chagrin of Hayes, who fears that the investigation may be an attempt to replace him with Tilden.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Investigating the election

events.title: Democrats control congress
events.description: Following congressional midterm elections, the Democratic Party controls both houses of Congress for the first time since the Civil War. Consequently, Hayes will have little sway in Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats control congress

events.title: Greenback buyback and recovering economy
events.description: Hayes allows the resumption of gold payments for Civil War greenbacks, paper money not backed by specie, silver, or gold. This is a continuation of the Specie Act begun under President Grant. During the Hayes administration, as the government's gold supply grows and the issuance of silver coins increases, the economy begins to recover. By the spring of 1879, the government has retired all Civil War bonds.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Greenback buyback and recovering economy

events.title: Senate approves Customhouse appointment
events.description: After a political struggle between Hayes and Senator Conkling, the Senate approves Hayes's appointments for the New York Customhouse. Although these fail to end inefficiency in the civil service system, the country largely supports Hayes's commitment to reform.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Senate approves Customhouse appointment

events.title: Army Appropriations Bill
events.description: Congress passes the Army Appropriations Bill. The law includes a “rider” which forbids the use of federal troops at polls, which many regard as an attempt to nullify black voting rights. Hayes vetoes the bill, but the House sustains the veto. Hayes again vetoes the rebuffed version, and many Republicans feel the veto secures the election of 1880.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Army Appropriations Bill

events.title: Hayes vetoes appropriations bill
events.description: Hayes vetoes a version of the appropriations bill for the third time; a later bill excludes “certain judicial expenses” forbidding the army to “police the polls”; Hayes will agree to this language.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes appropriations bill

events.title: Hayes vetoes appropriations
events.description: The appropriations designated by Democrats exclude implementation of election law funds; Hayes vetoes the bill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hayes vetoes appropriations

events.title: Hayes supports a canal
events.description: In a speech to Congress, Hayes continues to support a Central American canal to unite the Atlantic and Pacific oceans. Following the trip to America by French diplomat Ferdinand de Lesseps -- the builder of the Suez Canal in Egypt -- Hayes states that “the policy of this country is a canal under American control.” A canal running through Panama will be completed in 1914.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Hayes supports a canal

events.title: RNC nominates James A. Garfield
events.description: The Republican National Convention meets in Chicago. Hayes had already pledged, in 1876, not to run for a second term; by the time the convention begins, the party has split into two factions: James G. Blaine's “Half-breeds” and Roscoe Conkling's “Stalwarts.” The Stalwarts nominate Ulysses S. Grant against the nominations of Blaine and John Sherman. After 36 ballots, Blaine unexpectedly lends his support to James A. Garfield, the Speaker of the House, giving Garfield the presidential nomination. In order to maintain party unity, Chester A. Arthur, a Stalwart, is nominated for vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates James A. Garfield

events.title: DNC nominates Winfield S. Hancock
events.description: The Democratic National Convention meets in Ohio and nominates Winfield S. Hancock, a Union commander, for President and William H. English for vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates Winfield S. Hancock

events.title: James A. Garfield elected
events.description: Garfield is elected President by a narrow popular margin (with only 48.5 percent) but with a comfortable majority of electoral votes, 214-155.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: James A. Garfield elected

events.title: The U.S. and China
events.description: The United States and China sign a treaty which repeals a section of the 1868 Burlingame treaty. The move gives the United States the power to “regulate, limit or suspend” but not completely prohibit Chinese immigration. The treaty also includes a clause banning the opium trade. In return, the United States grants China trading privileges.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The U.S. and China

events.title: Garfield Inaugurated
events.description: A self-made man, Republican James A. Garfield is sworn in as the twentieth President of the United States. Garfield served in the Union Army and in the House of Representatives before running for President. Winning the popular election by an very small margin -- only 10,000 votes -- Garfield owes his success partially to New York political boss and Senator William Conkling. In return, Garfield pledges to consult Conkling on government nominations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Inaugurated

events.title: Garfield selects his cabinet
events.description: Garfield completes his slate of cabinet members, naming James G. Blaine as Secretary of State and Abraham Lincoln's son, Robert, as Secretary of War. Garfield angers Conkling with his nomination of William Windom of Minnesota, a non-Eastern man, as Secretary of Treasury. Further, Garfield denies Conkling influence in New York politics by appointing William H. Robertson as collector of the port of New York and Thomas L. James postmaster of New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield selects his cabinet

events.title: Garfield sends nominations to Senate
events.description: Garfield sends his list of nominations to the Senate, which includes New York senator Conkling's contingent. Conkling will continue to be a source of conflict for the President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield sends nominations to Senate

events.title: Garfield removes E. A. Meritt
events.description: Garfield removes E. Meritt from the collectorship of the New York Customhouse after Conkling feels assured that the President would not make any such changes. Garfield then sends W. Robertson's name to the Senate as his replacement, intensifying the struggle between Garfield and Conkling.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield removes E. A. Meritt

events.title: Filibuster begins
events.description: A Democratic filibuster, which ties up the Senate beginning March 23, ends when Garfield agrees to remove certain appointments. The end of the filibuster allows Garfield to push for Robertson's confirmation to the New York Customhouse. Earlier, Senator Conkling threatens to publish the Hubbell letter, which appears to link Garfield to the Star Route Scandal, a scheme to skim money from the U.S. Post Office. The link is not a significant one, however, and the publication of the letter proves more damaging to Conkling than Garfield.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Filibuster begins

events.title: Garfield removes nominations
events.description: On the eve of the senatorial vote on the New York nominees, Garfield learns that Conkling intends to delay action on other nominees and moves for adjournment before Robertson can be considered. Garfield removes all of his nominations with the exception of Robertson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield removes nominations

events.title: New York senators resign
events.description: New York senators Roscoe Conkling and Tom Platt resign to protest Garfield's removal of New York nominees to secure Robertson's confirmation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New York senators resign

events.title: The Senate confirms Robertson
events.description: The Senate confirms Robertson as collector of customs for the port of New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Senate confirms Robertson

events.title: American Association of the Red Cross
events.description: Clara Barton organizes the American Association of the Red Cross, modeled after the International Red Cross, in Washington, D.C. Barton serves as the organization's volunteer president until 1904.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American Association of the Red Cross

events.title: Garfield Shot
events.description: President Garfield is shot in a D.C. train station by Charles Julius Guiteau, an unstable Stalwart attorney who had been denied a consular post. “I am a stalwart,” Guiteau proclaims. “Arthur is now President of the United States.” President James Garfield Shot On July 2, 1881, President James Garfield was shot by Charles J. Guiteau as he walked through the Baltimore & Potomac Railroad station with Secretary of State James Blaine. Wounded after only four months in office, the President died from his wounds on September 19, 1881, and Vice President Chester A. Arthur was sworn in as President. President Garfield had never paid a great deal of attention to securing himself from possible assassination, likening the possibility of the event to the risk of being struck by lightning-all but impossible to prevent and thus pointless to worry about. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Shot

events.title: Normal School for Colored Teachers established
events.description: Established in 1880, the Normal School for Colored Teachers, now Tuskegee University, officially opens its doors in Tuskegee, Alabama. Washington serves as the school's first president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Normal School for Colored Teachers established

events.title: Garfield Dies
events.description: James Garfield dies from blood poisoning and complications after surgeons search endlessly to find the lost bullet in his back, lodged in his pancreas. Vice President Chester A. Arthur becomes the twenty-first President of the United States The assassin, Guiteau, will be hanged on June 30, 1882.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Garfield Dies

events.title: Arthur Becomes Vice President
events.description: Arthur takes office as vice president in the presidential administration of James Garfield.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Becomes Vice President

events.title: James Garfield dies
events.description: James Garfield dies from blood poisoning and complications after surgeons search endlessly to find the lost bullet in his back, lodged in his pancreas. Vice President Chester A. Arthur becomes the twenty-first President of the United States The assassin, Guiteau, will be hanged on June 30, 1882.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: James Garfield dies

events.title: Arthur Becomes President
events.description: Arthur is sworn in as President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Becomes President

events.title: Arthur Inaugurated
events.description: Arthur formally takes the oath of office in Washington, DC.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur Inaugurated

events.title: Guiteau Trial Begins
events.description: The murder trial of Charles Guiteau begins. He will be convicted on January 25, 1882, and executed on June 30, 1882.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Guiteau Trial Begins

events.title: Secretary of State Resigns
events.description: Secretary of State James G. Blaine resigns due to political differences between himself and President Arthur.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of State Resigns

events.title: Congress Ties Representation to Census
events.description: Congress passes a bill mandating the use of the census for determining congressional representation, a move which increases the number of representatives in Congress to 325.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress Ties Representation to Census

events.title: Star-Route Scandal
events.description: Nine men are indicted for defrauding the government in a postal scam, an episode that becomes known as the Star-Route Scandal; the trial begins on June 1.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Star-Route Scandal

events.title: Geneva Convention Ratified
events.description: The Senate ratifies the Geneva Convention of 1864 for the care of wounded war personnel.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Geneva Convention Ratified

events.title: Edmunds Act Passes
events.description: Congress passes the Edmunds Act, which excludes bigamists and polygamists from voting and holding office, and establishes a five-man “Utah commission” to supervise voting in the territory of Utah.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Edmunds Act Passes

events.title: Chinese Exclusion Act vetoed
events.description: Arthur vetoes the first Chinese Exclusion Act, which would have banned the immigration of Chinese laborers for twenty years and denied American citizenship to current Chinese residents; the veto greatly angers labor groups, who feel increasingly threatened by the influx of Chinese labor.
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Chinese Exclusion Act revised
events.description: A revised version of the Chinese Exclusion Act, which reduces the period of non-immigration to ten years but maintains the ban on Chinese citizenship, becomes law. The act will be renewed regularly into the twentieth century.
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Arthur appoints Tariff Commission
events.description: Arthur approves a bill to appoint a tariff commission; the commission eventually recommends tariff reductions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur appoints Tariff Commission

events.title: U.S. recognizes Korean Independence
events.description: The United States recognizes the independence of Korea, although Korea's future is uncertain because of Chinese, Russian, and Japanese manipulations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. recognizes Korean Independence

events.title: Steamboat Safety Bill vetoed
events.description: Arthur vetoes the Carriage of Passengers at Sea Bill, a steamboat safety bill, claiming that it contains several major technical errors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Steamboat Safety Bill vetoed

events.title: River and Harbor Act vetoed
events.description: The President vetoes the River and Harbor Act, a pork-barrel piece of legislation that Arthur claimed would benefit only “particular localities;” Congress overrides the veto and passes the legislation the next day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: River and Harbor Act vetoed

events.title: Physician keeps President’s disease secret
events.description: Brode Herndon, Arthur's physician, writes in his private diary, “The President sick in body and soul.” Arthur had been diagnosed that year with Bright's disease, a fatal kidney ailment; his health will deteriorate rapidly while being kept secret from the general public.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Physician keeps President’s disease secret

events.title: Star-Route Trial concludes
events.description: The verdict in the Star-Route trial is rendered. Of the nine accused, only two minor defendants are found guilty. The foreman of the jury charges that a government agent attempted to bribe him, and the judge orders a retrial, to begin on December 7, 1882.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Star-Route Trial concludes

events.title: Midterm Elections
events.description: In the midterm elections, Democrats gain 50 seats in the House giving them a 197-118 majority (ten remaining seats were filled by minor parties). In the Senate, Republicans take one seat and gain a 38-36 majority (with two seats filled by minor parties).
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Midterm Elections

events.title: Civil Service Reform Act
events.description: Congress passes the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act. The bill establishes a three-man Civil Service Commission and specifies rules for filling federal government positions according to a merit system. The number of Civil Service positions affected by the bill would later be expanded.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Civil Service Reform Act

events.title: Arthur increases Naval funds
events.description: Recognizing the disgraceful state of the U.S. Navy, Arthur signs a bill appropriating funds for the Navy's first steel vessels.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur increases Naval funds

events.title: Mongrel Tariff Act passes
events.description: Congress passes the so-called “Mongrel” Tariff Act, a complex tariff revision that reduces rates on various items by less than 2 percent; Arthur had lobbied Congress for a 20 to 25 percent cut on all items. The act establishes the Republicans as the party in favor of higher protective tariffs.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mongrel Tariff Act passes

events.title: Brooklyn Bridge opens
events.description: President Arthur and New York Governor Grover Cleveland attend the opening of the Brooklyn Bridge. Opening of the Brooklyn Bridge On May 24, 1883, President Chester Arthur and New York Governor Grover Cleveland participated in the ceremonial opening of the Brooklyn Bridge. The Brooklyn Bridge was the first bridge to be built across the East River linking New York City and Brooklyn. The bridge was an engineering marvel, utilizing numerous construction techniques that had never before been attempted on such a massive scale. The original designer, John Roebling, died of an infection caused by an injury he received only days after having secured permission to begin his project. His son Washington took over, supervising the construction project that took thirteen years and more than $15 million to complete (three times as long and twice as expensive as had been anticipated). By the end of the project, Washington Roebling's health had deteriorated considerably. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Brooklyn Bridge opens

events.title: Second Star-Route case concludes
events.description: The court renders a verdict in the second Star-Route case. All nine defendants are found not guilty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second Star-Route case concludes

events.title: Korean Ambassadors visit
events.description: Arthur receives Korean ambassadors in New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Korean Ambassadors visit

events.title: U.S.-Luxembourg Treaty
events.description: The United States and Luxembourg conclude an extradition treaty in New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S.-Luxembourg Treaty

events.title: Arthur celebrates Washington
events.description: Arthur issues a proclamation recommending the observance of the 100th anniversary of General George Washington returning his commission as commander-in-chief to the Continental Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur celebrates Washington

events.title: Standard Time established
events.description: The United States participates in an international conference establishing standard time.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Standard Time established

events.title: Arthur requests funds for Navy
events.description: In a special message to Congress, Arthur asks the legislature to appropriate funds for naval reconstruction work.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arthur requests funds for Navy

events.title: Test Oath repealed
events.description: Congress passes a bill repealing the 1862 test oath, which required office holders to swear they had never engaged in illegal or disloyal conduct.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Test Oath repealed

events.title: Alaskan Government Act passes
events.description: Congress passes an act regarding civil government in Alaska. The territory had been ceded to the United States by Russia in an 1867 treaty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alaskan Government Act passes

events.title: RNC nominates James G. Blaine
events.description: The Republican National Convention meets in Chicago. Political opponent of the President and former secretary of state James G. Blaine defeats Arthur for the nomination; John A. Logan is selected as vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates James G. Blaine

events.title: Bureau of Labor established
events.description: The United States Bureau of Labor is created within the Department of the Interior; an independent Department of Labor will not be created until 1913.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Bureau of Labor established

events.title: Proclamation against settlement in Oklahoma
events.description: Arthur issues a proclamation warning people not to settle on Oklahoma lands.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Proclamation against settlement in Oklahoma

events.title: Statue of Liberty presented
events.description: France presents the United States with the Statue of Liberty at a ceremony held in Paris.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Statue of Liberty presented

events.title: DNC nominates Grover Cleveland
events.description: The Democratic National Convention meets in Chicago, nominating Grover Cleveland and Thomas A. Hendricks for President and vice president, respectively.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates Grover Cleveland

events.title: Cleveland defeats Blaine
events.description: In the federal presidential election, Grover Cleveland defeats James G
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland defeats Blaine

events.title: U.S.-Mexico Convention
events.description: The United States and Mexico conclude a convention on their shared territorial boundary.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S.-Mexico Convention

events.title: Washington Monument dedicated
events.description: The Washington Monument is dedicated in Washington, D.C.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington Monument dedicated

events.title: Public Land Fencing prohibited
events.description: Congress passes an act prohibiting the fencing of public lands in the west.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Public Land Fencing prohibited

events.title: Contract Labor Law passes
events.description: Congress passes the Contract Labor Law, also known as the Foran Act, which virtually outlaws alien contract labor. The act is designed to ban companies from importing immigrant workers to break strikes and drive down wages.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Contract Labor Law passes

events.title: Cleveland Inaugurated
events.description: Grover Cleveland is inaugurated as the twenty-second President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Inaugurated

events.title: Grover Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Former New York Governor Grover Cleveland is sworn in and takes office as the twenty-second President of the United States. Cleveland is the nation's first Democratic President since James Buchanan, who served prior to the onset of the Civil War. In his inaugural address, Cleveland promises to adhere to “business principals,” indicative of his conservative outlook.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: Vice President Thomas Hendrick dies
events.description: Vice President Thomas Hendricks dies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Vice President Thomas Hendrick dies

events.title: Cleveland Signs Presidential Succession Act
events.description: On January 19, 1886, President Grover Cleveland signed the Presidential Succession Act. The act specified that in the absence of a President and vice president, heads of executive departments would succeed to the presidency in the order in which the departments were created, starting with the secretary of state. The Presidential Succession Act of 1886 remained in force until 1947. Vice President Thomas Hendricks died in his Indianapolis home in November 1885. President Grover Cleveland's message to Congress on December 8 regarding the death of the new vice president called for a constitutional amendment to clarify the line of succession should both the President and vice president die or become unable to serve. While the Presidential Succession Act that was proposed and passed shortly afterward on January 15, 1886, was primarily the work of the Congress, President Cleveland supported the legislation and signed the bill into law. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Signs Presidential Succession Act

events.title: Cleveland addresses Congress
events.description: In a message to Congress, Cleveland asserts that labor is a vital element of national prosperity and should be a concern of the federal government. He suggests the creation of a government committee to resolve disputes between labor and capital, making him the first President to do so.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland addresses Congress

events.title: Cleveland vetoes military pensions
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the first of several bills granting military pensions to Civil War Union veterans who had appealed to Congress after their claims were rejected by the Pensions Bureau. Hundreds of these claims are bogus.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes military pensions

events.title: Accepting the Statue of Liberty
events.description: Cleveland recommends to Congress that the nation accept France's gift of the Statue of Liberty. The gift commemorates the alliance between the two countries during the Revolutionary War. The statue will be placed on Liberty Island, adjacent to Ellis Island off the New Jersey coast. Ellis Island will serve as a welcoming center for the soaring number of immigrants to New York City.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Accepting the Statue of Liberty

events.title: Cleveland announces his engagement
events.description: Cleveland announces that he is to marry 21-year-old Frances Folsom, touching off a media frenzy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland announces his engagement

events.title: Cleveland marries
events.description: Cleveland and Francis Folsom marry.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland marries

events.title: Statue of Liberty dedicated
events.description: The Statue of Liberty is dedicated.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Statue of Liberty dedicated

events.title: American Federation of Labor
events.description: Samuel Gompers forms the American Federation of Labor (AFL), a trade union. Gompers, the son of Jewish immigrants from London, arrives in New York City in 1863. The AFL grows for two decades; by 1904, its members comprise ten percent of all nonagricultural wageworkers. In 1912, the AFL will back presidential hopeful Woodrow Wilson in an effort to establish political coalitions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American Federation of Labor

events.title: Interstate Commerce Commission
events.description: Following complaints about railroad rates and policies, the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) is created to ensure fairness in the management of interstate railroads. Eventually, the scope of the ICC will expand to include all common carriers. The commission is the nation's first independent regulatory agency. Although Cleveland approves its creation, he has reservations about the agency.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Interstate Commerce Commission

events.title: Cleveland Signs Dawes Act
events.description: On February 8, 1887, President Grover Cleveland signed the General Allotment Act into law. The law, commonly known as the Dawes Act after Senator Henry L. Dawes of Massachusetts who proposed it, divided tribal lands of Native Americans into individual allotments and encouraged the assimilation of Native Americans into American society. In 1881, Helen Hunt Jackson published A Century of Dishonor , detailing the federal government's mistreatment of American Indians. Her work created a strong general sentiment for reform of the government's policy toward Native Americans. Senator Dawes proposed the General Allotment Act in 1886 to attempt to put an end to the abuses described in Jackson's book. The bill gave 160 acres of land to each family, 80 acres to single adults and 40 acres to orphaned children, and prohibited recipients from selling the land for a period of 25 years. Indians who renounced their tribal holdings were made eligible for U.S. citizenship. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland Signs Dawes Act

events.title: Cleveland vetoes Dependent Pension Bill
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the Dependent Pension Bill, which would have given a military pension to anyone serving a minimum of ninety days in any war. He argues that the bill will only encourage fraudulent assertions.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Cleveland vetoes Dependent Pension Bill

events.title: Cleveland vetoes Texas Seed Bill
events.description: Cleveland vetoes the Texas Seed Bill, which was designed to provide relief to drought-stricken farmers. Cleveland believes the bill oversteps the powers of the federal government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes Texas Seed Bill

events.title: Tenure of Office Act of 1867
events.description: The Tenure of Office Act of 1867 is repealed after Cleveland challenges its constitutionality. The act had required that the President gain Senate approval to remove from office any individuals who had received Senate confirmation upon appointment. Congress had passed the bill in order gain control over President Andrew Johnson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tenure of Office Act of 1867

events.title: Cleveland tours South and Southwest
events.description: Cleveland embarks on a tour that takes him to states in the southern and western United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland tours South and Southwest

events.title: Cleveland addresses Congress
events.description: In his annual address to Congress, the President argues against protective tariffs, which he claims are creating an excessive surplus. High tariffs were adopted during the Civil War to protect American industrial interests as a temporary measure; they remained in force, however, after the war. Following Cleveland's message, Representative Roger Q. Mills, a Texas Democrat, introduces a moderate bill that reduces rates and favors the South. The Senate rejects this bill and tariff reform becomes one of the divisive issues of the 1888 presidential election.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland addresses Congress

events.title: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar
events.description: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar of Mississippi to the Supreme Court. Previously, Lamar serves as Cleveland's secretary of the interior.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland appoints Lucius Q.C. Lamar

events.title: The Civil Service Commission amends rules
events.description: The Civil Service Commission announces amended rules, prompting Cleveland to respond with a letter containing detailed objections. Cleveland is a proponent of civil service reform, and by the time he leaves office in 1889, he will have expanded the list of classified positions filled under the merit system from sixteen thousand to twenty-seven thousand.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: The Civil Service Commission amends rules

events.title: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller
events.description: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller chief justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. During his second term, the President will come in for significant criticism from political opponents as he will be associated with the conservative decisions of the Fuller Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland appoints Melville W. Fuller

events.title: Department of Labor established
events.description: The Department of Labor is established.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Department of Labor established

events.title: RNC nominates Benjamin Harrison
events.description: Republicans nominate Indiana senator Benjamin Harrison for President. He is the grandson of President William Henry Harrison. New York banker Levi P. Morton serves as Harrison's running mate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates Benjamin Harrison

events.title: DNC nominates Cleveland
events.description: Cleveland accepts the Democratic nomination for President. Ex-Ohio senator Allen G. Thurman is the vice-presidential nominee.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates Cleveland

events.title: Cleveland Signs Chinese Exclusion Act
events.description: On October 8, 1888, President Grover Cleveland signed the Chinese Exclusion Act, which restricted Chinese immigration to the United States. The law prohibited Chinese immigrants who returned to China from coming back to the United States. President Chester Arthur passed the first bill limiting Chinese immigration in 1882, and the federal government did not eradicate barriers to Chinese immigration until 1943. During the 1880s, racial tension on the West Coast between whites and Chinese laborers put significant pressure on the U.S. government to place restrictions on Chinese immigration. This pressure resulted in an outright ban on immigrant Chinese laborers in the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882. But this ban was not enough to quell the growing anti-Chinese sentiment, which erupted in riots in Rock Springs, Wyoming, and Tacoma and Seattle, Washington in 1885. In 1887, Secretary of State Thomas F. [truncated]
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Cleveland loses to Harrison
events.description: Although Cleveland wins the popular vote in presidential election, he loses to Benjamin Harrison in the electoral vote, 233 to 168. On November 6, 1888, President Grover Cleveland was defeated in his bid for re-election by the Republican candidate, Benjamin Harrison. Although President Cleveland won the popular vote, Harrison won the Electoral College and thus the presidency. The Republicans entered the election of 1888 with a well-organized and effective campaign structure. Their candidate, Benjamin Harrison, was the grandson of President William Henry Harrison. He was a former Civil War general and Indiana senator who ran an energetic campaign and exhausted himself delivering more than eighty speeches in sixteen weeks. The Democratic campaign was far less organized, and President Cleveland put forth little effort. Much of the public speaking in the Democratic campaign was left to the nominee for vice president, Allen G. Thurman, who was in poor health. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland loses to Harrison

events.title: Department of Agriculture created
events.description: Cleveland signs a bill creating the Department of Agriculture.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Department of Agriculture created

events.title: North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington becomes states
events.description: Cleveland signs a bill that turns the territories of North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, and Washington into states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Washington becomes states

events.title: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated
events.description: Benjamin Harrison is inaugurated. In a prophetic statement, Frances Cleveland tells the White House staff that she and Grover will return in four years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated

events.title: Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Cleveland is inaugurated, becoming the only President to serve two nonconsecutive terms.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: Cleveland withdraws Hawaiian annexation treaty
events.description: Cleveland withdraws the Hawaiian annexation treaty, signed just prior to his inauguration. He takes the advice of a special commissioner who reports that proponents of the annexation are sugar planters; the majority of the population opposes such action. Cleveland advocates the restoration of the queen but the provisional government rejects this idea.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland withdraws Hawaiian annexation treaty

events.title: Gold reserves fall
events.description: For the first time, the U.S. Treasury gold reserve falls below $100 million. This is partially due to the failure of an important British bank which discharges its American holdings in return for gold.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Gold reserves fall

events.title: Cleveland defends gold standard
events.description: Cleveland vows to defend the gold standard.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland defends gold standard

events.title: The Panic of 1893
events.description: The Panic of 1893 begins after the National Cordage Company and the Philadelphia and Reading railroads go bankrupt on May 4. A sharp decline in the New York stock market follows the next day, known as “Industrial Black Friday.” The panic also distresses farm regions.
events.impact: Economic crisis causing widespread suffering

events.title: Oral cancer
events.description: A malignant growth is detected on the roof of Cleveland's mouth.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Oral cancer

events.title: Special session
events.description: Cleveland calls a special session of Congress for August 7, with the intent of handling the economic crisis through tariff reform and the repeal of the silver-purchase law. Concerned about the low gold reserve, Cleveland wants to end the practice of issuing silver notes that can be redeemed in gold.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session

events.title: Cleveland's secret opperation
events.description: In a secret operation aboard the yacht Oneida in New York's East River, Cleveland's cancerous growth -- and a portion of his jaw -- are removed.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland's secret opperation

events.title: Debating silver and tariffs
events.description: Congress begins debate on the silver issue and tariffs. On August 16, William Jennings Bryan delivers a speech in support of free silver coinage and on August 28, the House votes to repeal the silver clauses of the Sherman Act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Debating silver and tariffs

events.title: Cleveland's second child born
events.description: Esther Cleveland, Grover's second child, is born. Her birth is the first in the White House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland's second child born

events.title: Sherman Silver Purchase Act
events.description: The Sherman Silver Purchase Act, implemented under President Harrison, is repealed. Cleveland supports this policy shift.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sherman Silver Purchase Act

events.title: U.S. Treasury Bonds
events.description: The United States offers treasury bonds for sale in an effort to increase gold reserves. The measure is unsuccessful, and a second bond sale is offered with similar results.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. Treasury Bonds

events.title: Tariff revision bill
events.description: The House passes a tariff revisions bill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tariff revision bill

events.title: "Army of the Commonwealth of Christ"
events.description: Led by rich quarry owner and populist Jacob Coxey, the four hundred members of the “Army of the Commonwealth of Christ” departs from Massillon, Ohio, on a march to Washington. They come to demand that the government take action to alleviate economic depression by providing the unemployed with worthwhile jobs.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Army of the Commonwealth of Christ"

events.title: The “Army" arrives in Washington
events.description: The “Army of the Commonwealth of Christ” arrives at Capitol Hill. Their arrival had been greatly anticipated and feared by many, but the event proves anti-climactic. Coxey and others are arrested for trespassing.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The “Army" arrives in Washington

events.title: Pullman railway strikes
events.description: Eugene Debs, president of the American Railway Union, organizes a strike by employees of the Pullman railway car company, beginning in Pullman, Illinois. Company workers find themselves forced to live in the company town where costs are higher than elsewhere. Additionally, George Pullman lowers wages, in light of the 1893 depression, but maintains rent and other charges. The strike spreads throughout the West and halts rail service, affecting twenty-seven states and territories. Following a recommendation by Attorney General Richard Olney, Cleveland sends federal troops to Chicago on July 3. Eventually, Debs and others are arrested, and the strike is broken.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Pullman railway strikes

events.title: Republic of Hawaii recognized
events.description: Hawaii's provisional government declares the Republic of Hawaii. In its constitution, the body includes a provision for possible American annexation. On August 8, the U.S. government recognizes the Republic of Hawaii.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republic of Hawaii recognized

events.title: Wilson-Gorman Tariff Bill
events.description: The Wilson-Gorman Tariff Bill becomes law without Cleveland's signature (he refuses to veto or sign the measure). The law includes an income tax of 2 percent on all personal income greater than $4,000 and on all corporate income above operating expenses.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson-Gorman Tariff Bill

events.title: Venezuela-Britain boundary dispute
events.description: The United States intervenes in a boundary dispute between Venezuela and Britain, eventually invoking the Monroe Doctrine to assert its rights. Britain agrees to arbitration rather than going to war with the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Venezuela-Britain boundary dispute

events.title: Restoring gold reserves
events.description: A third treasury bond sale to a syndicate headed by J.P. Morgan restores gold reserves and validates the credit of the government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Restoring gold reserves

events.title: Cuba revolts
events.description: A revolution begins in Cuba against Spanish rule. By this point, the United States surpasses Spain in its trade with and investment in Cuba. American sympathy lies with the rebels. Under Cleveland, the United States adopts a policy of neutrality; this changes during the administration of President William McKinley.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cuba revolts

events.title: Supreme Court nullifies income tax law
events.description: The Supreme Court nullifies the income tax law in Pollock v. Farmers' Loan and Trust Company .
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Supreme Court nullifies income tax law

events.title: Upholding injunction
events.description: The Supreme Court justifies the arrest of Eugene Debs, the leader of the Pullman Strike, upholding the government practice of using injunctions to break strikes in Debs v. United States .
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Upholding injunction

events.title: Marion Cleveland born
events.description: Marion Cleveland, Grover's third child, is born at Gray Gables, the Cleveland family's vacation home.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marion Cleveland born

events.title: Utah becomes a state
events.description: Utah is admitted to the union as the forty-fifth state.
events.impact: New state joined the nation

events.title: Expanding federal debt
events.description: A fourth bond sale of $100 million is announced. By month's end, it has restored gold reserves to a generally safe level of $124 million. The four bond sales between 1894 and 1896 create $262 million in federal debt.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Expanding federal debt

events.title: RNC nominates William McKinley
events.description: The Republican National Convention chooses William McKinley, on a gold-standard platform, as its candidate for the upcoming presidential election.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates William McKinley

events.title: DNC nominates William Jennings Bryan
events.description: The Democratic Convention meets and nominates William Jennings Bryan, a Nebraska congressman and champion of silver. The move signals the party's abandonment of the gold standard, upsetting many party members. Populists decide to back Bryan.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates William Jennings Bryan

events.title: "Gold Democrats"
events.description: Democrats unhappy with Bryan's nomination meet in Indianapolis and form the “Gold Democrats.” They ask Cleveland to run again for President. When Cleveland turns down their appeal on September 3, the Gold Democrats nominate Senator John M
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Gold Democrats"

events.title: William McKinley elected
events.description: William McKinley is elected President, carrying 51 percent of the popular vote and 271 electoral votes to Bryan's 176.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William McKinley elected

events.title: Treaty of arbitration with Britain
events.description: The United States and Britain sign a treaty of arbitration ending the Venezuelan dispute which began in December 1894.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of arbitration with Britain

events.title: Cleveland vetoes illiteracy ban
events.description: Cleveland vetoes a bill which would ban illiterate immigrants.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cleveland vetoes illiteracy ban

events.title: William McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as the twenty-fifth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William McKinley inaugurated

events.title: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated
events.description: Republican Benjamin Harrison is inaugurated as the twenty-third President after losing the popular vote to Grover Cleveland. The Republicans hold small majorities in both houses of Congress, making this the first time since 1875 that Republicans control both Congress and the White House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Benjamin Harrison inaugurated

events.title: First Cabinet meeting
events.description: The Harrison Cabinet meets for the first time. It decides against the use of an informal “Kitchen Cabinet” and criticizes the practice of “senatorial courtesy” and the spoils system. Secretary of State James G. Blaine serves as a prominent figure in Harrison's core group, campaigning heavily for American interests in Latin America and Hawaii.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Cabinet meeting

events.title: Berlin Conference
events.description: The Berlin Conference on Samoan Affairs begins, with the United States, Germany, and the United Kingdom attempting to bring peace to the troubled area. The conference will conclude with the making of a treaty, “The Final Act of the Berlin Conference on Samoan Affairs,” which declares the neutrality and nominal independence of Samoa while creating a three-power protectorate over the islands. Secretary of State Blaine handles the negotiations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Berlin Conference

events.title: Roosevelt visits the White House
events.description: Harrison invites Theodore Roosevelt to the White House and appoints him Civil Service Commissioner on May 7. Roosevelt, a reform Republican from New York, heads the department until 1895.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Roosevelt visits the White House

events.title: Harrison tours New England
events.description: Building on the work of President Arthur, Harrison tours New England and reveals plans for an expanded merchant marine and two-ocean Navy. Expansion of the Navy will be a distinguishing feature of Harrison's presidency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison tours New England

events.title: First Pan-American Conference
events.description: Secretary of State Blaine initiates the first Pan-American Conference, primarily to increase U.S. commercial interests in Latin America. Blaine hopes to heighten the American presence in Latin America to the detriment of Britain. Pan-American Conference Begins On October 2, 1889, the first Pan-American Conference began in Washington, D.C. The conference was a meeting between the United States and various countries in Latin America. Its goal was to improve economic and political relations between participants. Secretary of State James Blaine had first proposed a Pan-American Conference in 1881 during his brief tenure under President James Garfield. After Garfield's assassination and Blaine's subsequent resignation, President Chester Arthur's secretary of state, Frederick Frelinghuysen, cancelled the conference. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Pan-American Conference

events.title: North and South Dakota become states
events.description: North and South Dakota join the Union as the thirty-ninth and fortieth states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North and South Dakota become states

events.title: Montana becomes a state
events.description: Montana becomes the forty-first state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Montana becomes a state

events.title: Washington becomes a state
events.description: Washington is admitted as the forty-second state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington becomes a state

events.title: Harrison's first message to Congress
events.description: Harrison sends his first message to Congress. Among his recommendations are civil rights and civil service reform, naval legislation, improved conditions for railroad workers, and pensions for veterans.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Harrison's first message to Congress

events.title: Harrison nominates a Supreme Court justice
events.description: Harrison nominates David J. Brewer to the Supreme Court. The Senate approves the choice two weeks later.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison nominates a Supreme Court justice

events.title: Dependent Pension Bill passed
events.description: The Dependent Pension Bill is passed, providing benefits to Union veterans as well as to their children and widows. Former President Cleveland vetoed the same bill three years earlier. By 1907, the law will have cost the government more than a billion dollars.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dependent Pension Bill passed

events.title: Sherman Anti-Trust Act
events.description: The Sherman Anti-Trust Act is enacted, forbidding business practices that restrain trade and commerce or attempt to create monopolies. Until the presidency of Theodore Roosevelt, the government will rarely invoke the law: between the act's inception and 1901, only eighteen antitrust suits appear, with four of them coming against labor unions.
events.impact: Broke up harmful monopolies

events.title: Idaho becomes a state
events.description: Idaho is admitted as the forty-third state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Idaho becomes a state

events.title: Wyoming becomes a state
events.description: Wyoming is admitted as the forty-fourth state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wyoming becomes a state

events.title: Sherman Silver Purchase Act
events.description: Harrison signs into law the Sherman Silver Purchase Act after convincing free silver senators to compromise on the legislation. Support comes from farmers who argue that increased silver coinage will inflate the currency supply and raise prices, as well as from leaders of new western states with silver mines. The law permits the Treasury to buy 4.5 million ounces of silver each month, doubling the previous purchase amount. Nevertheless, the bill has little effect on the economy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sherman Silver Purchase Act

events.title: Harrison requests to ban lottery tickets
events.description: Harrison sends a special message to Congress in which he requests legislation to ban lottery ticket sales by mail.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison requests to ban lottery tickets

events.title: Anti-Lottery Bill
events.description: Congress passes the Anti-Lottery Bill proposed by John Caldwell of Ohio. It is signed into law on September 19.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Anti-Lottery Bill

events.title: McKinley Tariff
events.description: Congress passes the McKinley Tariff, introduced by Ohio Senator William McKinley, future President of the United States. Average duties on manufactured goods are increased to 49.5 percent. It is the most controversial legislation passed during Harrison's term, greatly expanding the power of the President in foreign trade. A section of the tariff allows the President to negotiate reciprocity agreements for certain commodities. Secretary of State Blaine endorses this portion of the bill, believing it will enable Harrison to influence Latin American countries to lower rates on American exports. In June, Harrison will sign several such accordance agreements.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: McKinley Tariff

events.title: Democrats gain seats
events.description: The mid-term elections result in a Democratic sweep of the House, while in the Senate the Republican majority falls to eight. The Democratic victory reflects society's displeasure with the higher tariffs imposed by the McKinley Tariff.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats gain seats

events.title: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown
events.description: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison appoints Henry B. Brown

events.title: Circuit Courts of Appeals created
events.description: Harrison signs a historic measure creating nine Circuit Courts of Appeals. The new Courts are set up to relieve the demands on the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Circuit Courts of Appeals created

events.title: New Orleans lynchings
events.description: A mob in New Orleans lynches eleven Italian immigrants from Sicily, resulting in Italy severing its diplomatic ties to the United States and threatening war. Those murdered are among a group of nineteen Italian immigrants indicted for the murder of police chief David C. Amidst allegations of threats and bribes to the jury, all nineteen had been cleared. The incident helps usher the word “mafia” into common parlance.
events.impact: Racial terrorism and murder

events.title: Clash with Chilean forces
events.description: Responding to a request from the Balmaceda government of Chile, the United States seizes a Chilean rebel ship, the Itata , as it is carrying an arms shipment from San Diego. The rebels eventually defeat the Balmaceda government in a civil war, leading to the emergence of tense relations between the United States and Chile.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clash with Chilean forces

events.title: Escalating conflict with Chile
events.description: A brawl between American sailors and Chilean nationals in Valparaiso, Chile, results in the deaths of two Americans and many arrests. Tensions between the United States and Chile escalate, and many fear the outbreak of war between the two nations.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Escalating conflict with Chile

events.title: Harrison addresses Congress
events.description: In his annual message to Congress, Harrison denounces the Valparaiso attack as “savage, brutal, unprovoked.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison addresses Congress

events.title: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as Secretary of War
events.description: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as the new secretary of war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison nominates Stephen B. Elkins as Secretary of War

events.title: Sending an ultimatum
events.description: The United States sends an ultimatum to Chile.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sending an ultimatum

events.title: Considering war with Chile
events.description: Harrison states that all members of his cabinet are in favor of war with Chile. During the first three weeks of January, Secretary of State Blaine is the only cabinet member arguing against an ultimatum.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Considering war with Chile

events.title: Special message to Congress
events.description: In a special message to Congress, Harrison asks that lawmakers take “appropriate action” regarding Chile.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special message to Congress

events.title: Chile backs down
events.description: Chile backs down in the conflict. Ultimately, it pays an indemnity of $75,000.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Chile backs down

events.title: Harrison decides on second run
events.description: Harrison decides to run for re-election; party bosses oppose him.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison decides on second run

events.title: Secretary of State resigns
events.description: Secretary of State Blaine resigns. His disagreements with the President have increased. Additionally, Blaine has grown increasingly ill and will die less than eight months after leaving office.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of State resigns

events.title: RNC nominates Harrison
events.description: Harrison is nominated on the first ballot at the Republican National Convention in Minneapolis. Whitelaw Reid of New York is nominated as his running mate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC nominates Harrison

events.title: Democrats nominate Grover Cleveland
events.description: The Democrats nominate Grover Cleveland and Adlai E
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Grover Cleveland

events.title: Pinkertons bust steel strike
events.description: After being locked out over a contract dispute, steel workers at the Homestead plant (part of Carnegie Steel) in Pennsylvania fight with men from the Pinkerton Detective Agency, who have been brought in to bust the strike. Seven Pinkertons and nine workers die. Six days later, 8,000 militiamen accompany and protect the Pinkerton men.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Pinkertons bust steel strike

events.title: Silver mine strike turns violent
events.description: Silver miners at Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, go on a violent strike. Thirty men are killed as they fight non-union help. Harrison sends in federal troops to restore order.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Silver mine strike turns violent

events.title: Homestead Steel Strike
events.description: Harrison privately supports mediation in the Homestead Steel Strike and sends Whitelaw Reid as an emissary to Henry Clay Frick, the man whom Carnegie has left in charge of Homestead. This proves to be fruitless; the strike lasts five months, breaks the union, and deals a major blow to organized labor. When the strike ends on November 20, Carnegie realizes his major aim as the union is virtually destroyed.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Homestead Steel Strike

events.title: Harrison's wife dies
events.description: After suffering from tuberculosis, Harrison's wife dies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harrison's wife dies

events.title: Grover Cleveland wins the Presidency
events.description: Garnering 43 percent of the popular vote, Harrison is defeated by Grover Cleveland, who gains 46 percent in the presidential election. Cleveland receives 277 electoral votes to Harrison's 145.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland wins the Presidency

events.title: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii deposed
events.description: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii is deposed on January 17, with a provisional government being established under Sanford B. News of the revolt reaches Washington on January 29. Harrison responds by deploying 150 marines to Hawaii to protect the new government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Queen Liliuokalani of Hawaii deposed

events.title: Attempting to annex Hawaii
events.description: Harrison sends a treaty to the Senate requesting “full and complete” annexation of Hawaii. The Senate, intensely divided, refuses to act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Attempting to annex Hawaii

events.title: Grover Cleveland inaugurated
events.description: Grover Cleveland is inaugurated as President, and Harrison returns to Indianapolis.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Grover Cleveland inaugurated

events.title: William McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as the twenty-fifth President of the United States. McKinley asserts: “The country is suffering from industrial disturbances from which speedy relief must be had. Our financial system needs some revision; our money is all good now, but its value must not further be threatened.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William McKinley inaugurated

events.title: Special session called
events.description: President McKinley calls Congress into a special session for the purpose of revising the tariff laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session called

events.title: First Boston Marathon
events.description: McDermott wins the first Boston Marathon. The 26.2 miles from Hopkinton to Copley Square will become one of the world's most prestigious marathons.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Boston Marathon

events.title: Relief for Cuba
events.description: Congress appropriates $50,000 for the relief of Americans in Cuba.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Relief for Cuba

events.title: Alaskan gold
events.description: The first shipment of gold discovered in Alaska, totaling $750,000, arrives in San Francisco.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alaskan gold

events.title: Dingley Tariff Law
events.description: President McKinley signs the Dingley Tariff Law, which raises custom duties by an average of 57 percent. Although American industries no longer needed such heavy protection against foreign goods, the tariff was raised nonetheless; imported woolen products, for example, faced a 91 percent rate. Republicans associate the high tariff with national prosperity while Democrats and progressives will blame the tariff for causing subsequent price increases.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Dingley Tariff Law

events.title: Coal mine strikes turn violent
events.description: More than twenty workers are killed in Lattimer, Pennsylvania, after deputy sheriffs open fire on striking coal miners. In sympathy, coal miners in the Ohio, West Virginia, and the Lehigh Valley of Pennsylvania walk off their jobs. The strike is settled soon thereafter, with Pennsylvania workers being awarded an eight-hour day.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Coal mine strikes turn violent

events.title: First annual message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley's first annual message to Congress is read aloud. The President states that while the government of Spain should be given time to reform its behavior in Cuba, America would continue to devote significant diplomatic attention to the island. McKinley also reminds Americans to refrain from factionalism: “Questions of foreign policy, of revenue, the soundness of the currency, the inviolability of national obligations, the improvement of the public service, appeal to the individual conscience of every earnest citizen to whatever party he belongs or in whatever section of the country he may reside.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: First annual message to Congress

events.title: Opposing Cuban autonomy
events.description: In Havana, Cuba, pro-Spanish groups riot in opposition to Cuban autonomy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Opposing Cuban autonomy

events.title: American defense in Cuba
events.description: Battleship Maine arrives in Havana on a nominally “friendly visit.” Its true mission is to protect American life and property.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American defense in Cuba

events.title: A letter written by Spanish minister to the United…
events.description: A letter containing insults directed at President McKinley, written by Spanish minister to the United States Señor Don Enrique Dupuy de Lôme , is published in William Randolph Hearst's New York Journal .
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: A letter written by Spanish minister to the United…

events.title: Maine explodes
events.description: The battleship Maine explodes and sinks in Havana harbor, killing 266 Americans. Subsequent press coverage of the event points to Spanish sabotage as the cause of the disaster, despite dubious evidence. The sinking of the Maine fans popular opinion, already sympathetic to the cause of Cuban independence, in support of American intervention. Battleship Maine Sinks On the night of February 15, 1898, a fire in a coal bunker of the battleship Maine ignited a reserve gunpowder magazine, blowing the ship in half and killing 266 crew members. “Remember the Maine !” became a rallying cry for Americans who wanted to go to war against Spain over the island of Cuba. President William McKinley tried to find a diplomatic solution to resolve the conflict but ultimately Spain declared war against the United States, beginning the Spanish-American War. Spain had long controlled the Caribbean island of Cuba but throughout the 19th century, the Cuban people had struggled to gain independence. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Maine explodes

events.title: Increasing defense funds
events.description: At President McKinley's behest, Congress votes a $50 million appropriation for national defense.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Increasing defense funds

events.title: The U.S. Navy reports on the Maine
events.description: Navy reports that the Maine explosion was the result of external factors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The U.S. Navy reports on the Maine

events.title: Conflicting naval report
events.description: The Spanish Navy releases its own report on the Maine disaster, concluding that an internal explosion destroyed the battleship.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Conflicting naval report

events.title: Civil war in Cuba
events.description: President McKinley asks Congress for authority to “use armed force” in Cuba to end the civil war. Meanwhile, Spanish Prime Minister Sagasta makes a last-minute peace concession by offering the Cubans limited autonomy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Civil war in Cuba

events.title: Authorizing American intervention in Cuba
events.description: Congress adopts a joint resolution authorizing President McKinley to intervene in Cuba. The resolution also states that the United States has no plans to annex Cuba. Spain counters by severing diplomatic relations with the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Authorizing American intervention in Cuba

events.title: Ordering a Cuban blockade
events.description: President McKinley orders a blockade of northern Cuban ports.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ordering a Cuban blockade

events.title: Volunteer Army Act
events.description: Congress passes the Volunteer Army Act, which authorizes the organization of the First Volunteer Cavalry, or Rough Riders, under the command of Colonel Leonard Wood and Lt. Colonel Theodore Roosevelt. That same day, the U.S. captures its first spoils of war, the Spanish ship Buena Ventura .
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Volunteer Army Act

events.title: Volunteers for war
events.description: President McKinley calls for 125,000 volunteers to fight the war with Spain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Volunteers for war

events.title: Spain declares war on the U.S.
events.description: Spain declares war on the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Spain declares war on the U.S.

events.title: Congress declares war on Spain
events.description: The United States Congress declares war on Spain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress declares war on Spain

events.title: War at Manila Bay
events.description: Commodore George Dewey, commanding an American squadron of six ships, soundly defeats a larger but outgunned Spanish fleet at Manila Bay. This action opens the door for American occupation of Manila in August.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: War at Manila Bay

events.title: Additional volunteers for war
events.description: President McKinley issues a new call for volunteers, asking for an additional 75,000. troop expedition of 2,500 men also sets sail for Manila, Philippines, from San Francisco, California.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Additional volunteers for war

events.title: Erdman Arbitration Act
events.description: Congress passes the Erdman Arbitration Act, which authorizes government mediation between interstate carriers and their employees. The legislation prohibits interstate carriers from discriminating against or blacklisting union laborers. The Supreme Court would rule the Erdman Act unconstitutional in June 1908.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Erdman Arbitration Act

events.title: Landing at Guantanamo
events.description: Roughly 600 U.S. Marines land at Guantanamo, Cuba.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Landing at Guantanamo

events.title: War Revenue Act
events.description: Congress passes the War Revenue Act, which generated about $150 million of tax revenue a year from taxes levied on beer, tobacco, amusements, and some business transactions. President McKinley signs the bill on June 13.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: War Revenue Act

events.title: Troops deploy from Key West
events.description: Some 17,000 U.S. troops under the command of General William Shafter embark from Key West, Florida, headed for Cuba.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Troops deploy from Key West

events.title: Guam surrenders
events.description: Guam, whose Spanish commander was oblivious to the outbreak of the war, surrenders to advancing western Pacific fleets. The ignorance of the Spanish garrison becomes apparent to the captain of the U.S.S. Charleston when, following his bombardment of Guam, the Spanish apologize for not having returned the salute.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Guam surrenders

events.title: Battle of Las Guasimas
events.description: The United States defeats Spanish troops at the Battle of Las Guasimas, the first major land battle of the Spanish-American War.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of Las Guasimas

events.title: Taking El Caney and San Juan Hill
events.description: After heavy fighting, American forces in Cuba take the Spanish garrisons at El Caney and San Juan Hill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taking El Caney and San Juan Hill
