events.title: Spanish fleet destroyed
events.description: American naval forces destroy the Spanish fleet off Santiago de Cuba.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Spanish fleet destroyed

events.title: Annexing Hawaii
events.description: On July 7, 1898, President William McKinley signed a bill that annexed the Hawaiian Islands, making them part of the United States. American military victories in the Pacific during the Spanish-American War helped bring to a close almost a decade of uncertainty about the status of the Hawaiian Islands. Prominent American colonists had supported the overthrow of Hawaiian Queen Liliuokalani in early 1891, but Democratic and anti-imperialist Republican opposition to annexation blocked the island chain's incorporation into the United States. When William McKinley assumed the presidency in 1897, he reversed the policy of his predecessor, Democrat Grover Cleveland, and advocated Hawaiian annexation. McKinley, however, was unable to push a new annexation treaty through Congress that year. After American forces swung into action in the Pacific during the Spanish-American War, the island chain's strategic importance became apparent. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Annexing Hawaii

events.title: Santiago de Cuba surrenders
events.description: Santiago de Cuba surrenders, along with 24,000 Spanish troops, to American General William Shafter.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Santiago de Cuba surrenders

events.title: American forces invade Puerto Rico
events.description: American forces invade Puerto Rico, encountering little resistance.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American forces invade Puerto Rico

events.title: Spain and the United States sign an armistice
events.description: Spain and the United States sign an armistice in which Spain agrees to grant Cuba its independence and cede Puerto Rico and Guam to the United States. The fate of the Philippines is left to be determined at a postwar conference between the United States and Spain.
events.impact: Ended hostilities

events.title: Negotiating with Spain
events.description: President McKinley appoints U.S. peace commissioners for negotiations with Spain; Secretary of State William R. Day will lead the delegation. McKinley asks Day to resign his office to assume the leadership of the peace commission. John Hay becomes secretary of state on September 30.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Negotiating with Spain

events.title: Spanish forces in the Philippines surrender
events.description: Spanish forces in the Philippines surrender to the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Spanish forces in the Philippines surrender

events.title: Dodge Commisison
events.description: President McKinley appoints the Dodge Commission to investigate the conduct of the War Department.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dodge Commisison

events.title: Striker's riot
events.description: A strikers' riot in Virden, Illinois, leads to thirteen deaths and twenty-five injuries.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Striker's riot

events.title: Peace in the Philippines
events.description: American peace commissioners in Paris receive instructions to demand from Spain the cession of the Philippine Islands.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Peace in the Philippines

events.title: Republicans gain peace
events.description: The Republicans gain Senate seats in mid-term elections, emerging with a 53-26-8 lead; they lose strength in the House, where their advantage narrows to 185-163-9.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans gain peace

events.title: McKinley's second annual message
events.description: President McKinley's second annual message is read to both Houses of Congress. He declares his intention to build an inter-oceanic canal through Nicaragua and discusses the merits of fighting the Spanish-American war: “Military service under a common flag and for a righteous cause has strengthened the national spirit and served to cement more closely than ever the fraternal bonds between every section of the country.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's second annual message

events.title: The Treaty of Paris
events.description: The United States and Spain sign the Treaty of Paris.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Treaty of Paris

events.title: Signing of the Treaty of Paris
events.description: The United States and Spain sign the Treaty of Paris, which ends the Spanish-American War. Spain agrees to give up Puerto Rico and Guam, and the United States purchases the Philipines. Cuba is granted its independence, but the United States takes control of the country.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Signing of the Treaty of Paris

events.title: First Philippine Commisison
events.description: President McKinley appoints Jacob Gould Schurman chairman of the first Philippine Commission.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Philippine Commisison

events.title: Battle of Manila
events.description: The Battle of Manila begins between US forces and Philippine forces, led by Emilio Aguinaldo. It is the largest battle of the Philippine-American war. The initial skirmishes, in which 57 American fighters are killed and 215 are wounded, last several days.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of Manila

events.title: Peace treaty ratified
events.description: The Senate ratifies the peace treaty between the United States and Spain by a vote of 57 to 27. The United States acquires Puerto Rico and Guam, and assumes the temporary administration of Cuba. While the United States pays Spain $20 million for certain Filipino holdings, the sum is interpreted by some as payment for the outright purchase of the Philippines.
events.impact: Ended armed conflict

events.title: Voting machines
events.description: Congress authorizes voting machines for federal elections, subject to the request of individual states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Voting machines

events.title: Retreat from civil service reform
events.description: President McKinley issues an executive order exempting between 3,000 to 4,000 positions from competitive civil service examinations. McKinley's order marks a retreat from President Grover Cleveland's more aggressive practices of civil service reform.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Retreat from civil service reform

events.title: Secretary of War Russell A. Alger resigns
events.description: Secretary of War Russell A. Alger resigns, effective August 1, after the Dodge Commission criticizes the War Department's handling of the war. Elihu Root replaces Alger.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of War Russell A. Alger resigns

events.title: Open Door notes to Britain, France, Russia, Japan
events.description: Secretary of State John Hay issues the Open Door notes to Britain, France, Russia, and Japan. Hay calls for broad, multi-lateral access to Chinese markets across foreign spheres of influence as well as for the preservation of the territorial sovereignty of the Chinese Empire.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Open Door notes to Britain, France, Russia, Japan

events.title: First presidential car ride
events.description: President McKinley becomes the first President to ride in an automobile when he motors in a Stanley Steamer in his hometown of Canton, Ohio.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First presidential car ride

events.title: McKinley's third message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley sends his third annual message to Congress. It focuses largely on foreign affairs, with McKinley calling for beefing up the U.S. Navy to benefit overseas commerce. With regard to the Philippines, McKinley affirms, “I shall use the authority vested in me by the Constitution and the statutes to uphold the sovereignty of the United States in those distant islands as in all other places where our flag rightfully floatsÖ.Aiming only at the public good, we cannot err. A right interpretation of the people's will and of duty cannot fail to insure wise measures for the welfare of the islands which have come under the authority of the United States, and inure to the common interest and lasting honor of our country.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's third message to Congress

events.title: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty
events.description: Britain and the United States sign the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty to provide for an isthmian canal in Central America.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty

events.title: Gold Standard Act
events.description: President McKinley signs the Gold Standard Act, which fixes the standard of value for all money issued or coined by the United States. It marks a victory for the so-called “goldbugs” from the Northeast and urban Midwest who pushed for gold-backed currency to stabilize industrial investment. Likewise, it is a crushing defeat for the free silver forces from the South and West who advocated silver coinage as a way to flush the agricultural economy with more available currency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Gold Standard Act

events.title: Carnegie Steel Company, Incorporated
events.description: The Carnegie Steel Company, organized in 1899, is incorporated in New Jersey and capitalized at $160 million.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Carnegie Steel Company, Incorporated

events.title: Second Philippine Commission
events.description: President McKinley appoints a Second Philippine Commission, chaired by William Howard Taft.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second Philippine Commission

events.title: Territory of Hawaii established
events.description: Congress passes an act establishing the Territory of Hawaii.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Territory of Hawaii established

events.title: RNC renominates McKinley
events.description: The Republican National Convention re-nominates McKinley for the presidency. Spanish-American War Hero and New York governor Theodore Roosevelt is nominated for vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: RNC renominates McKinley

events.title: Amnesty in the Philippines
events.description: Amnesty is given to Filipino insurgents in a decree issued by the military governor of the Philippines.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Amnesty in the Philippines

events.title: Second Open Door Note issued
events.description: Secretary of State John Hay issues the second Open Door Note, a circular letter outlining American desires to keep China intact in the midst of Western intervention during the Boxer Rebellion.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second Open Door Note issued

events.title: DNC nominates William Jennings Bryan
events.description: The Democratic National Convention nominates William Jennings Bryan for the presidency and Adlai E. Stevenson for the vice presidency. During the subsequent campaign, Bryan charges McKinley with being an imperialist and runs once again on a “free silver” platform.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: DNC nominates William Jennings Bryan

events.title: McKinley formally accepts nomination
events.description: President McKinley formally accepts the Republican presidential nomination in a speech at Canton, Ohio. McKinley's campaign slogan reminded voters of previous prosperity and promised more of the same: “Four more years of the full dinner pail.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley formally accepts nomination

events.title: McKinley reelected
events.description: William McKinley is re-elected President of the United States, with Theodore Roosevelt elected as vice president. McKinley wins with 292 electoral votes against 155 for William Jennings Bryan; Social Democratic candidate Eugene V. Debs secures 94,768 popular votes. Republicans strengthen their hold on both houses of Congress, securing a 55-31 majority in the Senate and a 197-151 majority in the House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley reelected

events.title: McKinley's fourth message to Congress
events.description: President McKinley submits his fourth annual message to Congress. He notes that in foreign affairs, “the dominant question has been the treatment of the Chinese problem. Apart from this our relations with the powers have been happy.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley's fourth message to Congress

events.title: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty ratified
events.description: The Senate ratifies a modified version of the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty, in which the British government agrees to an American canal with the conditions that it be neutral and unfortified. This treaty abrogates the Clayton-Bulwer Treaty of 1850.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty ratified

events.title: Negotiating Dutch West Indies
events.description: Negotiations conclude between the Dutch and the Americans regarding the Dutch West Indies (which become the U.S. Virgin Islands), allowing Congress simply to appropriate funds to carry out the transfer. This transfer did not occur until 1917.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Negotiating Dutch West Indies

events.title: The first great oil strike in Texas
events.description: The first great oil strike in Texas occurs near Beaumont.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The first great oil strike in Texas

events.title: Platt Amendment
events.description: Congress adopts the Platt Amendment, which governs future relations between the United States and Cuba, as part of the Army Appropriation Act of 1901. The amendment allows American intervention in Cuban domestic affairs to preserve the sovereignty of the island nation against threats from other foreign powers.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Platt Amendment

events.title: McKinley inaugurated
events.description: William McKinley is inaugurated as President for a second term, with Theodore Roosevelt sworn in as vice president. McKinley calls for the Filipino rebellion to end “without further bloodshed,” wising that “there be ushered in the reign of peace to be made permanent by a government of liberty under law!”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: McKinley inaugurated

events.title: George H. White leaves Congress
events.description: North Carolina's George H. White leaves Congress, the last black member to serve for more than twenty-five years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: George H. White leaves Congress

events.title: British reject Hay-Paucefote treaty
events.description: The British government informs the United States that it will not accept the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty as amended by the Senate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: British reject Hay-Paucefote treaty

events.title: Filipino resistance leader captured
events.description: On March 23, 1901, the United States captured Emilio Aguinaldo, the leader of the independence movement in the Philippine Islands. Once in U.S. custody, Aguinaldo signed an oath of allegiance to the United States, which ended any lingering hope of the rebellion's success. President William McKinley tried to convince the Filipinos that U.S. rule would benefit the country, but the United States continued fighting rebel troops across the islands for another year until American forces secured full control of the country. The Philippine independence movement began in 1896 as Filipinos revolted against Spanish rule. Both sides agreed to a truce in December 1897, with Aguinaldo going into exile in Hong Kong. During the Spanish-American War, U.S. leadership brought Aguinaldo back to the islands to aid in mobilizing the local population against Spain. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Filipino resistance leader captured

events.title: Rebellion in the Philippines ends
events.description: The rebellion in the Philippines ends by proclamation. Sporadic fighting continues for another year before American military forces fully secure the islands.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Rebellion in the Philippines ends

events.title: McKinley declines to run again
events.description: President McKinley announces he will not be a candidate for a third term.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley declines to run again

events.title: Tariff reciprocity
events.description: Speaking in Buffalo, New York, President McKinley endorses the concept of tariff reciprocity. McKinley also notes, in what would be his last speech, “The period of exclusiveness is past. The expansion of our trade and commerce is the pressing problem.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tariff reciprocity

events.title: McKinley shot
events.description: Leon Czolgosz shoots McKinley in the stomach while the President shakes hands at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. Czolgosz, an anarchist, admitted to the shooting, and he expressed no remorse for his actions. He died in the electric chair on October 29, 1901.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: McKinley shot

events.title: McKinley dies
events.description: President McKinley dies from his wounds as the result of complications due to gangrene, and Vice President Theodore Roosevelt takes the oath of office to become the twenty-sixth President of the United States. President McKinley Dies On September 14, 1901, President William McKinley died, eight days after he was shot by an assassin. He had served only six months of his second presidential term before his death. After vacationing in Canton, Ohio, in the summer of 1901, President McKinley and his staff set off for the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. On September 5, McKinley delivered a speech to a crowd of nearly 50,000 on his policy goals for his second term; his particular focus was on the promotion of American foreign trade. The next afternoon, the President attended a public reception at the Exposition's Temple of Music, accompanied by three secret service bodyguards. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: McKinley dies

events.title: "Speak softly..."
events.description: While emphasizing the need for a strong foreign policy, Roosevelt talks of the need to “Speak softly and carry a big stick.” The saying catches the fancy of the whole nation, and the “big stick” becomes a favorite object of political cartoonists.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Speak softly..."

events.title: Roosevelt takes office
events.description: Vice President Theodore Roosevelt (TR) takes the oath of office in Buffalo, New York, after President William McKinley is assassinated. Roosevelt becomes the twenty-sixth President of the United States and the youngest President yet at 43 years old. Roosevelt's succession appalls Republicans who blanched at his liberal leanings; TR was nominated for the vice presidency in 1900 partly because Republican leaders were attempting to relegate him to a harmless position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt takes office

events.title: TR dines with Booker T. Washington
events.description: TR dines with Booker T. Washington at the White House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: TR dines with Booker T. Washington

events.title: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty signed
events.description: The United States and Great Britain sign the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty, by which the British grant control of an isthmian canal to the United States. The Senate would ratify Hay-Pauncefote on December 16, thereby abrogating the Clayton-Bulwer Treaty of 1850.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hay-Pauncefote Treaty signed

events.title: Chinese Exclusion Act
events.description: Congress extends the Chinese Exclusion Act, prohibiting the immigration of Chinese laborers from the Philippines.
events.impact: Banned immigration based on race

events.title: Coal miners strike
events.description: A coal-miners strike begins in Pennsylvania, during the course of which 140,000 workers would leave their jobs.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Coal miners strike

events.title: Crater Lake National Park
events.description: The President establishes Crater Lake National Park in Oregon.
events.impact: Preserved natural lands for the public

events.title: Newlands Reclamation Act
events.description: TR signs the Newlands Reclamation Act, thereby authorizing federal irrigation projects.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Newlands Reclamation Act

events.title: Isthmian Canal Act
events.description: Congress passes the Isthmian Canal Act, which called for the funding and building of a canal across the Isthmus of Panama.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Isthmian Canal Act

events.title: Philippine Government Act
events.description: Congress passes the Philippine Government Act, establishing the Philippine Islands as an unorganized territory and all inhabitants as territorial citizens.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Philippine Government Act

events.title: Anthracite Coal Strike
events.description: TR plays a key role in settling the Anthracite Coal Strike. During the spring of 1902, laborers tied to the United Mine Workers union had walked off the job in the hard coal mines of Pennsylvania. The prospect of coal shortages in the winter months loomed, and TR decided that public interest demanded vigorous executive action. Roosevelt summoned union leaders and mine operators to the White House, a significant gesture for both his presidency and for the development of his reform program, known as the “Square Deal.” The coal strike ended on October 21.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Anthracite Coal Strike

events.title: Republicans maintain control
events.description: In congressional elections, the Republicans maintain a majority in the Senate, 57 to 33. In the House, the Republicans emerge with a 208-178 majority.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans maintain control

events.title: Department of Commerce and Labor created
events.description: Roosevelt signs a bill creating the Department of Commerce and Labor, the ninth Cabinet office, which will itself emerge as two separate departments in 1913.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Department of Commerce and Labor created

events.title: Elkins Anti-Rebate Act
events.description: Congress approves the Elkins Anti-Rebate Act, making it illegal for railroads to give rebates on their published freight rates. The Elkins Act is a response to railroads engaging in business practices that gave certain shippers and certain areas a marked advantage. It would fall short of regulating railroads sufficiently; the Hepburn Act would have to be instituted three years later to further that cause.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Elkins Anti-Rebate Act

events.title: Northern Securities Co under federal prosecution
events.description: The Department of Justice announces that the federal government would prosecute the Northern Securities Company (a subsidiary of J.P. Morgan) for violating the Sherman Antitrust Act.
events.impact: Broke up harmful monopolies

events.title: Champion v. Ames
events.description: The Supreme Court hands down a decision in Champion v. Ames , making federal police power superior to that of the states. The ruling became the basis for the future federal regulation of food, drugs, and narcotics.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Champion v. Ames

events.title: First federal bird reservation
events.description: Roosevelt proclaims Pelican Island, Florida, as the first federal bird reservation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First federal bird reservation

events.title: Report of the Anthracite Coal Strike Commission
events.description: The Report of the Anthracite Coal Strike Commission, appointed by TR to investigate the mining industry, declares that workers cannot be discriminated against because they belong to a union.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Report of the Anthracite Coal Strike Commission

events.title: Revolt in Panama
events.description: A revolt breaks out in Panama against Colombian rule. The uprising is sponsored by Panamanian agents and officers of the Panama Canal Company, with tacit permission of the Roosevelt administration. The presence of the American Navy prevents Colombia from crushing the revolt.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Panama recognized as a republic
events.description: The United States recognizes the Republic of Panama.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Panama recognized as a republic

events.title: Hay-Buneau-Varilla Treaty
events.description: The United States negotiates the Hay-Buneau-Varilla Treaty with Panama to build the Panama Canal. The treaty gives the United States control of a ten-mile-wide canal zone in return for $10,000,000 in gold plus a yearly fee of $250,000.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Citizens of Puerto Rico, not of the U.S.
events.description: The Supreme Court rules that citizens of Puerto Rico are not aliens and therefore cannot be denied entry to the continental United States. But the Court also holds that they are not U.S
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Citizens of Puerto Rico, not of the U.S.

events.title: Panama Canal Commission
events.description: TR appoints the Panama Canal Commission to oversee the construction of the Panama Canal.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Sherman Anti-Trust Act
events.description: In accordance with the Sherman Anti-Trust Act, the Supreme Court, in Northern Securities Company v. United States , orders the dissolution of the Northern Securities Company. The decision is major victory for TR and his belief in the necessity of trust-busting.
events.impact: Regulated corporate power

events.title: Republicans nominate Roosevelt
events.description: The Republican Party nominates Roosevelt for the presidency, along with Charles Fairbanks as his vice presidential running mate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans nominate Roosevelt

events.title: Democrats nominate Alton B. Parker
events.description: The Democratic Party nominates Alton B. Parker of New York for the presidency and Thomas Tibbles for the vice presidency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Alton B. Parker

events.title: American Tobacco Company created
events.description: A merger between the Consolidated and the American & Continental tobacco companies produces the American Tobacco Company.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American Tobacco Company created

events.title: Roosevelt elected
events.description: TR wins the presidential election, trouncing Democratic candidate Alton B. Parker, 336 electoral votes to 140. With the exception of Maryland, Roosevelt wins every state north of Washington, D.C., including all Midwestern and Western states; Parker sweeps the South and Texas. In the Senate, the Republicans maintain their 57 to 33 advantage, while in the House, they gain 43 seats, for a 250-136 majority. Roosevelt vows to not seek another presidential term in order to deflect Democratic charges that he would remain in office for life.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt elected

events.title: Roosevelt sends annual message to Congress
events.description: In his annual message to Congress, the President issues the “Roosevelt Corollary” to the Monroe Doctrine. Roosevelt augments the justification for U.S. intervention in the Western Hemisphere “in flagrant cases of wrongdoing or impotence,” arguing that America might be obliged to carry out “the exercise of an international police power.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt sends annual message to Congress

events.title: Trade agreements with the Dominican Republic
events.description: The United States signs a protocol with the Dominican Republic, thereby giving it control of the latter's customs and international in and mollifying European creditors. Though the Senate refuses to ratify this agreement, Roosevelt makes a temporary arrangement with the republic to undertake the newly envisioned “Roosevelt Corollary.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Trade agreements with the Dominican Republic

events.title: National Forest Service established
events.description: Roosevelt establishes the National Forest Service.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: National Forest Service established

events.title: Jacobson v. Massachusetts
events.description: In Jacobson v. Massachusetts , the Supreme Court recognizes the legality of compulsory vaccination laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jacobson v. Massachusetts

events.title: Roosevelt inaugurated
events.description: Roosevelt is inaugurated for his first full term as President of the United States. Also sworn in is Vice President Charles W
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt inaugurated

events.title: Lochner v. New York
events.description: In Lochner v. New York , the Supreme Court rules that state laws limiting working hours are illegal.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lochner v. New York

events.title: Industrial Workers of the World
events.description: The Industrial Workers of the World (IWW) forms in Chicago, Illinois, to counteract the conservative American Federation of Labor.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Industrial Workers of the World

events.title: The Niagara Movement and the NAACP
events.description: A group of black intellectuals, including W.E.B. DuBois, meets near Niagara Falls to demand racial equality. This begins the Niagara Movement, a forerunner of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP).
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: The Niagara Movement and the NAACP

events.title: Portsmouth Treaty
events.description: Russia and Japan sign the Portsmouth Treaty, ending the Russo-Japanese War. Roosevelt played a significant role in mediating this conflict, urging an end to hostilities and brining both sides to the conference table in Portsmouth, N.H. For his actions, Roosevelt would win the 1906 Nobel Peace Prize. The treaty also allowed the United States to maintain a balance of power in the Far East while preserving an Open Door Policy in China.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Algeciras Conference
events.description: The Algeciras Conference opens, with TR hoping to mediate a disagreement between France and Germany over Morocco.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Algeciras Conference

events.title: Clashes in Brownsville
events.description: Clashes erupt in Brownsville, Texas, after white civilians taunt black soldiers. Three whites are killed.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Clashes in Brownsville

events.title: San Francisco earthquake
events.description: A devastating earthquake strikes San Francisco, California, killing 452 and leveling 490 blocks.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: San Francisco earthquake

events.title: National Monuments Act
events.description: Roosevelt signs the National Monuments Act, establishing the first eighteen national monuments, including Devils Tower, Muir Woods, and Mount Olympus.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: National Monuments Act

events.title: Hepburn Act
events.description: TR signs the Hepburn Act, which gives the Interstate Commerce Commission increased power to regulate railroad rates. Roosevelt's leadership is key for the passage of this act, as many observers claim the act would have not come out of the Senate without TR's advocacy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hepburn Act

events.title: Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act
events.description: Roosevelt signs the Meat Inspection Act and the Pure Food and Drug Act. The legislation calls for both an honest statement of food content on labels and for federal inspection of all plants engaging in interstate commerce. The major impetus for these measures was The Jungle , the scathing report on meatpacking plants written by muckraking journalist Upton Sinclair, which TR personally read.
events.impact: Protected consumers from unsafe products

events.title: Cuban President Tomás Estrada Palma asks Roosevelt to send…
events.description: Cuban President Tomás Estrada Palma asks Roosevelt to send American troops to Cuba to quell a rebellion which arose from a disputed election. TR demurs at first, but sends troops in October.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Atlanta, Georgia race riot
events.description: A race riot in Atlanta, Georgia, leaves twenty-one people dead, including eighteen black Americans.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Atlanta, Georgia race riot

events.title: Platt Amendment invoked over Cuba
events.description: The Platt Amendment is invoked, authorizing U.S. military control of Cuba. Future President William Howard Taft serves as provisional governor.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Platt Amendment invoked over Cuba

events.title: Republicans gain seat
events.description: The Republicans gain four seats in the Senate, for a 61 to 31 majority. In the House, the Republicans lose 28 seats, but maintain a 222-164 advantage. The national labor movement became involved in these elections, thereby marking a turning point in the history of national elections. Samuel Gompers and the American Federation of Labor had issued “Labor's Bill of Rights,” asking both parties to support the program. When Republicans declined to do so, the AFL backed the Democrats, who claimed to be the “First to Recognize Organized Labor.”
events.impact: Guaranteed fundamental individual freedoms

events.title: The Roosevelts visit Panama
events.description: The President and Mrs. Roosevelt go to Panama to inspect the building of the Panama Canal, marking the first trip abroad by a sitting American President.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Roosevelt awarded Nobel Peace Prize
events.description: The Nobel Prize Committee awards Roosevelt its Peace Prize for his role in ending the Russo-Japanese War during the Portsmouth Conference in 1905.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Straus appointed to Commerce and Labor
events.description: TR appoints Oscar Straus of New York City to head the Commerce and Labor Department. Straus is the first Jewish American to hold a cabinet post.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Straus appointed to Commerce and Labor

events.title: Campaign finance restrictions
events.description: Congress passes a law prohibiting campaign contributions to candidates for national office.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Campaign finance restrictions

events.title: Reaching an agreement with the Dominican Republic
events.description: The Dominican Republic and the United States sign a treaty empowering American agents to collect Dominican customs taxes for the purpose of satisfying the nation's creditors. The Senate ratifies the treaty on February 25; in 1905, it had refused to ratify a similar agreement.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Reaching an agreement with the Dominican Republic

events.title: Immigration Act of 1907
events.description: TR signs the Immigration Act of 1907, which includes a provision allowing the President to restrict Japanese immigration. The issue had been a matter of great debate during TR's tenure, and Roosevelt proclaimed in his 1905 State of the Union address, that “probably a very large proportion, including most of the undesirable class [of immigrants], does not come here of its own initiative, but because of the activity of the agents of the great transportation companies, they wheedle and cajole many immigrants, often against their best interest, to come here.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Immigration Act of 1907

events.title: Strong arming for forest reserves
events.description: To get around restrictive language in an appropriation bill inhibiting the creation of new forest reserves in six Western states, TR issues proclamations establishing forest reserves in affected states before the law goes into effect. In doing so, TR faced down Westerners who disdained interference from Washington.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Strong arming for forest reserves

events.title: Inland Waterways Commission formed
events.description: An executive Inland Waterways Commission is appointed to study the relationship between forest preservation and commercial waterways.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Inland Waterways Commission formed

events.title: Political disturbances in Honduras
events.description: Marines land in Honduras to protect life and property during a series of political disturbances.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Political disturbances in Honduras

events.title: Second International Peace Conference
events.description: The Second International Peace Conference opens at The Hague, The Netherlands. The United States argues, unsuccessfully, for the establishment of a World Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second International Peace Conference

events.title: The Panic of 1907
events.description: The Panic of 1907 begins when shares of the United Copper Company begin to fluctuate wildly. Rumors spread like wildfire about the Kinckerbocker Trust Company, which triggered a run on several New York Banks. Panic sets in, destabilizing the shaky foundation of the American banking system.
events.impact: Economic crisis causing widespread suffering

events.title: TR returns to Washington from a hunting trip to de…
events.description: TR returns to Washington from a hunting trip to deal with Secretary of the Treasury George B. Cortelyou and to dissipate rumors of a financial crash. Although $68 million dollars is distributed, the government's response fails to calm fears about a possible depression.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: TR returns to Washington from a hunting trip to de…

events.title: Oklahoma becomes a state
events.description: Oklahoma is admitted to the Union as the 46th state.
events.impact: New state joined the nation

events.title: The Great White Fleet
events.description: Under Roosevelt's orders, the Great White Fleet (so named because of the boats' color) embarks on a voyage around the world from Hampton Roads, Virginia. The fleet returns triumphantly on February 22, 1909, having been enthusiastically welcomed at many ports and underscoring America's growing naval strength. The voyage would serve as Roosevelt's proudest accomplishment while in office. Great White Fleet Sails On December 16, 1907, President Theodore Roosevelt assembled the entire class of sixteen American battleships in Hampton Roads, Virginia, and launched them on a training cruise around the world. Labeled the “Great White Fleet,” in reference to the ships' new coat of white paint, the fleet visited Japan and China, passed through the Suez Canal, and called at several Mediterranean ports. Roosevelt scheduled the fleet to return to Hampton Roads on February 22, 1909, ten days before he left office. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Great White Fleet

events.title: Loewe v. Lawlor
events.description: Lawlor , the Supreme Court rules that antitrust law applies to labor unions.
events.impact: Broke up harmful monopolies

events.title: Restricting Japanese immigration
events.description: The United States and Japan reach an agreement on the restriction of Japanese immigration. The Japanese government agrees not to issue any more visas permitting Japanese laborers to emigrate to the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Restricting Japanese immigration

events.title: First Conference of Governors
events.description: At the White House, the first Conference of Governors meets to discuss the problems of conservation.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Child labor law
events.description: Congress passes a child labor law for the District of Columbia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Child labor law

events.title: National Commission for the Conservation of Natural Resources
events.description: Roosevelt establishes the National Commission for the Conservation of Natural Resources, headed by Gifford Pinchot.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Republican Party nominates William Howard Taft
events.description: The Republican Party nominates William Howard Taft for the presidency and James Sherman for the vice-presidency. Republicans were far from united in their support for Taft; party chairman Henry Cabot Lodge speaks in praise of President Roosevelt, touching off a forty-five minute demonstration among the delegates.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republican Party nominates William Howard Taft

events.title: Democratic Party nominates William Jennings Bryan
events.description: William Jennings Bryan wins the Democratic nomination for the presidency, with John Kern as his vice-president running mate. Bryan had lost much of the glamour in this, his third run for the office.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democratic Party nominates William Jennings Bryan

events.title: General Motors Company, Incorporated
events.description: The General Motors Company files incorporation papers in Hudson County, New Jersey.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: General Motors Company, Incorporated

events.title: Ford introduces the “Model T”
events.description: Ford introduces the “Model T” automobile, which costs $850, making Henry Ford's mass-produced cars available to the average wage earner.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ford introduces the “Model T”

events.title: William Howard Taft elected
events.description: William Howard Taft is elected President over Democrat William Jennings Bryan. Running for his third and last shot at the presidency, Bryan garners 162 electoral votes, far behind Taft's winning total of 321. Taft sweeps the Northeast and the Midwest, while Bryan wins every state south of the Mason-Dixon line. The Democrats gain one seat in the Senate, but still trail the Republicans 61 to 32. In the House, the Republicans lose three seats but maintain a 219-172 advantage.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William Howard Taft elected

events.title: NAACP formed
events.description: Black intellectuals, including W.E.B. DuBois, and white progressives, led by Oswald Garrison Villard, form the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP).
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: NAACP formed

events.title: North American Conservation Conference
events.description: The North American Conservation Conference convenes at the White House.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Taft inaugurated
events.description: Roosevelt's administration ends with the inauguration of William Howard Taft as the twenty-seventh President. Roosevelt leaves on a yearlong African safari in order to avoid charges that he was attempting to run the White House from the shadows.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft inaugurated

events.title: Taft inaugurated
events.description: William Howard Taft takes the oath of office, becoming the twenty-seventh President of the United States. Taft had been handpicked by his predecessor, Theodore Roosevelt, and trusted to carry through Theodore Roosevelt's progressivism. Not surprisingly, Taft makes many references to his “distinguished predecessor” in his inaugural address. Nevertheless, a newfound chill had arisen between the two men, mirroring the frigid temperatures in the capital that day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft inaugurated

events.title: Special session convenes
events.description: A special session of the United States Congress convenes to consider revision of the tariff. On March 16, Taft sends a special message to Congress urging prompt revision of the tariff.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Special session convenes

events.title: Peary reaches the North Pole
events.description: Peary reaches the North Pole.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Peary reaches the North Pole

events.title: Nellie Taft suffers a stroke
events.description: Helen “Nellie” Taft suffers a stroke, leaving her speech impaired. Her recovery lasts approximately one year.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nellie Taft suffers a stroke

events.title: Taft's first message to Congress
events.description: Delivering a message to Congress, Taft proposes a two-percent tax on the net income of all corporations except banks, which he believes will make up for revenue lost by tariff reductions. He also proposes that Congress adopt a constitutional amendment that would permit the collection of personal federal income taxes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft's first message to Congress

events.title: Calling for the Sixteenth Amendment
events.description: The Senate passes a resolution calling for a Sixteenth Amendment to the Constitution, authorizing Congress to collect income taxes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Calling for the Sixteenth Amendment

events.title: Negotiation with China
events.description: Taft cables the Chinese regent Prince Chun, requesting that China grant American investors a share of a loan that had been floated in Europe for the purposes of building a railroad in southern China. The Chinese reluctantly grant the United States investment privileges.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Negotiation with China

events.title: Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act
events.description: Taft signs the Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act, which establishes a Tariff Board and reduces the tariff.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Payne-Aldrich Tariff Act

events.title: Taft tours the South, Southwest
events.description: President Taft begins a tour of the southern and western states of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft tours the South, Southwest

events.title: Supporting tariffs
events.description: While on a tour of the United States, Taft calls the Payne-Aldrich Act “the best” tariff bill ever passed by the Republican Party, leaving both Republican progressives and party regulars dismayed.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Supporting tariffs

events.title: Taft visits Porfirio DÌaz
events.description: Taft visits Mexican dictator Porfirio DÌaz at El Paso, Texas, and at Juarez, Mexico.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft visits Porfirio DÌaz

events.title: Taft returns from tour
events.description: Taft returns from his trip across the United States, having made 259 speeches. An observer in Winona, MN comments about Taft, “I knew he was good natured but I never dreamed he was so dull.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft returns from tour

events.title: Charges of conspiracy
events.description: Louis Glavis, chief of the Field Division of the Department of the Interior, charges in Collier's Weekly magazine that Secretary of the Interior Richard Ballinger conspired to defraud the public domain in the Alaskan coal fields and that the Taft administration was complicit in Ballinger's wrongdoing.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Charges of conspiracy

events.title: Warships to Nicaragua
events.description: Taft orders two U.S. warships to Nicaragua in response to the deaths of 500 revolutionaries, and two of their American advisors, at the hands of Nicaragua dictator José Santos Zelaya. The further threat of American force convinces Zelaya to retire on December 16.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Warships to Nicaragua

events.title: Kellog vs Standard Oil
events.description: Special government prosecutor Frank Kellogg wins a Court of Appeals case against Standard Oil, which is ruled a monopoly and in violation of the Sherman Anti-Trust Act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Kellog vs Standard Oil

events.title: General Wood becomes Army Chief of Staff
events.description: Taft appoints General Leonard Wood as Chief of Staff of the Army. He also elevates circuit judge Horace H. Lurton to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: General Wood becomes Army Chief of Staff

events.title: Taft fires Gifford Pinchot
events.description: Taft fires Gifford Pinchot, head of the United States Forest Services, upon the release of a letter Pinchot had written to Senator Dolliver of Iowa on behalf of two of his employees implicated in the Glavis case. Pinchot was a leading conservationist and one of the most recognizable officials in the federal government.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Secretary of State tours Central, South America
events.description: Secretary of State Philander Knox tours Central and South America on a good-will mission.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Secretary of State tours Central, South America

events.title: Republican policy victory
events.description: Representative George Norris, a progressive Republican from Nebraska, wins a major procedural victory in the House of Representatives when that body approves a plan by which the members of the House Rules Committee would be elected by the full House, rather than appointed by the Speaker of the House. This represented a major defeat for Speaker “Uncle Joe” Cannon (R-IL), a leading opponent of the progressives.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republican policy victory

events.title: Taft appoints Hughes to the Supreme Court
events.description: President Taft appoints Governor Charles E. Hughes of New York to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Hughes to the Supreme Court

events.title: Glavis-Ballinger dispute
events.description: At a congressional investigation into the Glavis-Ballinger dispute, attorney Louis Brandeis, representing Glavis, reveals damaging information about the Taft administration. Congress clears Ballinger and the Taft administration of any wrongdoing, however.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Glavis-Ballinger dispute

events.title: Injunction on freight rates
events.description: Taft obtains an injunction to prevent western railroads from raising freight rates. Taft was a fervent anti-trust supporter whose unrelenting anti-trust crusade outmatched even that of Teddy Roosevelt.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Injunction on freight rates

events.title: Taft snubs Roosevelt
events.description: Taft elects not to greet Theodore Roosevelt upon the latter's return from Africa, a move that widens the rift between the two men.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft snubs Roosevelt

events.title: TR declines Taft's invitation
events.description: TR declines Taft's invitation to the White House but praises the President's progress on a number of fronts, including railroad legislation, a postal savings bill, and conservationism.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Mann Act
events.description: Congress passes the Mann Act, also known as the “white slave traffic act,” which prohibits the interstate or international transport of women for “immoral purposes.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mann Act

events.title: Postal Savings Bank Act
events.description: Taft signs the Postal Savings Bank Act, which allowed one bank in each state, under federal supervision, to give two percent interest on accounts under $500.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Postal Savings Bank Act

events.title: Roosevelt's "New Nationalism" speech
events.description: TR returns and delivers the most radical speech of his political career at Osawatomie, Kansas. In his “New Nationalism” speech, Roosevelt outlines a new role for the government in dealing with social issues. His program takes American progressivism in a new direction, endorsing conservation, control of trusts, labor protection, and a graduated income tax. It also embraces the growing conviction that the nation must address the plight of children, women, and the underprivileged.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Taft rejects joint honor
events.description: Taft rejects a proposed dinner, given by the National Conservation Congress, that would honor both himself and TR.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: The International Court of Arbitration
events.description: The International Court of Arbitration at The Hague settles a dispute between Britain and the United States over the Newfoundland fisheries.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The International Court of Arbitration

events.title: Regarding Roosevelt
events.description: Taft, in a letter to his brother, comments that Roosevelt “has proposed a program ("New Nationalism") which it is absolutely impossible to carry out except by a revision of the federal Constitution. In most of these speeches he has utterly ignored me. His attitude toward me is one that I find difficult to understand and explain.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Regarding Roosevelt

events.title: Taft supports Roosevelt
events.description: At the New York State Republican Convention in Saratoga, New York, Taft supports Roosevelt's choice for governor of New York, Henry Stimson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft supports Roosevelt

events.title: The National Urban League
events.description: The National Urban League is formed in New York. Its mission is “to enable African Americans to secure economic self-reliance, parity and power and civil rights.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: The National Urban League

events.title: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter
events.description: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter to the Supreme Court to replace Justice William Moody.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Willis Van Devanter

events.title: Democrats win seats
events.description: In congressional elections, Democrats win control of the House of Representatives for the first time since 1894, gaining a 228 to 162 to 1 majority. In the Senate, Republicans hold a 51 to 41 advantage.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats win seats

events.title: Taft appoints Chief Justice
events.description: Taft appoints Associate Justice Edward White as Chief Justice of the Supreme Court; in January, Taft would also appoint Joseph R. Lamar to the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Chief Justice

events.title: National Progressive Republican League
events.description: Wisconsin Senator Robert LaFollette establishes The National Progressive Republican League in Washington, D.C.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: National Progressive Republican League

events.title: Saving the seals
events.description: The United States and Great Britain sign a treaty guaranteeing the preservation and protection of pelagic fur seals in Bering Sea waters.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Saving the seals

events.title: Investigating postal rates
events.description: Taft appoints a commission to investigate postal rates for newspapers and magazines; its report helps to convince Congress that a recent rate increase was justified.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Investigating postal rates

events.title: Mobilizing along Mexican border
events.description: Taft orders the mobilization of 20,000 American soldiers along the Mexican border after American ambassador to Mexico Henry Lane Wilson reports that the safety of Americans residing in Mexico may be endangered.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mobilizing along Mexican border

events.title: Taft appoints Secretary of Interior
events.description: Taft appoints Walter Fisher, an ally of Gifford Pinchot, as Secretary of the Interior to replace Richard Ballinger, who resigned.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Secretary of Interior

events.title: Taft appoints Secretary of War
events.description: Taft appoints Henry Stimson secretary of war to replace Jacob Dickinson.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft appoints Secretary of War

events.title: Triangle Shirtwaist factory fire
events.description: The Triangle Shirtwaist Company bursts into flames in Manhattan. Women who worked in very cramped and unsafe conditions stampeded toward inadequate exits; 146 women would die, some even leaping to the pavement hoping to survive. The tragedy highlights the need to provide social justice for immigrant sweatshop workers, and the New York legislature responds by undertaking remedial legislation to ensure better working conditions and provide fire safety measures.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Triangle Shirtwaist factory fire

events.title: Dissolution of Standard Oil
events.description: Supreme Court orders the dissolution of the Standard Oil Company. Standard Oil Company Dissolved On May 15, 1911, Chief Justice Edward White issued the Supreme Court's majority opinion upholding the dissolution of the Standard Oil Company. White agreed that the Standard Oil Company's business practices did violate the Sherman Antitrust Act because they were anticompetitive and abusive. However, he muted the circuit court's breakup plan for the company, allowing Standard Oil six months to spin off its subsidiaries instead of the initial three months mandated. After the circuit court of St. Louis initially ruled against the Standard Oil Company, the company's lawyers prepared their appeal to the Supreme Court. With the support of President William Taft, Attorney General George Wickersham and prosecutor Frank Kellogg presented the government's case in January 1911. Mimicking Kellogg's successful argumentation in front of the St. [truncated]
events.impact: Broke up harmful monopolies

events.title: President Porfirio DÌaz of Mexico resigns
events.description: President Porfirio DÌaz of Mexico resigns.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Porfirio DÌaz of Mexico resigns

events.title: American Tobacco Co dissolved
events.description: The Supreme Court finds the American Tobacco Company in violation of the Sherman Anti-trust Act and orders its dissolution.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American Tobacco Co dissolved

events.title: Treaty with Nicaragua
events.description: The United States signs a treaty with Nicaragua which would have made that nation a U.S. protectorate. The Senate later rejects the treaty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty with Nicaragua

events.title: Robert LaFollette announces run
events.description: Senator Robert LaFollette, a progressive from Wisconsin, announces his candidacy for the Republican presidential nomination.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Robert LaFollette announces run

events.title: Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement
events.description: Taft signs the Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Canadian Tariff Reciprocity Agreement

events.title: Arbitration with France and England
events.description: Taft signs general arbitration treaties with France and England. Roosevelt, along with his friend and ally Senator Henry Cabot Lodge, lead the campaign in opposition to the treaties.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arbitration with France and England

events.title: Taft vetoes tariff reductions
events.description: Taft vetoes tariff reductions on wool and woolen goods, arguing that the Tariff Board had not completed its investigation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft vetoes tariff reductions

events.title: Canadian reciprocity defeated
events.description: In the Canadian parliamentary elections, reciprocity with the United States is defeated, killing the treaty signed earlier in the year by the United States and Canada.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Taft tours the western United States
events.description: Taft tours the western United States to drum up support for his arbitration treaties with England and France. In March 1912, the Senate will approve the treaties, which are rejected by Britain and France.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft tours the western United States

events.title: Taft files suit against U.S. Steel
events.description: Taft files suit against U.S. Steel for violating the Sherman Act. In papers filed for the suit, Taft alleges that Roosevelt in 1907 had mistakenly let U.S. Steel purchase the Tennessee Coal and Iron Company. This action damages the Taft-TR relationship irreparably.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft files suit against U.S. Steel

events.title: Madero becomes president of Mexico
events.description: Francisco Madero, a wealthy landowner, assumes office after being elected President of Mexico.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Madero becomes president of Mexico

events.title: Carnegie Corporation
events.description: Andrew Carnegie founds the Carnegie Corporation with an initial endowment of $125,000,000.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Carnegie Corporation

events.title: New Mexico becomes a state
events.description: New Mexico is admitted as the forty-seventh state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New Mexico becomes a state

events.title: Taft calls for federal budget
events.description: Taft urges the adoption of an annual federal budget.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft calls for federal budget

events.title: American interests in Chinese Revolution
events.description: American troops occupy Tientsin, China, to protect American interests from the Chinese Revolution.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American interests in Chinese Revolution

events.title: Arizona becomes a state
events.description: Arizona is admitted as the forty-eighth state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Arizona becomes a state

events.title: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney
events.description: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney for a seat on the U.S. Supreme Court. Pitney is confirmed by the Senate and takes his oath on March 13.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Taft nominates Mahlon Pitney

events.title: Theodore Roosevelt throws in
events.description: Theodore Roosevelt announces that his “hat is in the ring” as a candidate for President. Taft and running mate James S. Sherman are re-nominated together, the first time that Republicans endorse a sitting President and vice president for the party ticket.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Theodore Roosevelt throws in

events.title: Halting major railroad merger
events.description: The Justice Department begins proceedings to halt the merger of the Southern Pacific and Union Pacific railroads.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Halting major railroad merger

events.title: Dr. Harvey Wiley resigns
events.description: Harvey Wiley, Head Chemist at the Department of Agriculture, resigns because of differences with Secretary of Agriculture James Wilson. Wiley was a chief proponent of safe food and drug laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dr. Harvey Wiley resigns

events.title: Washington's first cherry trees
events.description: Taft plants the first of the cherry trees in Washington, D.C., given to the United States by Japan as a symbol of international friendship, along the Tidal Basin of Potomac Park.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington's first cherry trees

events.title: Creation of the Children's Bureau
events.description: Taft signs a bill authorizing the creation of the Children's Bureau in the Department of Commerce. The agency is charged with monitoring child welfare.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Creation of the Children's Bureau

events.title: Titanic sinks
events.description: The British luxury liner Titanic sinks off the coast of Newfoundland. Taft's key aide, Archie Butt, perishes in the tragedy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Titanic sinks

events.title: Julia Lathrop heads the Children's Bureau
events.description: President Taft appoints Julia Lathrop head of the newly-created Children's Bureau. She is the highest ranking woman in the U.S. government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Julia Lathrop heads the Children's Bureau

events.title: Marines land in Cuba
events.description: American Marines land in Cuba to ensure order under the Platt Amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marines land in Cuba

events.title: Taft wins Republican nomination
events.description: Taft wins the Republican presidential nomination over Theodore Roosevelt. James Sherman is re-nominated for vice-president. The bitter primary campaign between TR and Taft featured a thorough discussion within the Republican Party on the issue of government regulation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft wins Republican nomination

events.title: Congress passes 8 hour workday
events.description: Congress passes a labor law authorizing an eight-hour working day for all workers with federal contracts.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress passes 8 hour workday

events.title: Democrats nominate Woodrow Wilson
events.description: The Democratic Party nominates Governor Woodrow Wilson of New Jersey as its candidate for President. Thomas Marshall of Indiana is nominated as vice president.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Woodrow Wilson

events.title: Progressives nominate Roosevelt
events.description: TR is nominated for President by the Progressive (Bull Moose) Party. Hiram Johnson of California is nominated for vice president on the ticket.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Progressives nominate Roosevelt

events.title: Battleships sent to Nicaragua
events.description: battleships are sent to Nicaragua to protect American economic interests and rail lines.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Battleships sent to Nicaragua

events.title: Panama Canal Act
events.description: Taft signs the Panama Canal Act, which exempts American coastwise shipping from paying tolls when transiting the Panama Canal. Many Americans, as well as Britons, consider this a violation of the Hay-Pauncefote Treaty of 1901.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Marines sent to Santo Domingo
events.description: Marines are sent to restore order in Santo Domingo.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marines sent to Santo Domingo

events.title: Vice President John Sherman dies
events.description: Vice President John Sherman dies, and Nicholas Butler, the president of Columbia University, replaces him on the Republican presidential ticket.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Vice President John Sherman dies

events.title: Wilson defeats Taft and Roosevelt
events.description: Democrat Woodrow Wilson defeats Taft and TR in the 1912 presidential election. Wilson wins the electoral college with 435 votes to TR's 88 and Taft's 8. In the popular vote, Wilson defeats TR by over 2 million votes, and Taft by almost 3 million, but TR musters the best third-party showing in history with 27 percent of the popular vote. In congressional elections, Democrats take a majority in the Senate, 51-44-1. In the House, Democrats enjoy a 291-127-17 lead. Election of 1912 On November 5, 1912, President William Taft was defeated by Democrat Woodrow Wilson in the presidential election of 1912. The three-way race between Taft, Wilson, and former President Theodore Roosevelt illustrated the rise of progressivism in presidential politics. Although Roosevelt's Progressive Party had one of the strongest third-party showings in American history, he and Taft divided the Republican Party vote, and Wilson easily won the election. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson defeats Taft and Roosevelt

events.title: Taft vetoes literacy tests
events.description: Taft vetoes a bill calling for literacy tests for immigrants.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Taft vetoes literacy tests

events.title: Huerta overthrows Madero
events.description: General Victoriano Huerta overthrows Mexican President Madero. Taft refuses to intervene, despite the American public's calls for action.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Huerta overthrows Madero

events.title: Sixteenth Amendment ratified
events.description: The Sixteenth Amendment, originally passed in Congress on July 2, 1909, is finally ratified. Previous federal income tax laws had been struck down by the Supreme Court--one in 1862 and another in 1894--on the grounds that they were “direct” taxes and could not be levied without breaching state apportionment. Prior to this amendment, tariff revenue had been the largest single source of government income. The debacle was finally resolved by the Sixteenth Amendment, which called for very low graduated rates on income (1-6 percent).
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sixteenth Amendment ratified

events.title: Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act
events.description: Over Taft's veto, Congress passes the Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act, which prohibits the shipment of liquor into “dry” states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Webb-Kenyon Interstate Liquor Act

events.title: Splitting Department of Labor and Commerce
events.description: Congress divides the Department of Labor and Commerce into two separate departments, both with cabinet status.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Splitting Department of Labor and Commerce

events.title: Wilson inaugurated
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is inaugurated as the twenty-eighth President of the United States. The Tafts leave Washington for a vacation in Augusta, Georgia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson inaugurated

events.title: Woodrow Wilson inaugurated
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is inaugurated as the twenty-eighth President of the United States. He proclaims it his duty “to cleanse, to reconsider, to restore, to correct the evil without impairing the good, to purify and humanize every process of our common life without weakening or sentimentalizing it.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Woodrow Wilson inaugurated

events.title: Ford's Model T
events.description: The Ford Motor Company institutes the first automobile assembly line to produce the Model T. Company founder Henry Ford breaks precedence and pays his line workers $5 a day, believing that higher wages would lead to greater worker productivity and loyalty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ford's Model T

events.title: Wilson speaks on tariffs
events.description: President Wilson appears before Congress to speak about revising tariffs. Not since John Adams in 1800 had a President addressed Congress personally.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Republic of China recognized
events.description: President Wilson extends official recognition to the new Republic of China.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republic of China recognized

events.title: Rockefeller Foundation
events.description: In one of the largest philanthropic acts in American history, John D. Rockefeller donates $100,000,000 to begin the Rockefeller Foundation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Rockefeller Foundation

events.title: Webb Alien Land-Holding Law
events.description: In a discriminatory measure against the Japanese, Gov. Johnson signs the Webb Alien Land-Holding Law, prohibiting Japanese ownership of land in California. The statute is enacted despite the objection of President Wilson and the Japanese Government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Webb Alien Land-Holding Law

events.title: Seventeenth Amendment
events.description: The Seventeenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution is enacted, providing for the direct popular election of U.S. Previously, senators were chosen by their respective state legislatures. This amendment succeeds in diminishing the prestige of state governments and enhances popular control of the federal legislature.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Seventeenth Amendment

events.title: Foreign policy with Mexico
events.description: After considerable political instability in Mexico, following the assassination of President Francisco Madero, President Wilson declares the United States policy towards Mexico to be one of “watchful waiting.” Wilson refuses to recognize the new government of General Victoriano Huerta, who led the coup against Madero on February 22.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Foreign policy with Mexico

events.title: Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act, considerably reducing rates set by previous Republican administrations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Underwood-Simmons Tariff Act

events.title: Completing the Panama Canal
events.description: From the White House, President Wilson detonates a charge to destroy the Gamboa Dike in Panama, leading to the completion of the Panama Canal.
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Elihu Root wins Nobel Peace Prize
events.description: The Nobel Prize Committee selects Elihu Root, Theodore Roosevelt's secretary of state from 1905 to 1909, as the recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize for his work as president of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Federal Reserve Act
events.description: In an effort to safeguard America's financial institutions, the American economy, and the supply of U.S. currency, the Federal Reserve Act is signed into law. In contrast to the economies of Europe, the U.S. economy had functioned without the sophisticated management of banking ever since Andrew Jackson destroyed the Second Bank of the United States in 1830. The Federal Reserve Act created a Federal Reserve System, comprised of a Federal Reserve Board, twelve regional reserve banks, and the underpinnings of a smooth central banking system. Federal Reserve Act Signed On December 23, 1913, President Woodrow Wilson signed the Federal Reserve Act into law. The act created a Federal Reserve System, comprised of a Federal Reserve Board, twelve regional reserve banks, and the underpinnings of a smooth central banking system. [truncated]
events.impact: Stabilized the banking system

events.title: Tensions at Tampico
events.description: In the port of Tampico, Mexican officials detain several U.S. Marines from the U.S.S. Despite the their quick release and an expression of regret by President Victor Huerta, U.S. Admiral Henry T. Mayo demands that Mexican troops salute an American flag as a sign of contrition. President Huerta refuses the demanded salute on April 11; three days later President Wilson orders American warships to Tampico Bay.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Tensions at Tampico

events.title: Sanctioned force against Mexico
events.description: In order to “obtain from General Huerta and his adherents the fullest recognition of the rights and dignity of the United States,” President Wilson requests authorization from Congress to use force in Mexico. After some debate, both houses sanction such force on April 22.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Sanctioned force against Mexico

events.title: Occupation of Vera Cruz
events.description: At Vera Cruz, Mexico, U.S. forces seize the customhouse. Marines occupy the city and a detachment is sent to exact an apology from President Huerta for the arrest of several drunken U.S. sailors earlier in the month.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Occupation of Vera Cruz

events.title: Resolving the Tampico controversy
events.description: President Wilson accepts the offer of arbitration presented by the “ABC Powers” of Argentina, Brazil, and Chile to resolve the Tampico controversy. The mediation proves unnecessary when Mexican President Huerta is forced to resign on July 15.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Resolving the Tampico controversy

events.title: Mother's Day established
events.description: Congress establishes Mother's Day as the second Sunday in May.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Mother's Day established

events.title: Smith-Lever Act
events.description: Congress passes The Smith-Lever Act, providing federal funds for agricultural instruction for farmers and state college students.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Smith-Lever Act

events.title: Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated
events.description: A Serbian nationalist assassinates Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria in Sarajevo, Serbia. This event serves as the proximate cause for the termination of diplomatic relations among the major European nations, contributing to the start of World War I. One month later, Austria-Hungary declares war on Serbia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated

events.title: Germany launches war on Belgium, France, and Britain
events.description: Germany launches war on Belgium, France, and Great Britain. The United States declares its official neutrality as the Great War begins.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Germany launches war on Belgium, France, and Britain

events.title: The Panama Canal officially opens
events.description: The Panama Canal officially opens after decades of toil, controversy, and diplomatic maneuvering. Panama Canal Opens On August 15, 1914, the Panama Canal opened to trans-oceanic traffic. Due to the outbreak of World War I earlier in the month, however, there was only modest commemoration and no official visit from President Woodrow Wilson. Only a few ships a day passed through the forty miles of locks in canal in its first few years of operation; after the World War I was over, this number increased to five thousand annually. In 1903, the United States signed the Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty with Panama, which gave the United States perpetual control of the canal for a price of $10 million and an annual payment of $250,000. Work on the Panama Canal began in 1904. The building of the canal was originally under the direction of John Stevens. [truncated]
events.impact: Major infrastructure achievement, but built with human cost

events.title: Federal Trade Commission established
events.description: President Wilson signs legislation establishing the Federal Trade Commission, which is designed to regulate business conglomeration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Trade Commission established

events.title: Clayton Anti-trust Act
events.description: Signing the Clayton Anti-trust Act, President Wilson advances the third legócorporate regulationóof his “New Freedom” program. The law strengthens the original Sherman Anti-trust Act of 1890 by prohibiting exclusive sales contracts, predatory pricing, rebates, inter-corporate stock holdings, and interlocking directorates in corporations capitalized at $1 million or more in the same area of business. The act restricts the use of the injunction against labor, and it legalizes peaceful strikes, picketing, and boycotts.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Clayton Anti-trust Act

events.title: Democrats gain seats
events.description: Democrats gain five seats in the Senate giving them a 56-40 majority. Democrats in the House fare worse, losing 61 seats. Nevertheless, Wilson's party retains a 230-196 majority with nine seats held by minor parties.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats gain seats

events.title: U.S. forces in Vera Cruz withdrawn
events.description: forces in Vera Cruz, Mexico, are withdrawn as a result of the resignation of Mexican President Huerta, who fails to win Wilson's support.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. forces in Vera Cruz withdrawn
