events.title: Literacy tests for immigrants approved
events.description: Congress approves a bill requiring literacy tests for all immigrants to the United States, although President Wilson vetoes the bill on January 28. Proponents of immigration restriction argue that the United States is allowing too many ill-qualified immigrants into the country, and justify their positions by appealing to religious, ethno-cultural, or racial prejudice.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Literacy tests for immigrants approved

events.title: First transcontinental telephone call
events.description: The first transcontinental telephone call is made by the same men who had made the original telephone call in 1876. Speaking from New York City, Alexander Graham Bell tells Dr. Watson in San Francisco, “Mr. Watson, come here, I want you.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First transcontinental telephone call

events.title: Rocky Mountain National Park established
events.description: Congress establishes Rocky Mountain National Park in Colorado.
events.impact: Preserved natural lands for the public

events.title: Nevada's easy divorce bill
events.description: Nevada signs an easy divorce bill, requiring only six months' residence for a divorce to take effect.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Sinking of the Lusitania
events.description: A German U-Boat torpedoes the British passenger liner Lusitania off the coast of Ireland. The American public recoils at the loss of 1,198 civilians, including 114 Americans. The Wilson administration issues a fiery response to Germany, holding that nation responsible for the loss of American lives and the violation of American neutrality. Eager to keep the United States at bay, Berlin promptly expresses its regret but claims that the British were illegally smuggling arms aboard the ship. Lusitania Sinks On May 7, 1915, the German submarine U-20 torpedoed the British luxury liner Lusitania within sight of the Irish coast. The largest passenger ship in wartime transatlantic service at the time, the Lusitania was struck by a single torpedo and sank in twenty minutes after a second internal explosion. Of the more than 1,900 people on board, nearly 1,200 died, including 128 Americans. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Sinking of the Lusitania

events.title: Rule in U.S. Steel's favor
events.description: The District Court of New Jersey rules that U.S. Steel is a lawful corporation and not in violation of anti-trust laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Rule in U.S. Steel's favor

events.title: William Jennings Bryan resigns
events.description: William Jennings Bryan resigns as secretary of state in protest over the Wilson administration's handling of the Lusitania sinking. Bryan thinks Wilson is acting too boldly and calls on him to take a more moderate approach, banning American travel on belligerents' ships. Wilson names Robert Lansing acting secretary of state.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: William Jennings Bryan resigns

events.title: A third warning
events.description: A third Lusitania note is dispatched to Germany, warning the nation that any consequent violation of American rights would be viewed as “deliberately unfriendly.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: A third warning

events.title: U.S. Marines land in Haiti
events.description: Marines land in Haiti to restore order after the assassination of Haitian president Vilbrun Guillaume Sam. With the country suffering seemingly endless political strife, Wilson justifies the intervention as an exercise in teaching Haitians “how to elect good men.”
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: U.S. Marines land in Haiti

events.title: Haiti signs protectorate agreement
events.description: Haiti signs an agreement with the United States to become an American protectorate for ten years. forces would not leave Haiti until 1934, when President Franklin D. Roosevelt withdraws them in accordance with his “Good Neighbor” policy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Haiti signs protectorate agreement

events.title: American bankers loan to Europe
events.description: American bankers, organized under J.P. Morgan & Company, authorize a $500 million loan to the British and French governments.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American bankers loan to Europe

events.title: Georgia grants the KKK a charter
events.description: Georgia grants the Ku Klux Klan a new state charter after decades of dormancy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Georgia grants the KKK a charter

events.title: President Wilson marries Edith Bolling
events.description: President Wilson marries Edith Bolling Galt in a Washington, D.C., ceremony. The two honeymoon briefly in Virginia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Wilson marries Edith Bolling

events.title: Brushaber v. Union Pacific Railroad
events.description: In Brushaber v. Union Pacific Railroad , the federal income tax survives a Supreme Court challenge.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Brushaber v. Union Pacific Railroad

events.title: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis
events.description: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis to the Supreme Court. He is the first Jewish justice in American history.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson appoints Louis B. Brandeis

events.title: Pershing invades Mexico
events.description: General John Pershing begins a punitive expedition into Mexico, without the approval of the Mexican government, to capture Pancho Villa and his bandit force. Villa had staged raids along the U.S.-Mexico border after President Wilson failed to support his claims on the leadership of the Mexican government.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Pershing invades Mexico

events.title: Marines land in Santo Domingo
events.description: Marines land in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic, to restore political stability. The American occupation continues until 1924.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marines land in Santo Domingo

events.title: "Sussex Pledge"
events.description: Germany issues the “Sussex Pledge” after a U-Boat sinks another passenger ship, the French liner Sussex , without warning on April 24. Following protests from Washington about German unrestricted submarine attacks, the German government promises not to sink any more merchant ships without prior warning and without time for passengers and crew to abandon ship.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: "Sussex Pledge"

events.title: National Defense Act
events.description: Congress passes the National Defense Act in response to deteriorating relations between Germany and the United States. The act bolsters the standing Army to 175,000 and the National Guard to 450,000.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: National Defense Act

events.title: Republicans nominate Hughes
events.description: New York Governor Charles Evans Hughes earns the nomination for President at the Republican National Convention. Delegates select Charles Warren Fairbanks of Indiana as Hughes' running mate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans nominate Hughes

events.title: Democrats re-nominate Wilson
events.description: Democrats re-nominate Woodrow Wilson and vice president Thomas Marshall at their national convention.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats re-nominate Wilson

events.title: After U.S. forces enter his country, the Mexican c…
events.description: forces enter his country, the Mexican consul at Brownsville, Texas issues an ultimatum for their withdraw. Four days later, on June 21, American troops come under fire from Mexican forces in Carrizal with seventeen troops killed or wounded.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: After U.S. forces enter his country, the Mexican c…

events.title: Federal Farm Labor Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Federal Farm Labor Act, establishing a banking system for farmers to improve their holdings.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Farm Labor Act

events.title: Preparedness Day Parade bombing
events.description: A bomb explodes in San Francisco during a Preparedness Day parade, killing ten and wounding forty. Labor leaders Tom Mooney and Warren K. Billings are convicted in the case on dubious evidence in 1917. Mooney, originally sentenced to death, would be pardoned in 1939; Billings would be released in 1940.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Preparedness Day Parade bombing

events.title: Toms River Island explosion
events.description: An ammunition depot explodes and destroys docks at Toms River Island near Jersey City, New Jersey. Investigators blame German saboteurs in for the attack and for an explosion at a munitions plant in Kingsland, New Jersey, on January 17, 1917.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Toms River Island explosion

events.title: Purchase of Danish West Indies
events.description: and Denmark sign a treaty for the purchase of the Danish West Indies for $25 million. These became the U.S. Virgin Islands.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Purchase of Danish West Indies

events.title: National Park Service established
events.description: The National Park Service is established under the Department of the Interior.
events.impact: Preserved natural lands for the public

events.title: Adamson Eight-Hour Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Adamson Eight-Hour Act, mandating an eight-hour day standard for most railroad workers.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Nation's first birth control clinic
events.description: Margaret Sanger, Fania Mindell, and Ethel Burne open the nation's first birth control clinic in Brooklyn, New York.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nation's first birth control clinic

events.title: Woodrow Wilson reelected
events.description: Woodrow Wilson is reelected President of the United States by a 23-vote margin in the Electoral College. Wilson staves off stiff competition from Charles Evans Hughes, winning a 49.6 percent majority of the popular vote versus Hughes' 46.1 percent. Wilson runs on the slogan “He kept us out of War” despite the growing implausibility of U.S. neutrality in the Great War. The election hinged on Wilson's slim 4,000-vote majority in California, where Hughes' loss of support from Governor Hiram Johnson may have cost him the election. In congressional elections, the Democrats maintain a 53-42 majority in the Senate and a thin 216-210 majority in the House of Representatives.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Woodrow Wilson reelected

events.title: Wilson's dispatch to Europe
events.description: In an effort to mediate a settlement to the battlefield stalemate in Europe, President Wilson dispatches identical peace notes to all the belligerents, asking for the war aims of each.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson's dispatch to Europe

events.title: Wilson's "Peace without victory" speech
events.description: President Wilson criticizes the European powers' war aims in a speech in the Senate, urging the combatants to accept “peace without victory” to ensure a settlement free of rancor that could ignite another war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson's "Peace without victory" speech

events.title: Pershing recalled
events.description: The War Department recalls U.S. forces under General Pershing from Mexico after searching in vain for Pancho Villa for almost a year.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Pershing recalled

events.title: Germany resumes submarine warfare
events.description: The German government informs the United States that its naval forces will resume unrestricted submarine warfare in the Atlantic the next day.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Germany resumes submarine warfare

events.title: U.S. severs relations with Germany
events.description: In reaction to the German resumption of unrestricted attacks against merchant shipping, the United States severs diplomatic relations with Germany.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. severs relations with Germany

events.title: Congress overrides Immigration Act veto
events.description: Congress overrides President Wilson's veto of the Immigration Act, which requires a literacy test for immigrants and restricts the entry of Asian laborers not covered by separate diplomatic agreements.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Congress overrides Immigration Act veto

events.title: The Zimmermann Telegram
events.description: British officials present Walter Hines Page, U. ambassador to Great Britain, with a coded message from German foreign minister Alfred Zimmermann to the German ambassador of Mexico. The note instructs its recipient to seek a German-Mexican alliance in the event of war with the United States, and authorizes the German ambassador to offer the Mexican government the return of territory it lost to the United States in the Mexican-American war in return for Mexican military involvement.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Zimmermann Telegram

events.title: Zimmermann Telegram released
events.description: The White House releases the contents of the Zimmermann Telegram to the press, three days after Wilson asks Congress for the authority to arm merchant ships.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Zimmermann Telegram released

events.title: Wilson inaugurated
events.description: President Woodrow Wilson and Vice President Thomas Marshall are inaugurated for second terms. In his inaugural address, Wilson reiterates the U.S. stance on neutrality but clearly hints at the almost certain likeliness of American intervention in the World War. Wilson declares that “The tragic events of the thirty months of vital turmoil through which we have just passed have made us citizens of the world. There can be no turning back. Our own fortunes as a nation are involved whether we would have it so or not.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson inaugurated

events.title: Wilson adresses Congress
events.description: As the 65th Congress opens its first session, President Wilson asks for a declaration of war against Germany. Wilson argues that the United States needs to wage war to “make the world safe for democracy.”
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Wilson adresses Congress

events.title: Jeannette Rankin, first woman in the House
events.description: The first woman in the House of Representatives, Rep. Jeannette Rankin (R-MT), is seated.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jeannette Rankin, first woman in the House

events.title: United States Declares War on Germany
events.description: On April 6, 1917, the United States declared war on Germany. Although President Woodrow Wilson had campaigned for reelection in 1916 emphasizing how he had kept the United States out of the war, he soon realized that the United States could not stand by and remain neutral in the Great War. At the end of January 1917, German U-Boats resumed unrestricted submarine warfare, attacking ships in the Atlantic Ocean. Shortly afterwards, the British released the Zimmermann telegram to the American government. The telegram revealed a German plot to try to entice Mexico into joining against the United States. President Wilson told the nation at his second inaugural on March 5 that he felt the United States had no control over its neutral status and that outside pressures “have drawn us more and more irresistibly into their current and influence.” Nevertheless, Wilson remained locked in a remarkable struggle between conflicting principles in his own ideology over the decision whether to go to war. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: United States Declares War on Germany

events.title: Committee on Public Information
events.description: President Wilson issues an executive order creating the Committee on Public Information and appoints Denver journalist George Creel as its head. The CPI coordinates propaganda and censorship efforts for the federal government throughout the war.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Committee on Public Information

events.title: Liberty Loan drive
events.description: President Wilson signs a bill instituting the first Liberty Loan drive, authorizing Secretary of Treasury William G. McAdoo to sell $3 billion of debt at 3.5 percent to the public.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Liberty Loan drive

events.title: Selective Service Act
events.description: Congress passes the Selective Service Act, requiring all men between the ages of 21 and 30 to register with locally administered draft boards for a federal draft lottery. It is the first conscription act in the United States since the Civil War.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Selective Service Act

events.title: Espionage Act
events.description: Congress approves the Espionage Act, which President Wilson had requested in his April 2 speech. The act severely limits freedom of expression, mandating that public criticism of the military or the government be punished by a $10,000 fine or up to twenty years in jail.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Espionage Act

events.title: U.S. troops arrive in France
events.description: The first U.S. troops arrive in France at St
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. troops arrive in France

events.title: Industrial Workers of the World raids
events.description: Federal agents stage raids against the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW) in twenty-four cities, seizing literature and arresting ten, including William “Big Bill” Haywood.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Industrial Workers of the World raids

events.title: Fighting at the Rhine
events.description: The first engagement involving U.S. forces in Europe takes place near the Rhine-Marne Canal in France.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Fighting at the Rhine

events.title: Women win the right to vote
events.description: Women in New York win the right to vote in accordance with a state constitutional amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Women win the right to vote

events.title: The 42nd “Rainbow Division”
events.description: 42nd “Rainbow Division” arrives in France, comprised of troops from every state in the Union. Colonel Douglas MacArthur proclaims, “The 42nd Division stretches like a Rainbow from one end of America to the other.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The 42nd “Rainbow Division”

events.title: Eighteenth Amendment
events.description: Congress submits the Eighteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution to the states for ratification. The amendment forbids the sale, manufacture, or transport of alcohol except under special circumstances.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Eighteenth Amendment

events.title: Wilson's "14 Points"
events.description: In an address to Congress, President Wilson lists his “14 Points” for a just and lasting peace. His objectives include the self-determination of nations, free trade, disarmament, a pact to end secret treaties, and a league of nations to realize collective security. This speech becomes the basis for Wilson's peace proposals at the end of the war. The Fourteen Points On January 8, 1918, President Woodrow Wilson gave a speech to Congress in which he presented his Fourteen Points that outlined his program of peace to end World War I. The first five points called for an end to secret treaties, freedom of the seas, free trade, reduction of arms, and adjustment of colonial claims, taking into account the wishes of the colonial population. Wilson's sixth point called for Germany to withdraw from Russian territory and for Russian self-determination of its own government. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Wilson's "14 Points"

events.title: Hoover calls for food conservation
events.description: To promote food conservation, food administrator Herbert Hoover calls for one meatless day, two wheatless days, and two porkless days each week.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: Sedition Act
events.description: Congress passes the Sedition Act, which couples with the Espionage Act to limit freedom of expression during the war. The Sedition Act grants the Postmaster General the right to ban the mailing of publications deemed subversive, and erects heavy penalties for those criticizing the government or the war effort.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sedition Act

events.title: War Industries Board
events.description: President Wilson issues an executive order creating the War Industries Board, an agency designed to coordinate wartime production and transportation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: War Industries Board

events.title: Holding Germans at Chateau-Thierry
events.description: Second Division blunts a German advance on Paris at Chateau-Thierry.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Holding Germans at Chateau-Thierry

events.title: Countering at Belleau Wood
events.description: Second Division and Fourth Marine Brigade counter a German offensive in the battle of Belleau Wood during World War I.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Countering at Belleau Wood

events.title: Cost of living in NY
events.description: The Labor Department announces that the cost of living jumped seventeen percent in New York City from July 1917 to July 1918.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cost of living in NY

events.title: Eugene Debs sentenced
events.description: Prominent socialist and presidential candidate Eugene V. Debs is sentenced to a ten-year jail term for violating the Espionage Act, the result of an antiwar speech he delivered in Canton, Ohio, on June 30.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Eugene Debs sentenced

events.title: Wilson defends women's suffrage
events.description: President Wilson addresses the Senate with the message that voting rights for women is a “vitally necessary war measure.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Wilson defends women's suffrage

events.title: Global influenza
events.description: Known as "Spanish flu," the world-wide influenza pandemic reaches its height in the United States. The extremely virulent strain of the disease first develops in east-coast cities and spreads rapidly across the country and the Atlantic as a result of war-related transportation. The epidemic eventually claims more than 600,000 lives in the United States (more than the Great War) and perhaps 20 million globally.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Global influenza

events.title: Republicans win majorities
events.description: Republicans win majorities in both houses of Congress, securing a two-seat majority in the Senate and a comfortable cushion of fifty votes in the House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans win majorities

events.title: Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicates
events.description: Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicates the throne of the German Empire after revolution breaks out in Germany.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Kaiser Wilhelm II abdicates

events.title: Allied and German armistice
events.description: Allied and German military leaders implement an armistice. The new German government issues an appeal to President Wilson to negotiate peace along the lines he enumerated in his Fourteen Points speech.
events.impact: Ended hostilities

events.title: Wilson announces Paris Peace Conference
events.description: Wilson announces he will attend the Paris Peace Conference.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson announces Paris Peace Conference

events.title: Wartime Prohibition Act
events.description: President Wilson signs the Wartime Prohibition Act, banning the manufacture of alcohol for domestic sale effective from June 30, 1919, until demobilization.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wartime Prohibition Act

events.title: The Paris Peace Conference opens
events.description: The Paris Peace Conference opens, two weeks after President Wilson receives glowing welcomes in Rome and Paris.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The Paris Peace Conference opens

events.title: Eighteenth Amendment ratified
events.description: The State Department announces the ratification of the Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution as of January 16, 1919, when Nebraska's approval achieved the amendment's required three-fourths majority. A nation-wide ban on the sale, distribution, or production of alcoholic beverages will go into effect on January 16, 1920.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Eighteenth Amendment ratified

events.title: Wilson presents League of Nations draft
events.description: President Wilson presents his draft for the League of Nations covenant to the Paris Peace Conference.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson presents League of Nations draft

events.title: Schenck v. United States
events.description: The Supreme Court upholds the constitutionality of the Espionage Act in Schenck v. United States , establishing that civil liberties can be restricted by the government if there is a “clear and present danger” to law and order.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Schenck v. United States

events.title: Nineteenth Amendment
events.description: Congress adopts the Nineteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, giving women the franchise. The joint resolution reads: “The right of citizens of the U.S. to vote shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or by any State on account of sex.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nineteenth Amendment

events.title: Treaty of Versailles, League of Nations
events.description: After failing to secure a peace without rancorous provisions from his fellow Allied leaders, President Wilson submits the Treaty of Versailles and League of Nations to the Senate for ratification. Senatorial deliberation on the treaty will last longer than the Paris Conference itself.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Versailles, League of Nations

events.title: Communist Labor Party of America founded
events.description: The Communist Labor Party of America is founded in Chicago and adopts the platform of the Third International as its own. Wilson Embarks on League of Nations Tour On September 3, 1919, President Woodrow Wilson boarded a train to begin a transcontinental speaking tour to try to build support for the League of Nations and the Treaty of Versailles, which ended World War I. He gave his first speech in Columbus, Ohio, on September 4. President Wilson had traveled to Europe in December 1918 to attend the Paris Peace Conference with representatives from Britain, France, and Italy. Germany signed the Treaty of Versailles on June 28, and Wilson returned to the United States on July 8. Two days later, he submitted the Treaty of Versailles to the Senate for ratification. Senatorial approval of the treaty faced an uphill battle. A number of senators remained skeptical of the League of Nations Covenant contained with the treaty. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Communist Labor Party of America founded

events.title: Wilson embarks on nation-wide tour
events.description: Against the advice of his doctors and advisors, President Wilson opens his nation-wide speaking tour to promote the Treaty of Versailles and the League of Nations in Columbus, Ohio.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson embarks on nation-wide tour

events.title: Boston police strike
events.description: Police in Boston walk out on strike.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Boston police strike

events.title: Wilson suffers stroke
events.description: President Wilson suffers a serious stroke in Wichita, Kansas, in the middle of his national speaking tour and returns to Washington, DC.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson suffers stroke

events.title: Volstead Act
events.description: Congress passes the Volstead Act over President Wilson's veto to provide enforcement power to the Eighteenth Amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Volstead Act

events.title: Treaty of Versailles fails
events.description: After a lengthy national debate, the Treaty of Versailles fails to achieve ratification in the Senate by a vote of 53 to 38.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Versailles fails

events.title: "Red Scare" raids
events.description: Foreign-born radicals arrested by the Department of Justice in the “Red Scare” raids of 1919 are deported, leaving from New York harbor on the U.S. transport Buford , popularly referred to as the “Soviet Ark,” bound for the U.S.S.R.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: "Red Scare" raids

events.title: Palmer's extensive raids
events.description: Attorney General A. Mitchell Palmer stages the most extensive series of raids of the entire “Red Scare,” arresting nearly 2,700 people in 33 cities.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Palmer's extensive raids

events.title: Treaty of Versailles defeated, again
events.description: The Senate defeats a resubmitted version of the Treaty of Versailles with reservations added by Foreign Relations Committee chairman Henry Cabot Lodge.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Treaty of Versailles defeated, again

events.title: U.S. withdraws from Siberia
events.description: forces cease their operations in support of counter-revolutionary forces in Siberia and are withdrawn.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. withdraws from Siberia

events.title: Sacco and Vanzetti murder case
events.description: Shoe factory employees Frank Parmenter and Alexander Berardelli are murdered in a robbery in South Braintree, Massachusetts. Immigrant laborers Nicola Sacco and Bartolomeo Vanzetti are arrested three weeks later for the crimes in what becomes one of the most politically charged murder cases of the early twentieth century.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sacco and Vanzetti murder case

events.title: Wilson vetoes end of war
events.description: Congress passes a joint resolution declaring an end to the war with Germany. President Wilson vetoes the resolution.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wilson vetoes end of war

events.title: Republicans nominate Harding
events.description: Republicans gather in Chicago to select candidates for the presidential and vice presidential elections. After party leaders break the convention deadlock in what one attendee calls a behind-the-scenes deal “in a smoke-filled room,” Ohio Senator Warren G. Harding is nominated for the presidency. Massachusetts governor Calvin Coolidge receives the vice-presidential nomination.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans nominate Harding

events.title: Democrats nominate Cox and Roosevelt
events.description: Ohio governor James M. Cox and Franklin Delano Roosevelt of New York receive the nominations for President and vice president at the Democratic National Convention in San Francisco.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Cox and Roosevelt

events.title: Nineteenth Amendment Ratified
events.description: On August 18, 1920, the Nineteenth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States was ratified. The amendment gave women the right to vote. Women activists in the United States had agitated for equal political rights since the mid-nineteenth century. Reformers in the Progressive Era succeeded in making suffrage a significant political issue, and four western states granted women the right to vote in state constitutions. However, business groups and conservative women's organizations opposed women's suffrage and blocked federal efforts. During World War I, women contributed greatly to the war effort through their labor and volunteer efforts. Women activists capitalized on women's newfound economic prominence during the war to agitate further for suffrage. Activist organizations such as the National American Woman Suffrage Association (NAWSA), which had pursued women's suffrage since 1890, connected the issue of a lasting peace with the extension of voting rights to women. [truncated]
events.impact: Positive impact: Nineteenth Amendment Ratified

events.title: Nineteenth Amendment becomes law
events.description: The Nineteenth Amendment, granting women the right to vote, officially becomes law.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nineteenth Amendment becomes law

events.title: Harding denounces League of Nations
events.description: In a speech given from his front porch in Marion, Ohio, Harding denounces the League of Nations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harding denounces League of Nations

events.title: Warren G. Harding elected
events.description: Harding is elected the twenty-ninth President of the United States with an overwhelming 404 electoral votes (60.3 percent of the popular vote to Democratic rival James Cox's 127 electoral votes (only 34.1 percent of the popular vote). Debs garners nearly one million popular votes for the Socialist Party despite his imprisonment for violating the Espionage Act the previous year. The election splits the North and South, with Cox winning all states (except for Tennessee) below the Mason-Dixon line and Harding winning the rest.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Warren G. Harding elected

events.title: Wilson wins Nobel Peace Prize
events.description: Woodrow Wilson wins the Nobel Peace Prize for his efforts to secure a lasting peace after the Great War.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Census Bureau report
events.description: Census Bureau reports that for the first time in American history, 51 percent of Americans live in cities and towns of more than 2500 people.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Census Bureau report

events.title: Warren G. Harding inaugurated
events.description: Harding is inaugurated as the twenty-ninth President of the United States. Described by one contemporary as a “great looking President,” Harding lacks experience in international affairs, reflecting the general disinterest of the American public toward such issues.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Warren G. Harding inaugurated

events.title: Thompson-Urrutia Treaty
events.description: The Thompson-Urrutia Treaty with Colombia is ratified. The treaty grants Colombia $25 million as compensation for the loss of Panama, which had gained its independence in 1903 with the help of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Thompson-Urrutia Treaty

events.title: Emergency Quota Act
events.description: Harding signs the Emergency Quota Act into law, limiting the number of immigrants from any given country to 3 percent of that nationality already in the United States by 1910. The temporary act lasts three years and serves as the precursor to the harsher and permanent 1924 act. The law represents the growing nativism of the 1920s, motivated, in part, by the massive influx of south and east European immigrants into the United States following the end of World War I.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Emergency Quota Act

events.title: Emergency Tariff Act
events.description: In response to American public opinion, Harding and Congress pass the Emergency Tariff Act. Raising tariffs, especially on farm products, the temporary bill will be replaced one year later by the Fordney-McCumber Tariff Act, a permanent bill with even higher tariff rates. Designed to protect American products and end the post-war recession, such protectionist legislation ultimately destabilizes international commerce by heightening economic nationalism.
events.impact: Economic downturn causing job losses

events.title: Denby transfers oil control
events.description: In a relatively unnoticed move, Secretary of the Navy Edwin Denby transfers control of the naval oil reserves in California and Wyoming to the Department of the Interior, headed by Albert B. The reserves at Teapot Dome, Wyoming, will later figure prominently in the scandals that stain the Harding administration.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Denby transfers oil control

events.title: Budget and Accounting Act
events.description: Harding signs the Budget and Accounting Act in order to better organize the federal government's accounts. The act establishes the Bureau of the Budget and the General Accounting Office under the Treasury Department.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Budget and Accounting Act

events.title: Alice Robertson presides over House
events.description: Alice Robertson of Oklahoma becomes the first woman to preside over the House of Representatives. Her session lasts thirty minutes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alice Robertson presides over House

events.title: Harding appoints Taft Chief Justice
events.description: Harding appoints former President William Howard Taft Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harding appoints Taft Chief Justice

events.title: Official end of war with Germany
events.description: Harding signs a joint congressional resolution declaring the official end of war with Germany. The question of reparations will continue to be debated over the next few years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Official end of war with Germany

events.title: Hoover presides over unemployment conference
events.description: Secretary of Commerce Herbert Hoover presides over a conference on unemployment in Washington, D.C., as unemployment reaches a post-war high of 5.7 million. In addition, the nation witnesses a wave of violence by a revitalized Ku Klux Klan. Blacks, returning from the war, are not as ready to return to their previous condition of subservience and are met by whippings, brandings, and lynchings by the KKK.
events.impact: Racial terrorism and murder

events.title: Washington Naval Armament Conference
events.description: The United States convenes the Washington Naval Armament Conference. Along with major naval powers Britain, France, Italy, and Japan, the United States signs a treaty limiting capital ship tonnage. The conference will also produce a larger agreement that also includes China, the Netherlands, Belgium, and Portugal which recognizes America's Open Door Policy toward China as international policy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Washington Naval Armament Conference

events.title: Sheppard-Towner Maternity and Infancy Act
events.description: In response to reports indicating that fully 80 percent of American women do not receive adequate prenatal care, Harding signs the Sheppard-Towner Maternity and Infancy Act, granting matching federal funds to states for maternal and child care. The legislation also recognizes the emergent political power of women, a constituency which gained the right to vote during the previous year. Harding Signs Sheppard-Towner Act On November 23, 1921, President Warren Harding signed the Sheppard-Towner Maternity and Infancy Protection Act, which contributed matching federal funds to states to establish and run prenatal and child health care centers. Although it was not a strong act, it was still a significant move by the federal government toward providing public health care to mothers and infants. Reformers had sought similar legislation since 1917, but it was not until 1921 that a number of factors combined to push it through. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Sheppard-Towner Maternity and Infancy Act

events.title: Harding pardons Eugene Debs
events.description: Harding pardons Eugene Debs along with twenty-three others found guilty under the wartime Espionage Act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harding pardons Eugene Debs

events.title: Capper-Volstead Act
events.description: Responding to the continuing problems facing American farmers, which force 300,000 farm foreclosures during the Harding administration alone, the President signs the Capper-Volstead Act. The bill allows farmers to buy and sell cooperatively without the risk of prosecution under anti-trust laws.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Capper-Volstead Act

events.title: Nineteenth Amendment deemed constitutional
events.description: The Supreme Court unanimously finds the Nineteenth Amendment, providing for women's suffrage, constitutional.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Nineteenth Amendment deemed constitutional

events.title: The Teapot Dome Scandal
events.description: Interior Secretary Albert B. Fall leases the Teapot Dome oil reserves to Harry Sinclair, setting in motion what comes to be known over the next two years as the Teapot Dome scandal.
events.impact: Government corruption scandal

events.title: Federal Narcotics Control Board
events.description: Harding signs into law the creation of the Federal Narcotics Control Board.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Narcotics Control Board

events.title: United Mine Workers v. Coronado Coal Co.
events.description: In United Mine Workers v. Coronado Coal Co. , the Supreme Court rules, under the Sherman Act, that striking miners are liable for damage inflicted upon company property. The ruling is the first in a number of federal efforts to control organized labor that have become increasingly volatile during the postwar recession. The next month, another strike will break out after the Railroad Labor Board reduces wages. In September, the attorney general will win an injunction against the striking railroad workers as the two sides continue to battle.
events.impact: Economic downturn causing job losses

events.title: Harding vetoes Soldiers' Bonus Bill
events.description: Harding vetoes the Soldiers' Bonus Bill, arguing that balancing the budget takes precedence over the nation's debt to veterans of the Great War. The bill will later pass over the veto of then-president Calvin Coolidge.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Harding vetoes Soldiers' Bonus Bill

events.title: Cable Act
events.description: The Cable Act, which allows an American woman to maintain her citizenship following marriage to an alien, is signed by Harding.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cable Act

events.title: Rebecca L. Felton's symbolic appointment
events.description: Filling a vacancy caused by death, Rebecca L. Felton becomes the first female senator following her appointment by the Governor of Georgia. The appointment is more symbolic than real as the term ends the following day.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Rebecca L. Felton's symbolic appointment

events.title: Secretary of the Interior resigns
events.description: As the Teapot Dome scandal begins to unfold, Harding accepts the resignation of his Secretary of the Interior, Albert Fall.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Secretary of the Interior resigns

events.title: Final troops leave Germany
events.description: The final American troops leave Germany as Harding issues an executive order halting U.S. occupation of the Rhine.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Final troops leave Germany

events.title: Head of Veterans' Bureau resigns
events.description: Charles Forbes, head of the Veterans' Bureau, resigns in anticipation of the Senate investigation of his department. He will later be indicted and convicted on charges of fraud, conspiracy, and bribery. The case will speed inquiries into Teapot Dome and set off a media frenzy against what is increasingly viewed as a corrupt presidency. Over the next few months, two different officials will commit suicide, further discrediting the administration.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Head of Veterans' Bureau resigns

events.title: Adkins v. Children's Hospital
events.description: The federal government's battle with organized labor continues as the Supreme Court rules in Adkins v. Children's Hospital that the minimum wage law for women and children adopted in Washington, D.C., is unconstitutional.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Adkins v. Children's Hospital

events.title: New York repeals prohibition enforcement
events.description: In what many regard as the beginning of the end for prohibition, New York State disregards Harding's warnings and repeals its enforcement act.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New York repeals prohibition enforcement

events.title: Harding leaves for transcontinental speaking tour
events.description: Harding and his wife leave for his “voyage of understanding,” a transcontinental speaking tour across Alaska and the West designed to bolster faith in the Harding administration amid the various scandals emerging seemingly daily.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Harding leaves for transcontinental speaking tour

events.title: Harding suffers ptomaine poisoning, dies
events.description: In late July, Harding, traveling from Alaska to San Francisco, suffers an attack of ptomaine poisoning and develops pneumonia. Although he initially appears to recover, Harding's health had been in decline for at least six months and the grueling schedule of his speaking tour appears to be too much for him. He dies with his wife by his side in a San Francisco hotel room on the evening of August 2. The scandals surrounding his presidency initially lead to rumors that foul play was involved in his death. While these claims will be disproved and Harding himself is never found to be directly involved in his administration's corruption, the scandals will nonetheless tarnish his presidential legacy. President Harding Dies On August 2, 1923, President Warren Harding died in San Francisco, California, while on a speaking tour. His death was most likely due to a heart attack. [truncated]
events.impact: Government corruption

events.title: Calvin Coolidge sworn in
events.description: In a simple 2:30 a.m. ceremony, presided over by his father at his home in Plymouth, Vermont, Calvin Coolidge is sworn in as the thirtieth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Calvin Coolidge sworn in

events.title: Calvin Coolidge sworn in
events.description: At a 2:30 a.m. ceremony in Plymouth, Vermont, Calvin Coolidge is sworn in by his father as the thirtieth President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Calvin Coolidge sworn in

events.title: Martial law and the KKK
events.description: Walton places Oklahoma under martial law in order to suppress the increasing terrorism of the Ku Klux Klan, which has reemerged in the South and Midwest in response to worsening economic conditions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Martial law and the KKK

events.title: Coolidge's first State of the Union
events.description: In his first State of the Union address, Coolidge expresses his support for prohibition and U.S. involvement in the World Court. He also states his opposition to government interference with business and calls for lowering taxes, thereby extending Harding's policies. It is the first broadcast of an official presidential address, made possible by the more than 2.5 million radios in U.S. homes; in 1920 there had been less than 5,000.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Coolidge's first State of the Union

events.title: Soldiers' Bonus Bill passes
events.description: Providing twenty-year annuities for veterans at an overall cost of $2 billion, the Soldiers' Bonus Bill is passed by the House. One month later, the Senate also passes the bill only to have Coolidge veto it; Congress will later override the veto.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Soldiers' Bonus Bill passes

events.title: Pact of Anapala
events.description: Representatives from Nicaragua, Guatemala, and El Salvador sign the Pact of Anapala with the United States, agreeing to cut off aid to the insurgent forces in neighboring Honduras threatening to overthrow President Rafael Gutierrez. This was one of many attempts by the United States, which had first sent Marines to Honduras as early as 1919, to keep Gutierrez in power. These efforts ultimately failed when insurgent leader Tiburcio Carias became dictator in 1933.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Pact of Anapala

events.title: New immigration law
events.description: Congress passes a new immigration law with even more restrictive quotas than those established by a temporary act two years earlier. Japanese immigrants are barred completely while Canadians and Mexicans remain exempted from the quotas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: New immigration law

events.title: Parties nominate their candidates
events.description: The national political parties hold conventions to nominate presidential and vice-presidential candidates for the upcoming elections. The Republican Party nominates Coolidge and Charles G. Dawes; the Democrats, John W. Davis and Charles W. Bryan; and the Progressives, Robert La Follette and Burton K
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Parties nominate their candidates

events.title: Dawes Plan signed
events.description: The Dawes Plan is signed by the United States, France, Great Britain, Italy, and Belgium to solve the German reparations problem and to end the occupation of the Ruhr by French and Belgium troops. Overseen by Secretary of State Charles Evans Hughes, the plan was drawn up by Coolidge's running-mate, Charles G. Dawes, and based the reparations schedule on what Germany could pay rather than on what she could be forced to pay. For his part, Dawes would win the Nobel Peace Prize the following year.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Marines withdraw from Dominican Republic
events.description: The last U.S. Marines, first sent to Santo Domingo in 1916 by Woodrow Wilson, withdraw from the Dominican Republic, finalizing a process begun three years earlier by Secretary of State Charles Evans Hughes. During this period, the United States helped the country prepare for free elections and, ultimately, independence. The withdrawal reversed decades of U.S. involvement in the Dominican Republic under the auspices of the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marines withdraw from Dominican Republic

events.title: Coolidge elected
events.description: Coolidge wins the election easily with 382 electoral votes (15,725,000 popular votes) to the Democrats' 136 (8,386,000 popular votes). La Follette wins a surprisingly large 4,823,000 popular votes, though only 13 electoral votes. Coolidge Wins Election On November 4, 1924, Calvin Coolidge was elected President of the United States. Vice President Coolidge had assumed the office of the presidency the year before after President Warren Harding died. But Coolidge then had to convince the American public to elect him President in his own right. Coolidge had little time to enjoy his new job as President before he had to begin campaigning to keep the position. Indeed, at the time of Harding's death in August 1923, the President was just nearing the end of his two-month trip across the country designed to repair his and his party's image before the 1924 presidential campaign. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Coolidge elected

events.title: Coolidge inaugurated
events.description: Coolidge is inaugurated President in his first elected term. The following day, Frank B. Kellogg is named Secretary of State, replacing Charles Evans Hughes, who had resigned two months earlier. A lawyer by training, Kellogg had previously served as a senator from Minnesota and ambassador to Great Britain.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Coolidge inaugurated

events.title: Isle of Pines Treaty ratified
events.description: The Isle of Pines Treaty is finally ratified by the Senate. Pending since 1904, the treaty recognizes Cuban possession of the Isle of Pines.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Isle of Pines Treaty ratified

events.title: Scopes trial
events.description: Scopes, a public school teacher in Dayton, Tennessee, is indicted for teaching Darwin's theory of evolution. His trial in July captures national attention as William Jennings Bryan is called on behalf of the prosecution and Clarence Darrow, the skilled and nationally-renowned attorney, agrees to represent Scopes. Although Scopes ultimately loses and pays a $100 fine, the trial serves as a national debate between Darrow and Bryan, science and religion.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Scopes trial

events.title: KKK demonstrates in Washington
events.description: The Ku Klux Klan holds a massive political demonstration in Washington, D.C. Possibly the largest Klan parade in history, around 40,000 men and women march down Pennsylvania Avenue decked out in their white Klan robes, a scene which reflects the group's resurgence during the 1920s.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: KKK demonstrates in Washington

events.title: The U.S. and the World Court
events.description: The Senate adopts a resolution allowing the United States to join the World Court in the event that U.S. participation accord with five separate conditions. All but one are satisfied; failure to meet every condition leads the Senate to reject full U.S. participation. While America will work with the World Court and the League of Nations over the next decade, it never becomes a member of either.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The U.S. and the World Court

events.title: Revenue Act becomes law
events.description: Coolidge signs the Revenue Act into law, as Harding's policy of “normalcy” morphs into keeping “cool with Coolidge.” With the goal of cutting the size of the Federal government, the Act reduces income taxes as well as other duties. While it helps the Republican Party weather the investigations of corruption under Harding, it further weakens the already deteriorating national economy.
events.impact: Government corruption

events.title: Cancelling French debt
events.description: France and the United States sign an agreement that eventually cancels sixty percent of the French debt from the Great War.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Cancelling French debt

events.title: First successful flight over North Pole
events.description: Rear Admiral Richard E. Byrd and Floyd Bennett make the first successful flight over the North Pole.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First successful flight over North Pole

events.title: Marines land in Nicaragua
events.description: Marines land in Nicaragua to quiet a revolt. America military forces will maintain a presence in Nicaragua until 1933.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Marines land in Nicaragua

events.title: Air Commerce Act passed
events.description: The Air Commerce Act is passed by Congress. While the federal government already subsidized airmail, this act gave the Commerce Department regulatory powers over sectors of the aviation industry, such as the licensing of pilots and aircrafts.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Air Commerce Act passed

events.title: Army Air Corps, Distinguished Flying Cross
events.description: Congress establishes the Army Air Corps and the Distinguished Flying Cross.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Army Air Corps, Distinguished Flying Cross

events.title: Naval depot explodes
events.description: A naval ammunition depot at Lake Denmark, New Jersey, explodes after it is struck by lightning. With explosions continuing for several days, 31 dead, and $93 million in damages, it is the worst such disaster in the history of the U.S
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Naval depot explodes

events.title: Court gives President right to remove
events.description: The Supreme Court rules that the President has the right to remove cabinet members at his own discretion. The ruling nullifies the 1868 Tenure of Office Act, which required consent of the Senate in order to restrict the powers of President Andrew Johnson during Reconstruction.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Court gives President right to remove

events.title: U.S. and Canadian relations
events.description: The United States and Canada establish diplomatic relations independent of Britain.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Federal Radio Commission created
events.description: Congress creates the Federal Radio Commission to regulate this burgeoning field of national and international communication. In doing so, lawmakers continue the trend of imposing increasing federal regulation on private sectors of the economy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal Radio Commission created

events.title: Voting restrictions unconstitutional
events.description: The Supreme Court rules that a Texas law prohibiting black people from voting in Democratic primaries is unconstitutional.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Voting restrictions unconstitutional

events.title: Lindbergh completes his flight
events.description: Lindbergh completes the first transatlantic flight, traversing the distance from New York to Paris in his monoplane, the Spirit of St. Louis , in less than thirty-four hours. A year later, Amelia Earhart will become the first woman to make the flight.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lindbergh completes his flight

events.title: Coolidge chooses not to run
events.description: Concerned that four more years in office might appear to some observers as a third term as President, Coolidge ends any talk of his candidacy for the 1928 election stating, “I do not choose to run.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Coolidge chooses not to run

events.title: Sacco and Vanzetti executed
events.description: Nicola Sacco and Bartolomeo Vanzetti are executed in Massachusetts for their alleged 1920 murder of a factory guard, despite protests that the two men had been unfairly prosecuted for their radical beliefs.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Sacco and Vanzetti executed

events.title: The first “talkie"
events.description: The first “talkie,” The Jazz Singer , starring Al Jolson, is released.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The first “talkie"

events.title: Conflict in Mexico's Constitution
events.description: Reversing its 1917 Constitution, Mexico's Congress grants unlimited concessions to foreigners for lands on which they engaged in any “positive” acts prior to May 1917. Soon after, however, the government revokes all rights to oil-rich territories, generating fierce protest by American businessmen. The United States recognizes new president Alvaro Obregon on the condition that he grant American firms subsoil rights. Under such pressure, the Mexican Supreme Court rules the law unconstitutional, returning all rights back to American companies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Conflict in Mexico's Constitution

events.title: South America opposed Roosevelt Corollary
events.description: At the Sixth International Conference of American States in Havana, Cuba, South American countries introduce a resolution opposing U.S. invocation of the Roosevelt Corollary. By the end of the year, the State Department will issue a statement redefining the Monroe Doctrine as a policy which “does not concern itself with purely inter-American relations.” This redefinition brought an end to the Roosevelt Corollary in word, if not in deed.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: South America opposed Roosevelt Corollary

events.title: Democrats nominate Alfred Smith
events.description: The Democratic Party nominates Alfred E. Smith for President and Joseph T. Robinson for vice president. A Catholic, Smith will have his loyalty questioned during the campaign as religious prejudice plays a key role in the election.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Alfred Smith

events.title: U.S. recognizes nationalist Chinese government
events.description: The United States recognizes Chiang Kai-shek's Kuomintang (KMT) nationalist government of China and signs a tariff treaty with the Chinese.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. recognizes nationalist Chinese government

events.title: Byrd flies south
events.description: Byrd begins the first leg of his flight to the South Pole in Antarctica. Reaching the pole in November, Byrd explores and studies the continent for the next twenty years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Byrd flies south

events.title: The Kellogg-Briand Pact
events.description: The Kellogg-Briand Pact, or the Pact of Paris, as it was also known, is signed by the United States and fifteen other nations. Named for its two principal authors, Secretary of State Frank B. Kellogg and French foreign minister Aristide Briand, the pact outlaws war as a means to settle disputes, substituting diplomacy and world opinion for armed conflict. Ultimately signed by 62 nations, the pact is more symbolic than practical, though Kellogg would win the Nobel Peace Prize for his efforts the following year.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Hoover elected
events.description: Hoover wins the presidential election in an apparent landslide, 444 electoral votes to Smith's 87. In fact, the popular vote shows a much closer race, with 21,392,000 for Hoover and 15,016,000 for Smith.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hoover elected

events.title: Herbert Hoover inaugurated
events.description: Herbert Hoover is inaugurated as the thirty-first President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Herbert Hoover inaugurated

events.title: Stimson appointed Secretary of State
events.description: Hoover appoints Henry L. Stimson Secretary of State.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Stimson appointed Secretary of State

events.title: State Department intervenes for Standard Oil
events.description: The State Department begins its effort to help Standard Oil of California (SOCAL) attain oil rights in Bahrain from the Gulf Oil Company. Since the 1880s, the Sheikhdom of Bahrain had been a British protectorate and by treaty was required to sell its oil to British companies. Through a Canadian subsidiary the two sides were able to agree to terms and by 1935, SOCAL would have 16 operating oil wells in Bahrain.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: State Department intervenes for Standard Oil

events.title: Police raid New York birth control center
events.description: New York City police raid the Birth Control Clinical Research Center established by Margaret Sanger, arresting two doctors and three nurses, and confiscating numerous records. Physicians and private citizens are outraged by the incident and a month later the case will be thrown out of court as a violation of a physician's right to practice medicine.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Police raid New York birth control center

events.title: First annual Academy Awards
events.description: The first annual Academy Awards are presented by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First annual Academy Awards

events.title: The pocket veto
events.description: Supreme Court upholds the use of the pocket veto by the President for the purpose of blocking legislation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: The pocket veto

events.title: Agricultural Marketing Act
events.description: Hoover signs the Agricultural Marketing Act to revitalize the increasingly poor market for farm products. It represents a marked reversal in federal policy; Coolidge had vetoed a number of similar bills designed to aid farmers during his presidency. The act creates the Federal Farm Board, designed to promote the sale of agricultural products through cooperatives and stabilization corporations. In addition, it provides for the purchase of surplus goods by the federal government to maintain price levels, and a $500,000,000 fund to aid the cooperatives.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Agricultural Marketing Act

events.title: Stock index doubles
events.description: The index of common stock prices reaches an average of 216, more than double what it had been three years earlier. The increase represented the largest bull market the country had ever seen. At the same time, national income statistics indicate that roughly 60 percent of Americans have annual incomes below the poverty line, estimated at $2000.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Stock index doubles

events.title: Construction of Empire State Building begins
events.description: The construction contract for the Empire State Building is awarded. It will be completed in 1931.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Construction of Empire State Building begins

events.title: "Black Thursday"
events.description: On “Black Thursday,” the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) experiences a collapse in stock prices as 13 million shares are sold. Even wealthy investors J. Morgan and John D. Rockefeller, in an effort to save the market by furiously buying stock, cannot check the fall.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Black Thursday"

events.title: "Black Tuesday"
events.description: On “Black Tuesday,” a record 16.4 million shares of stock are traded on the NYSE as large blocks of equities are sold at extremely low prices. The trading continues the sharp downward trend of the previous week. It is an abrupt change from the over-speculation of the previous months. By December 1, NYSE stocks will have lost $26 billion in value.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Black Tuesday"

events.title: Stimson invokes Kellogg-Briand Pact
events.description: Secretary of State Henry Stimson invokes the Kellogg-Briand Pact, ratified earlier that year, in an effort to prevent a Sino-Soviet war. Five months earlier, Chinese Nationalist troops had seized the Chinese Eastern Railroad in Manchuria in an effort to end Soviet control of the railroad. While the Pact is ignored, neither country is prepared to fight a war and they reach a peaceful settlement three weeks later.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Stimson invokes Kellogg-Briand Pact

events.title: Census report
events.description: Census reports a population of nearly 123 million, illiteracy hitting a new low of 4.3 percent of the population--down 1.7 percent from 1920--with about one in five Americans owning an automobile.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Census report

events.title: Hughes named Chief Justice
events.description: Hoover names Charles Evans Hughes, former Secretary of State under Harding and Coolidge, Chief Justice of the Supreme Court. Hughes replaces former President William Howard Taft.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hughes named Chief Justice

events.title: Bootlegging bust
events.description: A major bootlegging operation in Chicago is shut down with the arrest of 158 people from 31 organizations. Together, these groups were estimated to have distributed more than seven million gallons of whiskey nationwide with an estimated worth of around $50 million.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bootlegging bust

events.title: London Naval Treaty signed
events.description: The London Naval Treaty is signed by the United States, Britain, and Japan. France and Italy refuse to sign major provisions of the treaty, which remains in effect until 1937.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: London Naval Treaty signed

events.title: Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act
events.description: Against the urgings of many economists, Hoover signs the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, raising duties prohibitively high on many imports. Rather than solve the economic crash, the act causes other countries to follow America's lead by raising their tariffs. Such “economic nationalism” exacerbates both the international depression and nationalist tensions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act

events.title: Veterans Administration Act
events.description: Hoover signs the Veterans Administration Act, establishing the Veterans Administration. The act consolidates all existing federal agencies handling benefits for former servicemen into a single department.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Veterans Administration Act

events.title: Bank panic begins
events.description: A bank panic begins as 305 banks across the country close before the month is out. More than 500 will follow in October.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bank panic begins

events.title: Construction of Hoover Dam begins
events.description: Construction of the Hoover Dam begins in Las Vegas, Nevada. The dam will be completed in 1936.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Yuko Hamaguchi assassinated
events.description: Japanese Premier, Yuko Hamaguchi, is assassinated by a military fanatic. Hamaguchi had supported the London Naval Treaty signed in April, and his death opens the government to the increasing influence of military groups. Eighteen months later, Hamaguchi's replacement, Ki Inukai, will also be assassinated, with these groups assuming full control of the government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Yuko Hamaguchi assassinated

events.title: Kellogg awarded the Nobel Peace Prize
events.description: Kellogg is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for his work in drafting the 1928 Kellogg-Briand Peace Pact while secretary of state during the Coolidge administration.
events.impact: Recognized contributions to world peace

events.title: Funding public works
events.description: Hoover asks Congress to fund for public works projects in order to stem the growing tide of unemployment. Congress complies weeks later, providing $116 million in jobs for the estimated 4.5 million unemployed.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Funding public works

events.title: Bank of the United States closes
events.description: The Bank of the United States in New York City, with 60 branches and 400,000 depositors, closes. It is merely the largest of the more than 1300 bank closings across the country as the economic depression worsens.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bank of the United States closes

events.title: Emergency Committee report
events.description: The President's Emergency Committee reports that the number of unemployed is nearly five million.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Emergency Committee report

events.title: Bonus Loan passed
events.description: Over Hoover's veto, Congress passes the Bonus Loan Bill. The act allows veterans to obtain cash loans of up to 50 percent of their bonus certificates issued in 1924.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bonus Loan passed

events.title: "Star Spangled Banner" becomes national anthem
events.description: The “Star Spangled Banner” officially becomes the national anthem.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Star Spangled Banner" becomes national anthem

events.title: "Scottsboro Boys" arrested
events.description: Nine black youths are arrested in Scottsboro, Alabama, and charged with raping a white woman. Although the group is initially found guilty on the basis of questionable evidence, the Supreme Court will overturn the conviction of the “Scottsboro Boys” in 1935.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Scottsboro Boys" arrested

events.title: Debt moratorium
events.description: In an effort to ease the worldwide depression, Hoover proposes a one-year moratorium on debt payments owed America in return for Europe returning the favor on U.S. Passed by Congress in December, the policy does little to ameliorate the economic crisis.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Debt moratorium

events.title: Mukden incident
events.description: The Japanese military stages an incident in the Manchurian town of Mukden, creating a pretext for the Japanese invasion of the region. The action is in direct violation of the Kellogg-Briand Pact signed in 1928.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Mukden incident

events.title: Britain dumps gold
events.description: Britain goes off the gold standard in an effort to solve the continuing economic crisis. Americans, fearing that the United States will soon do the same, begin to withdraw their money from banks and hoard gold. Over the next month, 827 more banks will close.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Britain dumps gold

events.title: Al Capone convicted
events.description: The notorious gangster Al Capone is convicted of income tax evasion and sentenced to 11 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. For most of the 1920s, Capone ruled the Chicago underworld, taking in $105 million in 1927 alone, primarily from the lucrative and illegal business of bootlegging. He would be released in 1939, mortally ill from syphilis.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Al Capone convicted

events.title: "Hunger marchers" turned away
events.description: In response to the Great Depression, hundreds of “hunger marchers” descend on the nation's capitol. They are turned away at the White House, however, unsuccessful in their attempt to present a petition to Hoover asking for jobs.
events.impact: Worst economic disaster in US history

events.title: Spain abolished monarchy
events.description: After more than a decade of military dictatorship, Spain adopts a Republican constitution, abolishing its monarchy. King Alfonso XIII had used the military to remain in power, but the international depression produced mutinies within the army and he had been forced to flee the country that April.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Spain abolished monarchy

events.title: "Stimson Doctrine" delivered
events.description: Secretary of State Stimson delivers a diplomatic note to Japan, condemning its actions in Manchuria. The “Stimson Doctrine” indicates America's refusal to recognize territory seized by force of arms.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: "Stimson Doctrine" delivered

events.title: Reconstruction Finance Corporation established
events.description: Hoover establishes the Reconstruction Finance Corporation, an agency designed to lend money to banks, insurance companies, and other institutions to stimulate the economy. It will have $2 billion at its disposal.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Reconstruction Finance Corporation established

events.title: Lindbergh baby kidnapped
events.description: In one of the most publicized crimes of the century, the twenty-month-old son of Charles Lindbergh is kidnapped. After paying a $50,000 ransom, the boy is found dead on May 12. The public outcry against the crime will help to make kidnapping a federal crime punishable by death.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Lindbergh baby kidnapped

events.title: Amelia Earhart completes her flight
events.description: Amelia Earhart becomes the first woman to complete a solo flight across the Atlantic Ocean.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Amelia Earhart completes her flight

events.title: Veterans arrive in Washington
events.description: The first of nearly 20,000 veterans arrive in Washington, D.C., setting up camp in open or unused buildings near the capitol. They hope to pressure Congress into granting them the full value of their bonus certificates, which were not to mature until 1944. The “Bonus Army” fails in its objective, however, as they are eventually forced out of the city by U.S. Army troops in late July.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Veterans arrive in Washington

events.title: Democrats nominate Franklin D. Roosevelt
events.description: In his acceptance speech for the Democratic presidential nomination, Franklin Delano Roosevelt pledges “a new deal for the American people,” marking his first public use of that term.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Franklin D. Roosevelt

events.title: Hoover Orders Bonus Army Dispersed
events.description: On July 28, 1932, President Herbert Hoover ordered the United States Army to remove a group of protesting veterans from federal buildings in Washington, D.C. The troops and the veterans clashed in a violent confrontation. The aggressive removal of the Bonus Army marchers damaged Hoover's popularity as he began a difficult reelection campaign. During his administration, Hoover had provided significant programs in support of veterans. He established the Veterans Administration, providing an agency focused on veterans' issues. He supported significant benefits for housing and hospitalization, as well as support for the disabled. Hoover, however, refused to increase relief to able-bodied Americans, including veterans, as the Great Depression began. Although Congress had passed a bill in 1924 to pay a bonus to World War I veterans, the bonus was not due to be paid out until 1945. In 1931, Congress passed a Bonus Loan bill, which allowed veterans to receive an advance on their bonus. [truncated]
events.impact: Worst economic disaster in US history

events.title: Franklin D. Roosevelt elected
events.description: Roosevelt wins the presidential election over Hoover in dramatic fashion. Roosevelt wins 472 electoral votes (22.8 million popular) to Hoover's 59 (15.8 million popular). The election illustrates the widespread public opinion that Hoover is largely to blame for the continuing economic crisis. By the end of the year, 13 million Americans are unemployed, total wages are 60 percent less than they were in 1929, and business losses are estimated to be as much as $6 billion.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Franklin D. Roosevelt elected

events.title: Roosevelt inaugurated
events.description: Roosevelt is inaugurated as the thirty-second President of the United States. He also appoints Francis Perkins as secretary of labor, making her the first woman hold a cabinet post.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt inaugurated

events.title: Roosevelt declares a four-day “bank holiday” in or…
events.description: Roosevelt declares a four-day “bank holiday” in order to stop the panic “run” on the nation's banks. He also summons Congress to a special session on March 9.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roosevelt declares a four-day “bank holiday” in or…

events.title: First lady Eleanor Roosevelt holds the first First…
events.description: First lady Eleanor Roosevelt holds the first First Lady press conference where only female reporters are invited to attend.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First lady Eleanor Roosevelt holds the first First…

events.title: Congress meets beginning what is later known as Ro…
events.description: Congress meets beginning what is later known as Roosevelt's “Hundred Days.” During this period, Congress enacts many of the principal programs of FDR's “New Deal.” It passes the Emergency Banking Act on March 9, allowing banks to reopen as soon as they can prove they are solvent; within three days, more than 1,000 banks will reopen, helping to raise the nation's confidence almost overnight.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress meets beginning what is later known as Ro…

events.title: FDR delivers his first “fireside chat” radio addre…
events.description: FDR delivers his first “fireside chat” radio address to the nation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: FDR delivers his first “fireside chat” radio addre…

events.title: Congress passes the Reforestation Relief Act, whic…
events.description: Congress passes the Reforestation Relief Act, which provides for the creation of the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC). The CCC offers immediate work to some 250,000 young men (ages 18-25) through a national reforestation program; by its conclusion in 1941, it will have employed more than 2 million young men.
events.impact: Protected natural resources

events.title: FDR, by presidential proclamation, takes the Unite…
