events.description: Vince Foster, deputy counsel to the President, is found dead in a Northern Virginia park. Authorities rule his death a suicide, but subsequent federal investigators will re-open the case in the future.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Vince Foster death

events.title: Ruth Bader Ginsburg appoint to Supreme Court
events.description: The Senate confirms Ruth Bader Ginsburg's nomination to the Supreme Court. Ginsburg succeeds the retiring Byron White and become the second woman to sit on the high court. The story of Ginsburg's nomination
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Ruth Bader Ginsburg appoint to Supreme Court

events.title: Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act
events.description: President Clinton signs the Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act. The legislation, which passes both houses of Congress by slim majorities, lays out a plan to reduce the budget deficit by $496 billion through 1998, using a combination of spending cuts and tax increases.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act

events.title: President Clinton presides over a ceremony in Wash…
events.description: President Clinton presides over a ceremony in Washington, D.C., at which Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin and Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) Chairman Yasser Arafat sign the Israeli-Palestinian Declaration of Principles, also known as the Oslo I Accord; this is the first face-to-face agreement between the Israeli government and the PLO, providing for Palestinian self-government in the Gaza Strip and the West Bank.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: President Clinton presides over a ceremony in Wash…

events.title: Clinton unveils healthcare plan
events.description: President Clinton unveils a plan for universal health care that would fix what he called a “badly broken” system. Clinton emphasizes that under his plan, all Americans would have high quality health care and would be able to choose their physicians.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Clinton unveils healthcare plan

events.title: Battle of Mogadishu
events.description: On October 3, 1993, U.S. special forces stormed a compound in Mogadishu, Somalia, in order to capture aides to warlord Mohammed Farah Aidid. Commanders intended the attack to be swift and precise, but the operation quickly fell apart. Hostile Somalis shot down two hovering U.S. combat helicopters. ground forces were assaulted as they tried to flee and were ambushed as they attempted to reach the crews of the downed helicopters. With exits to the city blocked, and an increasing number of killed and wounded, American soldiers were forced to hunker down and await reinforcements. In the end, eighteen U.S. troops died, and eighty-four were wounded. America was left with horrific images of soldiers' bodies being dragged through the streets and angry questions of how and why such a disaster occurred. presence in Somalia dated from December 1992. Then-President George H.W. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Battle of Mogadishu

events.title: American forces ambushed in Somalia
events.description: An elite American special forces unit searching for Somali warlord Mohammed Farah Aidid in Somalia's capital city of Mogadishu is ambushed by Aidid's forces, leaving eighteen Americans dead. Three days later, President Clinton announces that all American military personnel in Somalia will be home by March 31, 1994.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: American forces ambushed in Somalia

events.title: Brady Act
events.description: President Clinton signs the Brady Act, which requires a potential handgun purchaser to wait five days while a background check is performed by law enforcement officers.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Brady Act

events.title: NAFTA creates free trade zone
events.description: After a hard-fought battle in Congress, President Clinton signs the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), eliminating nearly every trade barrier between the United States, Canada, and Mexico, creating the world's largest free trade zone. Clinton Signs NAFTA On December 8, 1993, President Bill Clinton signed the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), which eliminated nearly every trade barrier between the United States, Canada, and Mexico, creating the world's largest free trade zone. The House of Representatives approved NAFTA on November 17, 1993, by a vote of 234 to 200. Remarkably the agreement's supporters included 132 Republicans and only 102 Democrats. That unusual combination reflected the challenges President Clinton faced in convincing Congress that the controversial piece of legislation would truly benefit all Americans. President George H.W. Bush was NAFTA's original sponsor, signing the deal on December 17, 1992. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Clinton attends NATO summit
events.description: President Clinton attends the NATO summit meeting in Brussels, Belgium, at which he announces that the United States will maintain at least 100,000 troops in Europe. He also introduces the “Partnership for Peace” program aimed at building closer ties between NATO and former Warsaw Pact states.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton attends NATO summit

events.title: Vietnam trade embargo lifted
events.description: President Clinton ends the nineteen-year old trade embargo against Vietnam, noting that Vietnam is indeed trying to locate 2,238 Americans listed as missing in action since the Vietnam War.
events.impact: Prolonged war with heavy casualties

events.title: Last Marines leave Somalia
events.description: The last American Marines leave Somalia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Last Marines leave Somalia

events.title: China trade status renewed
events.description: President Clinton renews China's Most Favored Nation trade status, even though China has not made as much progress on human rights issues as he had hoped.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: China trade status renewed

events.title: Clinton talks welfare reform
events.description: President Clinton unveils his welfare reform initiatives. Clinton had campaigned in 1992 on the issue, promising to “end welfare as we know it.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Clinton talks welfare reform

events.title: Israel-Jordan talks
events.description: President Clinton meets with Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin of Israel and King Hussein of Jordan. The talks result in Israel and Jordan agreeing in principle to end nearly fifty years of official antagonism.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Israel-Jordan talks

events.title: Congress delays heath care reform
events.description: The White House and congressional leaders, including Senate Majority Leader George Mitchell (D-ME), announce that Clinton's ambitious plan for health care reform will not be acted upon in 1994. Clinton's initiatives fail to find support in Congress.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Congress delays heath care reform

events.title: Clinton expands law enforcement, death penalty
events.description: President Clinton signs into law the Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act that includes provisions providing for the hiring of 100,000 more police and the expansion of the death penalty to cover more than 50 federal crimes. Title IV of this law is the Violence Against Women Act, which targets domestic violence, sexual violence, dating violence, and stalking. It works to prevent and respond to gender-based violence.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton expands law enforcement, death penalty

events.title: Haiti general cedes power
events.description: After a tense stand-off with the Clinton administration, Haiti's military government, led by General Raoul Cedras, agrees to cede power. The administration, along with the United Nations, had tried for over a year to restore the democratically elected president of Haiti, Jean-Bertrand Aristide, who had been overthrown in a coup on September 30, 1991.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Deterring invasion of Kuwait
events.description: The Clinton administration announces plans to send more than 35,000 troops to the Persian Gulf to deter an Iraqi invasion of Kuwait. Less than three days after the announcement, Iraqi troops pull back from the Iraq-Kuwait border.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Deterring invasion of Kuwait

events.title: Republicans gain seats
events.description: In mid-term congressional elections, the Republican Party wins control of both houses of Congress for the first time in more than 40 years. It now holds a 53 to 47 advantage in the Senate and a 230 to 214 to 1 lead in the House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans gain seats

events.title: General Agreement on Tariffs approved
events.description: The Senate votes to approve the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) that 117 nations, including the United States, agree to in December 1993. The agreement cuts tariffs by more than a third on a wide-range of products and creates a freer international market for goods.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: General Agreement on Tariffs approved

events.title: START I signed in Budapest
events.description: President Clinton, along with the presidents of Belarus, Kazakhstan, Russia, and Ukraine, signs the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty (START I) in Budapest, Hungary. The treaty eliminates more than 9,000 warheads.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: START I signed in Budapest

events.title: Congressional Accountability Act
events.description: President Clinton signs the Congressional Accountability Act, requiring Congress to abide by the same anti-discrimination workplace rules that apply throughout the rest of the country.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congressional Accountability Act

events.title: Emergency loans to Mexico
events.description: President Clinton authorizes the U.S. Treasury Department to make an emergency loan of up to $20 billion to Mexico to forestall a financial crisis threatening the interconnected Mexican and American economies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Emergency loans to Mexico

events.title: Oklahoma City bombed
events.description: In an act of domestic terrorism, a bomb planted in a truck parked in front of the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, kills 168 people and causes massive structural damage. In the days following the tragedy, Clinton, in widely-praised efforts, speaks with victims and to the country about how to recover physically, emotionally, and spiritually from the attack. Recollections of the Oklahoma City bombing
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Oklahoma City bombed

events.title: U.S. recognizes Vietnam
events.description: The United States extended full diplomatic recognition of Vietnam, twenty-two years after the United States withdrew military forces from that country.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. recognizes Vietnam

events.title: NATO begins strikes on Serbia
events.description: NATO, with a strong contingent of American forces, begins two weeks of air attacks on Serbian positions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: NATO begins strikes on Serbia

events.title: Improving U.S.-Russia relations
events.description: President Clinton and Russian president Yeltsin meet in Hyde Park, New York, and continue to discuss ways to improve relations between their two nations, especially with regard to the issue of nuclear arms.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Improving U.S.-Russia relations

events.title: Principle peace in Bosnia
events.description: In Dayton, Ohio, the representatives of Bosnia, Croatia, and Serbia agree in principle to a peace agreement, brokered by American Richard Holbrooke, to end three years of war in Bosnia. The agreement establishes a unitary Bosnian state and allows refugees to return home. Dayton Peace Accords Reached On November 21, 1995, the Dayton Peace Accords were initialed in Dayton, Ohio; they were formally signed in Paris, France, on December 14, 1995. The agreement was reached between the warring nations of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia, and Serbia. It sought to end one of the worst European conflicts since World War II, a four-year struggle of hardship and atrocities that had claimed the lives of more than 250,000 people, and made refugees of more than two million. President Bill Clinton's Assistant Secretary of State Richard Holbrooke led the negotiations and worked with the leaders of Bosnia, Croatia, and Serbia to reach acceptable terms. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Principle peace in Bosnia

events.title: Clinton urges peace in Ireland
events.description: During a tour of Europe, President Clinton urges the continuation of peace efforts in Northern Ireland where longstanding conflict between Irish Protestants and Catholics escalated to violence over issues of economic and political autonomy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton urges peace in Ireland

events.title: Battle of the budget
events.description: President Clinton and the Republican-controlled Congress, led by Speaker of the House Newt Gingrich (R-GA), engage in a political death struggle over how to balance the budget by 2002. Failure to reach an agreement leads to the shut-down of certain parts of the federal government, furloughing more than a quarter of a million government workers.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Battle of the budget

events.title: Clinton delivers State of the Union
events.description: President Clinton, in the annual State of the Union address, declares that “the era of big government is over.” More important, he positions himself as a centrist, moderate Democrat for the upcoming presidential election, hoping that these types of pronouncements will blunt Republican charges that he is too liberal.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton delivers State of the Union

events.title: Line-item veto approved
events.description: President Clinton signs a bill giving him the power of the “line-item veto,” which had been requested by Presidents Reagan and Bush. With this new power, Clinton can veto specific items in spending and tax bills without vetoing the entire measure.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Line-item veto approved

events.title: “Safe, legal and rare”
events.description: President Clinton vetoes a bill that would have outlawed certain types of late-term abortions, namely the partial birth abortion. Clinton emerges during his presidency as a strong advocate of the “right to choose,” often stating his wish that abortions in the United States become “safe, legal, and rare.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: “Safe, legal and rare”

events.title: Gore, DNC finance scrutiny
events.description: Vice President Al Gore attends a Democratic National Committee fundraising event at a Buddhist temple in Los Angeles. Gore and the DNC raise more than $60,000, but so through questionable interpretations of several campaign finance laws. The Clinton administration comes under increasing criticism in its second term for these alleged violations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Gore, DNC finance scrutiny

events.title: Troops to remain in Bosnia
events.description: President Clinton announces that American troops will likely remain in Bosnia as the major component of an international peacekeeping force for an additional eighteen months.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Troops to remain in Bosnia

events.title: Whitewater fraud trail
events.description: In the first trial to result from the Whitewater investigation, Jim and Susan McDougal, and Arkansas Governor Jim Guy Tucker--Clinton's friends and former business partners in the Whitewater affair--are convicted of fraud.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Whitewater fraud trail

events.title: Expanding health care coverage
events.description: President Clinton signs a health care reform bill that he expects to expand coverage for many Americans. The measure specifically allows workers who change or lose their jobs to keep their health insurance coverage.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Expanding health care coverage

events.title: Welfare restructured
events.description: President Clinton signs a welfare reform bill that radically restructures the American welfare system. The provisions of the new law limit recipients of welfare benefits and enact a “welfare to work” initiative. Clinton Signs “Welfare to Work” Bill On August 22, 1996, President Bill Clinton signed into law the Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Reconciliation Act, reflecting his campaign promise to “end welfare as we know it.” The act contained several provisions expressing the necessity of work, the primacy of states, and, ultimately, limited government provision. No longer entitled to cash assistance, families could only receive federal aid for a total of five years. States now would receive fixed block grants each year with substantial discretion over how to distribute them. The act also made many legal immigrants ineligible to receive public benefits and reduced spending on the Food Stamp Program and disability benefits for children. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Welfare restructured

events.title: Missile strikes Iraq
events.description: President Clinton orders a cruise missile strike against Iraq after Saddam Hussein leads a siege against the Kurdish city of Irbil in northern Iraq.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Missile strikes Iraq

events.title: UN bans nuclear weapons testing
events.description: An overwhelming majority of United Nations members, including the United States, agree to a treaty banning all nuclear weapons testing.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Clinton reelected
events.description: President Clinton, with 49 percent of the vote, defeats Senator Bob Dole (R-KS), with 41 percent of the vote, for the presidency. Clinton becomes the first Democratic President since Franklin Roosevelt to win reelection to a second term.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton reelected

events.title: Madeline Albright becomes Secretary of State
events.description: President Clinton selects Madeline Albright, the American ambassador to the United Nations, to serve as his Secretary of State. After winning Senate confirmation, Albright is sworn in on January 23, 1997, becoming the first women to hold the position.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Fundraising investigations begin
events.description: The Senate votes 99-0 to approve an investigation into the “improper” and “illegal” fund-raising tactics of both the White House and members of Congress. Allegations by Republicans and some Democrats of illegal fund raising by the Clinton White House spur the investigation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Fundraising investigations begin

events.title: Further nuclear negotiations begin
events.description: President Clinton and President Yeltsin of Russia meet at Helsinki, Finland, and agree to begin negotiations on another nuclear arms reduction treaty (START III) as soon as both nations ratify START II. The United States Senate had ratified START II in January 1996.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Further nuclear negotiations begin

events.title: Chemical Weapons become illegal
events.description: The Senate ratifies the Chemical Weapons Convention, making illegal the production, acquisition, stockpiling, or use of chemical weapons. Chemical Weapons Convention Ratified On April 25, 1997, the United States Senate ratified the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC), which bans the development, production, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons. It is one of the most ambitious arms agreements in history. The international treaty was originally signed at the United Nations in January 1993, and it went into effect on April 29, 1997. Since the use of mustard gas in the trenches of World War I, chemical weaponry has held great potential for military scientists and posed a terrifying threat to troops and ordinary citizens. The fall of the Soviet Union in the late 1980s and early 1990s led the United States to reduce nuclear stockpiles; but the deadly reality of chemical weapons took a back seat in the American public consciousness. [truncated]
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Eliminating the budget deficit
events.description: The Clinton administration and Republican congressional leaders agree on principle to a five-year budget plan to eliminate the budget deficit. That goal would be accomplished, largely due to the strong economy of recent years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Eliminating the budget deficit

events.title: Paula Jones lawsuit
events.description: In a decision affecting both the scope of presidential power and the immediate future of the Clinton presidency, the Supreme Court rules that Paula Jones can pursue her sexual harassment lawsuit against President Clinton, even while he is in office.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Paula Jones lawsuit

events.title: Balanced budget signed
events.description: President Clinton signs legislation providing for a balanced budget by 2002, ending years of partisan wrangling between Clinton and Republican leaders.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Balanced budget signed

events.title: No major campaign finance violations
events.description: Attorney General Janet Reno, in a letter to Congress, announces that the Justice Department's investigation into allegations that the Clinton administration violated campaign finance laws, especially in its efforts to finance the 1996 presidential campaign, has uncovered no major violations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: No major campaign finance violations

events.title: Zemin makes state visit
events.description: President Clinton welcomes President Jiang Zemin of China for a state visit.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Zemin makes state visit

events.title: Stabilizing Southeast Asia
events.description: President Clinton orders the United States government to contribute $3 billion to an international bail-out of Indonesia totaling over $22 billion. The Clinton administration argues that the bailout will help stabilize the shaky financial situation in Southeast Asia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Stabilizing Southeast Asia

events.title: Clinton denies Lewinsky allegations
events.description: News breaks that President Clinton may have had a sexual relationship with a former White House intern named Monica Lewinsky. Clinton, adamantly denying the allegations, states, “I did not have sexual relations with that woman, Miss Lewinsky.” Was Monica Lewinsky going to testify?
events.impact: Presidential scandal and impeachment

events.title: Clinton leaves for Ghana
events.description: President Clinton leaves on a six-country, 12-day tour of Africa, the first for an American President since 1978 and the longest, with special focus on highlighting the history of American Slavery.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton leaves for Ghana

events.title: Jones lawsuit dismissed
events.description: A judge dismisses Paula Jones's sexual harassment lawsuit against President Clinton.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jones lawsuit dismissed

events.title: Good Friday Peace Accords
events.description: Catholic and Protestant leaders in Northern Ireland sign the “Good Friday Peace Accords,” a substantial agreement in the Northern Ireland peace process. President Clinton had worked very hard, with several personal appeals to leaders on both sides, to bring about the agreement.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Good Friday Peace Accords

events.title: Embassies bombed in Kenya, Tanzania
events.description: Terrorists bomb American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, killing 224 people, including 20 Americans. United States intelligence believes that Osama bin Laden, a Saudi exile and alleged terrorist leader, is behind the attacks. On August 20, the U.S. military, on orders from President Clinton, launch reprisal strikes on “terrorist related facilities” in Afghanistan, bin Laden's country of residence, and Sudan. The attacks on Sudan, however, come under particular scrutiny, as a number of international observers and members of the Sudanese government contend that the United States destroyed a civilian pharmaceutical facility, and not a chemical weapons plant, as the Clinton administration reported. Embassies in Kenya and Tanzania Bombed The morning news on August 7, 1998, greeted Americans with a shocking report: a truck bomb had demolished the U.S. embassy in downtown Nairobi, Kenya. More than 200 people, twelve of them American citizens, had been killed. [truncated]
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Embassies bombed in Kenya, Tanzania

events.title: Report released on Lewinsky affair
events.description: The Office of the Independent Counsel releases its report on the Clinton-Lewinsky affair, commonly known as the Starr Report. Two days earlier, Independent Counsel Kenneth Starr tells the House that he has uncovered information that may be grounds for impeachment.
events.impact: Presidential scandal and impeachment

events.title: Wye River Memorandum
events.description: After nine days of negotiations in rural Maryland, Israeli leader Benjamin Netanyahu and Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat sign the Wye River Memorandum. President Clinton mediates the negotiations, which result in an agreement highlighted by a three-stage withdrawal of Israeli troops from the West Bank.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Wye River Memorandum

events.title: Retaliatory attacks on Iraq
events.description: President Clinton orders a three-day bombing attack against Iraq after Saddam Hussein refuses to cooperate with United Nations weapons inspectors.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: House votes to impeach Clinton
events.description: On December 19, 1998, the House of Representatives voted to impeach President Bill Clinton on charges of perjury and obstruction of justice. The House of Representatives, which is responsible for instigating impeachment proceedings, originally considered four articles of impeachment, but declined to charge the President with one count of perjury and abuse of power. That impeachment proceeded on two grounds rather than four did not testify to a bastion of GOP support in the House, for Republican Representatives had staunchly refused a Democratic plea simply to censure the President for reprehensible behavior. Clinton had wait through two agonizing months as the Senate received the case from the House, took testimony, and debated whether or not he could continue as President. The nation, too, was engaged in a furious debate over impeachment. Republicans and their conservative supporters felt that they were taking a stand for morality and integrity. [truncated]
events.impact: Undermining the legal system

events.title: Clinton delivers State of the Union
events.description: President Clinton delivers his State of the Union address to a joint session of Congress under remarkable circumstances: six days earlier, the Senate had convened an impeachment trial against the President. Despite the impeachment process, public opinion polls show Clinton with his highest approval ratings.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton delivers State of the Union

events.title: Clinton acquitted
events.description: The Senate acquits President Clinton on both articles of impeachment, rejecting one article and splitting evenly on the second.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Clinton acquitted

events.title: NATO attack on Serbia
events.description: In response to Serbian aggression in Kosovo and Albania, and reports of ethnic cleansing, the United States leads NATO attacks against Serbia. On February 23, Serbian and Kosovar representatives had agreed to a plan that would have granted more autonomy to Kosovo over a three-year period. Serbia reneged on the agreement, prompting U.S. and NATO military action.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: NATO attack on Serbia

events.title: NATO-Serbian campaign ends
events.description: The NATO air campaign against Serbia ends after Serb forces agree on June 9 to withdraw from Kosovo. KFOR, an international peacekeeping force of 50,000 troops, enforces the agreement.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: NATO-Serbian campaign ends

events.title: Underground nuclear tests upheld
events.description: The United States Senate votes down the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty, which would have prevented the United States from conducting underground nuclear tests.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Underground nuclear tests upheld

events.title: Trade with China
events.description: The United States and China agree to a trade treaty reducing tariffs and other trade barriers. The treaty is to come into effect after China joins the World Trade Organization and Congress grants permanent normal trade relations between the two countries.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Trade with China

events.title: Longest economic expansion in history
events.description: The Labor Department announces that the nation's business expansion has reached eight years and eleven months, marking the longest economic expansion in American history.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Longest economic expansion in history

events.title: Permanent trade relations with China
events.description: President Clinton sends a bill to Congress asking for permanent normal trade relations with China. After securing House (May 24) and Senate (September 19) approval, Clinton signs the bill on October 10.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Permanent trade relations with China

events.title: First summit with Putin
events.description: President Clinton holds his first summit meeting with Russian president Vladimir Putin. They reaffirm their nations' commitment to strategic arms reductions, but disagree over American plans to research and develop a missile-defense system.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First summit with Putin

events.title: Israeli peace summit
events.description: President Clinton hosts Israeli leader Ehud Barak and Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat at Camp David in the hope of reaching a peace agreement. After two weeks of unsuccessful talks, the summit breaks up with no agreement.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Israeli peace summit

events.title: Democrats nominate Gore
events.description: President Clinton speaks at the opening day of the Democratic National Convention. Vice President Al Gore wins the Democratic nomination for President. His challenger is Republican governor George W. Bush of Texas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Gore

events.title: Independent Counsel concludes
events.description: Independent Counsel Robert Ray announces that his investigation has not discovered enough evidence to indict the Clintons for their Whitewater dealings.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Independent Counsel concludes

events.title: Disputed Serbian elections
events.description: In Serbia, President Slobodan Milosevic declares that Vojislav Kostunica is the rightful president of Serbia. The announcement comes after disputed elections, which Milosevic had tried to rig, produce massive street protests.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Disputed Serbian elections

events.title: A contested election
events.description: On election day, Vice President Gore and Governor Bush run so closely that no winner can be declared. Only after the Supreme Court rules on December 13 that there would be no recount of Florida's contested votes does Gore concede the election to Bush.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: A contested election

events.title: George W. Bush inaugurated
events.description: Texas governor George W. Bush is inaugurated as the forty-third President of the United States.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: George W. Bush inaugurated

events.title: Contested election
events.description: Americans vote in the 2000 presidential election. Vote differentials in several states are exceedingly close, with the Democratic and Republican candidates disputing many of those counts, leaving the final result inconclusive.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Contested election

events.title: Supreme Court halts recount
events.description: In a 5-4 ruling, the U.S. Supreme Court stops the recount of votes in several contested Florida counties. The Democratic candidate, Vice President Albert Gore Jr., concedes the election, leaving Governor George W. Bush of Texas, the Republican candidate, as President-elect.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Supreme Court halts recount

events.title: George W. Bush inaugurated
events.description: Bush is inaugurated as the 43rd President of the United States. He is the second son of a President to occupy the Oval Office, the first being John Quincy Adams in 1825.
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Bush bans abortion aid
events.description: In one of his first policy decisions, President Bush decides to reinstate the ban on aid to international groups performing or counseling on abortion. The ban was initiated by former President Ronald Reagan but is not enforced during the administration of President Bill Clinton.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush bans abortion aid

events.title: Deregulating religious charities
events.description: By executive order, President Bush creates the Office of Faith-Based and Community Initiatives. The new office will work to ease regulations on religious charities and promote grass-roots efforts to tackle community issues such as aid to the poor and disadvantaged.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Deregulating religious charities

events.title: Attacking Iraq
events.description: United States airplanes attack Iraqi radar sites to enforce a “no-fly zone.” Bush calls the military action a “routine mission.”
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: Kyoto Protocol rejected
events.description: The Bush administration affirms its decision to abandon ratification of the Kyoto Protocol, an international treaty signed by 180 countries to reduce global warming that set limits on industrial emissions.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Kyoto Protocol rejected

events.title: Spy plane downed over South China Sea
events.description: spy plane flying over the South China Sea is clipped by a Chinese fighter jet, forcing the American plane to make an emergency landing on Chinese soil. The incident strains diplomatic ties between the two nations as the United States demands that China return both the plane and its crew to American authorities.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Spy plane downed over South China Sea

events.title: 04/04/2001: Journalists review recount
events.description: The Miami Herald and USA Today release a comprehensive review of the 2000 presidential election recount efforts in Florida. The review shows that even if Democratic candidate Al Gore had succeeded in getting the recounts he wanted, President Bush would have won Florida by 1,665 votes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: 04/04/2001: Journalists review recount

events.title: Bush backs Taiwan over China
events.description: President Bush signals a change in relations with China by officially pledging military support for Taiwan in the event of an attack by China. This is the first time a presidential administration has publicly acknowledged a position that had previously been implicitly accepted.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush backs Taiwan over China

events.title: Trillion dollar tax cut
events.description: President Bush signs a $1.35 trillion tax cut into law. Although the amount falls short of the $1.60 trillion the administration has been seeking, the bill does slash income tax rates across the board and provides for the gradual elimination of the estate tax.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Trillion dollar tax cut

events.title: Halting stem cell research
events.description: President Bush addresses the nation, outlining his plans for the federal funding of stem cell research. The new policy allows for continued government funding of existing research on already extracted stem cells but prohibits the extraction of additional stem cells from human embryos for further research.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Halting stem cell research

events.title: September 11 Terrorist Attacks
events.description: On September 11, 2001, the United States endured a deadly attack when terrorists hijacked four commercial planes and intentionally crashed them. The hijackers flew the first three planes into important targets: both towers of the World Trade Center in New York City and the Pentagon building in Washington, D.C. The fourth plane, which some speculated was headed for the White House, crashed in Somerset County, Pennsylvania. Passengers aboard the plane, talking on their cell phones, learned what the hijackers intended to do and with astonishing heroism tried to overtake the hijackers, saving the fourth target and sacrificing their lives. Police and fire department personnel in New York City rushed into the twin towers of the World Trade Center after the planes crashed into them to begin rescuing victims from the burning towers. [truncated]
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: September 11 Terrorist Attacks

events.title: Targeting bin Laden; Tom Ridge appointed to Homeland Security
events.description: President Bush appears before a joint session of Congress to outline the administration’s plans to defeat world terrorism, singling out Osama bin Laden and his Al-Qaeda organization as the primary targets of such a policy. He states that every nation must take sides in the international conflict against worldwide terrorist networks; he also warns Americans to prepare for a protracted campaign against terrorism. The President then appoints Pennsylvania governor Tom Ridge to the new cabinet-level post of director of the Office of Homeland Security. Governor Ridge will coordinate the efforts of more than forty federal agencies to secure the United States against future terrorist attacks.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Targeting bin Laden; Tom Ridge appointed to Homeland Security

events.title: Operation “Enduring Freedom”
events.description: Speaking from the Treaty Room of the White House, President Bush announces the commencement of military action in Afghanistan, an operation code-named “Enduring Freedom.” Operation Enduring Freedom Begins On October 7, 2001, President George W. Bush announced that the United States had begun military action in Afghanistan. The military operation was code-named Enduring Freedom. “On my order, U.S. forces have begun strikes on terrorist camps of Al Qaeda and the military installations of the Taliban regime in Afghanistan,” Bush said in a somber, televised address from the White House Treaty Room. The air assaults, he said, were joined by Britain, with assorted intelligence efforts and logistical support coming from several other nations, including France, Germany, Australia, and Canada. [truncated]
events.impact: Protected rights of people with disabilities

events.title: D.C. Anthrax scare
events.description: The Capital shuts down amidst an Anthrax scare. Persons in Florida and New York have already tested positive for the frequently fatal bacteria. Bush calls for $1.5 billion to fight bioterrorism.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: D.C. Anthrax scare

events.title: Enron files bankruptcy
events.description: The Enron Corporation files for Chapter Eleven bankruptcy protection, the largest bankruptcy case in American history. The beleaguered company, once the world’s premier energy trading and services firm, files for court protection after watching its stock price plummet as a result of accounting issues relating to its operations. Earlier in the year, discoveries reveal that Enron’s chief financial officer engaged in partnerships which allowed the company to hide half a billion dollars worth of debt. The Bush administration has ties to key Enron executives, including CEO Kenneth Lay, but denies any involvement in the scandal.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Enron files bankruptcy

events.title: Withdrawal from Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty
events.description: After conferring with the National Security Council, President Bush notifies Russia of his intention to withdraw from the 1972 Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty. Meetings with Russian president Vladimir Putin fail to establish an agreement between the two nations. In June 2002, the United States officially withdraws from the Treaty, allowing it to conduct anti-missile defense tests.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Withdrawal from Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty

events.title: No Child Left Behind
events.description: President Bush signs an education reform bill with bipartisan support; this reauthorized the Johnson-era Elementary and Secondary Education Act of 1965. Known as the No Child Left Behind Act, it offers local authorities greater flexibility in spending federal dollars, but requires standardized math and reading tests.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: No Child Left Behind

events.title: Bush delivers State of the Union
events.description: In his State of the Union address, President Bush warns that the war against terrorism is only beginning. Specifically citing North Korea, Iran, and Iraq, Bush speaks of “an axis of evil” threatening world peace.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush delivers State of the Union

events.title: Israeli-Palestine relations
events.description: Bush renews his call on Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat to end attacks on Israel, indicating that a meeting with Vice President Dick Cheney could still take place if Arafat yielded to American demands. Cheney, on a trip to the Middle East, refuses to meet with Arafat in the current environment. Meanwhile, Arafat remains penned in Ramallah by Israeli threats since December. On March 29, Israeli troops will take over most of his headquarters.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Israeli-Palestine relations

events.title: Colin Powell travels to Middle East
events.description: Secretary of State Colin Powell travels to the Middle East from April 11 to April 17, 2002, for talks with Jordon, Israel, Syria, Egypt, and Palestinian leader Yasser Arafat.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Colin Powell travels to Middle East

events.title: Jimmy Carter travels to Cuba, visits Castro
events.description: Former President Jimmy Carter travels to Cuba for a tour and visit with Cuban President Fidel Castro. His arrival marks the first trip by an American President in forty years. On May 20, President Bush announces that the forty-year-old trade embargo against Cuba will continue until conditions, including free and fair elections, are met.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Jimmy Carter travels to Cuba, visits Castro

events.title: Searching for signs of 9/11
events.description: Congress presses the Bush administration for further information about warnings of the September 11, 2001, attacks. National Security Advisor Condoleezza Rice holds a briefing, maintaining, “I don’t think that anybody could have predicted that these people would take an airplane and slam it into the World Trade Center, take another one and slam it into the Pentagon.” She insists that there was no lapse in intelligence.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Searching for signs of 9/11

events.title: Bush-Putin nuclear treaty
events.description: At the Kremlin, President Bush and Russian president Vladimir Putin sign a nuclear arms treaty, vowing to reduce their nations’ arsenals by two-thirds over the next ten years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush-Putin nuclear treaty

events.title: Bush expands Homeland Security
events.description: In a televised address to the nation , President Bush announces broad changes to security departments in charge of protecting the nation from terrorism. The Office of Homeland Security will now coordinate a wide range of functions and oversee more than 100 organizations. The announcement follows criticism of the FBI and CIA for failing to prevent the September 11 attacks.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush expands Homeland Security

events.title: Bush calls for new Palestinian leadership
events.description: President Bush calls for the Palestinian people to replace Yasser Arafat, head of the Palestinian Authority and leader of the Palestinian cause for more than thirty years. Bush states that “when the Palestinians have new leaders, institutions and security arrangements, the U.S. will support the creation of a Palestinian state.”
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush calls for new Palestinian leadership

events.title: Curbing corporate abuses
events.description: Following the Enron and WorldCom scandals, in which both companies claimed profits which turned out to be highly inflated, President Bush calls for new laws on corporate abuse. On July 10, the Dow Jones index drops below 9,000, its largest one-day loss since September 2001.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Curbing corporate abuses

events.title: Action against Iraq
events.description: Seeking support for action against Iraq, President Bush addresses Congress, identifying Iraqi strongman Saddam Hussein as “a serious threat.” Bush mentions the concept of a regime change and announces the visit of British Prime Minister Tony Blair in the days to come. House Majority Whip Tom DeLay (R-TX) calls action in Iraq “inevitable.”
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Action against Iraq

events.title: Bush addresses UN Security Council
events.description: President Bush addresses the United Nations’ Security Council, making his case for military action to enforce UN resolutions in Iraq. Additionally, he warns that the United States will move alone if the Council does not act. In the coming days, Bush and Secretary of State Colin Powell will continue to press the Security Council for a resolution against Iraq. France, Germany, and Russia, all permanent members of the Security Council, express severe reservations.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Congress authorizes force against Iraq
events.description: A bipartisan Senate vote of 77-23 gives authorization to Bush to use force against Iraq. The Senate vote follows a similar vote of 296-133 in the House in support of the bill.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress authorizes force against Iraq

events.title: Republicans gain seats
events.description: In a sweeping mid-term election victory, Republicans gain control of the Senate and maintain their edge in the House.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans gain seats

events.title: Arms inspections in Iraq
events.description: Following a United Nations report issued by arms inspectors indicating that Iraq remained in violation of Security Council Resolution 1441, Bush speaks out again against Iraq. Inspections in Iraq continue.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Further tax cuts
events.description: Bush reveals a tax-cut plan of $674 billion over ten years. He suggests that the plan will stimulate the U.S. economy, end the recession, and create jobs. Democrats dismiss the plan as financially irresponsible and favorable to the rich.
events.impact: Economic downturn causing job losses

events.title: Columbia shuttle explosion
events.description: The seven-member crew of the shuttle Columbia dies in an explosion in space. Debris falls in Texas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Columbia shuttle explosion

events.title: North Korea’s nuclear capabilities
events.description: CIA director George Tenet announces that North Korea possesses a nuclear ballistic missile capable of hitting the United States. In the following weeks, reports emerge which suggest that North Korea will soon possess the ability to create a nuclear arsenal.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: North Korea’s nuclear capabilities

events.title: Taking military action in Iraq
events.description: After months of debate in the United Nations Security Council, President Bush announces the U.S. intention to move against Iraq with its coalition of allies. Bush issues an ultimatum for military action, giving Iraqi leader Saddam Hussein and his sons forty-eight hours to leave Iraq.
events.impact: Created framework for international cooperation

events.title: Bush declares war with Iraq
events.description: The 8:00 p.m. deadline for Hussein to leave Iraq passes. At 10:15 p.m., Bush addresses the nation and informs the American people that the United States is at war with Iraq.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush declares war with Iraq

events.title: Tax cuts reduced to fund war
events.description: Citing costs of the Iraq War, the Senate approves the reduction of Bush’s tax cut plan to $350 billion, less than half of the original amount.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Tax cuts reduced to fund war

events.title: Bush, Blair appeal to Iraqi people
events.description: President Bush and British Prime Minister Tony Blair air a joint address on Iraqi television that describes the goals of coalition forces and reassures the Iraqi people that they will be able to live their lives in peace and security in a post-Saddam era.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Bush, Blair appeal to Iraqi people

events.title: Bush claims “Mission Accomplished”
events.description: In a nationally televised address aboard the aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln, Bush stands in front of a “Mission Accomplished” banner and declares that major combat operations in Iraq are over. He links the Iraq War to the War on Terror and vows to continue searching for banned weapons in Iraq.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Bush claims “Mission Accomplished”

events.title: Iraq sanctions lifted
events.description: The UN Security Council votes to lift sanctions on Iraq imposed since the 1991 Gulf War. The resolution gives the United States and United Kingdom control of Iraq until it establishes a legitimate government and authority to use Iraqi oil revenues for humanitarian aid and reconstruction.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Iraq sanctions lifted

events.title: Bush signs third largest tax cut in history
events.description: Bush signs into law his $350 billion tax-cut package, the third-largest in history, in an effort to strengthen the U.S. economy and reverse a trend of increasing unemployment. Congressional Democrats who opposed the bill argued it is skewed towards the wealthy.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Bush signs third largest tax cut in history

events.title: Bush, Sharon, Abbas meet
events.description: Bush meets with Israeli Prime Minister Ariel Sharon and Palestinian Prime Minister Mahmoud Abbas in Aqaba, Jordan, to discuss implementation of a “road-map” for peace.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush, Sharon, Abbas meet

events.title: Federal law enforcement and race
events.description: President Bush issues comprehensive guidelines forbidding federal law enforcement agencies from considering race or ethnicity in routine patrol duties. Although more extensive than previous federal law, the guidelines provided clear exceptions for matters of national security and counterterrorism operations.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal law enforcement and race

events.title: Assumed Iraqi nuclear potential discredited
events.description: CIA Director George Tenet accepts full responsibility for the statement in Bush’s 2003 State of the Union Address regarding Iraq’s alleged effort to obtain uranium from Africa, saying its inclusion should not have been approved by the CIA because the intelligence was unsubstantiated and the claim had been discredited.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Assumed Iraqi nuclear potential discredited

events.title: U.S. forces kill Hussein’s sons
events.description: forces kill Saddam Hussein’s two sons Uday and Qusay in Mosul, Iraq. Officials hope that anti-U.S. attacks in Iraq will decrease as a result. Saddam Hussein’s whereabouts are unknown.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. forces kill Hussein’s sons

events.title: CCI releases 9/11 findings
events.description: The joint Congressional Committee on Intelligence releases an 800-page document on the findings of its inquiry into intelligence failures leading up to the terrorist attacks of September 11, which concludes that intelligence agencies failed to respond to alerts about potential targets and methods. The report faults the NSA, CIA, and the FBI for a breakdown in communications and advocates the creation of a cabinet-level “intelligence czar” to remove obstacles between agencies.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: CCI releases 9/11 findings

events.title: Justice Department probes operative leaks
events.description: The Justice Department announces a full criminal investigation into allegations that Bush administration officials had leaked the name of a covert CIA operative to the media in July. Bush urges full cooperation with the probe.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Justice Department probes operative leaks

events.title: Weapons Inspector finds no nuclear weapons in Iraq
events.description: Weapons Inspector David Kay reports that his 1,400 member team, the Iraq Survey Group, failed to find any biological, chemical, or nuclear weapons in Iraq. Kay acknowledged that they did find evidence that Iraq sought the capacity to create those weapons in the future. Bush used these findings as validation of his prewar claims that Iraq posed a significant security threat to the United States.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Weapons Inspector finds no nuclear weapons in Iraq

events.title: Bush bans late term abortions
events.description: Bush signs into law a ban on late-term abortion, the first law to ban an abortion procedure since the Supreme Court’s 1973 decision in Roe v. The Supreme Court later upholds the ban.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush bans late term abortions

events.title: Medicare overhaul
events.description: Bush signs a landmark bill overhauling Medicare that includes the program’s first prescription drug benefits to begin in 2006 and creates incentives for private insurance companies to cover Medicare subscribers.
events.impact: Provided healthcare for elderly Americans

events.title: Iran agrees to weapons inspections
events.description: Iran signs an agreement to grant unrestricted access to UN-IAEA weapons inspectors.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Iran agrees to weapons inspections

events.title: Bush delivers fourth State of the Union
events.description: Bush gives his fourth State of the Union Address, laying out a broad domestic and foreign policy agenda while stressing issues of national security.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush delivers fourth State of the Union

events.title: Iraq creates interim constitution
events.description: The Iraqi Governing Council signs an interim constitution to provide a framework for establishment of a transitional government.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Iraq creates interim constitution

events.title: Shiite, Sunni confrontations
events.description: forces in Iraq confront a violent uprising beginning with Shiite Muslims in Baghdad and spreading to opposing Sunni Muslims in Fallujah, leading to the heaviest fighting since the invasion began in March of 2003.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Shiite, Sunni confrontations

events.title: U.S. abuse of Iraqi prisoners
events.description: CBS broadcasts photographs of U.S. Army abuse of Iraqi prisoners in Abu Ghraib prison, a facility on the outskirts of Baghdad. Bush and other senior administration officials voice deep disapproval over these abuses.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: U.S. abuse of Iraqi prisoners

events.title: Massachusetts approves same-sex marriage
events.description: Massachusetts becomes the first state to offer marriage licenses to same-sex couples. Bush reiterates to Congress his call for a constitutional amendment banning same sex marriage.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Massachusetts approves same-sex marriage

events.title: CIA Director Tenet resigns
events.description: Bush announces he has accepted the resignation of CIA Director George Tenet, widely blamed for intelligence failures in the months leading up to September 11.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: CIA Director Tenet resigns

events.title: Circumventing bans on torture
events.description: Attorney General John Ashcroft appears before the Senate Judiciary Committee to answer questions regarding two leaked government memoranda that contained legal arguments for circumventing U.S. and international bans on torture, specifically for the questioning of terrorist suspects.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Circumventing bans on torture

events.title: Formal occupation of Iraq ends
events.description: The U.S.-led Coalition for Provisional Authority formerly ends foreign occupation of Iraq, granting the provisional government sovereignty. Still, 130,000 troops remain in Iraq.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Formal occupation of Iraq ends

events.title: Republicans nominate Bush
events.description: President Bush and Vice President Cheney are renominated as the Republican candidates at the GOP convention in New York City.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Republicans nominate Bush

events.title: First Bush-Kerry debate
events.description: Bush and Democratic nominee, Senator John Kerry, have their first of three presidential debates, this one focused on national security issues and foreign policy. The Bush administration’s handling of the Iraq War is the main focus.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: First Bush-Kerry debate

events.title: Second Bush-Kerry debate
events.description: Bush and Kerry have their second debate in a town-hall format in St. Louis, Missouri.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Second Bush-Kerry debate

events.title: Bush-Kerry in dead heat
events.description: Polls show the two candidates in a dead heat as Bush and Kerry meet for their third and final presidential debate on domestic policy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush-Kerry in dead heat

events.title: bin-Laden addresses America
events.description: Qatar-based television channel Al-Jazeera airs excerpts from a videotape of Osama bin-Laden, leader of the terrorist network al-Qaeda, who addresses the American people; he accepts responsibility for and justifies the September 11th attacks while condemning Bush’s response to them. Many view this tape as an attempt by al-Qaeda to influence the U.S. presidential election.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: bin-Laden addresses America

events.title: Bush reelected
events.description: Bush wins a second term with a close 51 percent of the popular vote and 274 electoral votes to John Kerry’s 252. The Republican Party builds slightly on its majority in the House and Senate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush reelected

events.title: Retaking Fallujah
events.description: troops launch an assault to retake the city of Fallujah, Iraq, in the largest military operation since the initial invasion in March of 2003.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Retaking Fallujah

events.title: Powell Resigns, Rice appointed
events.description: Retired Army general and Secretary of State Colin Powell resigns. Bush appoints former National Security Advisor Condoleezza Rice to the position.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Powell Resigns, Rice appointed

events.title: Bush inaugurated
events.description: President Bush is sworn in for the second term of his presidency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush inaugurated

events.title: Bush delivers State of the Union
events.description: In his State of the Union, President Bush calls for an historic restructuring of Social Security, allowing workers to use their payroll taxes to invest in the stock market. However, he is unable to move the policy through Congress.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush delivers State of the Union

events.title: Appointing new “Intelligence Czar”
events.description: Bush names former U.S. Ambassador to Iraq John Negroponte Director of National Intelligence, a newly created position of “intelligence czar” created in the wake of Congressional investigations into intelligence failures leading up to September 11.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Appointing new “Intelligence Czar”

events.title: Bush takes diplomatic trip to Europe
events.description: Bush travels to Europe to meet with French President Jacques Chirac, German Chancellor Gerhard Schroeder, and Russian President Vladimir Putin to smooth diplomatic relations after the U.S.-led invasion of Iraq.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush takes diplomatic trip to Europe

events.title: Schiavo medical case
events.description: Terri Schiavo, a Florida woman long-suffering from brain damage, dies following the removal of her feeding tube. Schiavo had been the focus of legal controversy between the wishes of her parents and those of her husband. Ten days earlier, President Bush had signed a law permitting Schiavo’s parents to challenge the removal of her feeding tube in federal court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Schiavo medical case

events.title: Hussein to be tried for war crimes
events.description: Iraqi government announces that a war crimes trial for Saddam Hussein is likely to begin within the next two months and prosecutors would seek the death penalty.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hussein to be tried for war crimes

events.title: Persuading North Korea to end nuclear pursuit
events.description: South Korean President Roh Moo Hyun meets with Bush to discuss efforts to persuade North Korea to join the six-party talks intended to end North Korea’s pursuit of nuclear weapons.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Persuading North Korea to end nuclear pursuit

events.title: Human rights in Vietnam
events.description: Vietnamese Premier Phan Van Khai meets with Bush to discuss human rights and treatment of ethnic and religious minorities in Vietnam. This marks the first visit by a Vietnamese Premier to the United States since the country reunited under Communist rule in 1975.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Human rights in Vietnam

events.title: Senate passes energy bill
events.description: The Senate passes an omnibus energy bill aimed at supporting the traditional energy industries of oil and natural gas, but also provide tax incentives for the use of alternative energy sources such as solar and wind power.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Senate passes energy bill

events.title: Discovery launches
events.description: The space shuttle Discovery takes off from the Kennedy Space Center on a mission to deliver repairs to the International Space Station. This is the first U.S. space mission since the failed return of the Columbia in 2003.
events.impact: Moderately positive impact: Discovery launches

events.title: Hurricane Katrina strikes
events.description: Hurricane Katrina strikes the Gulf coast of the United States with devastating effects. The storm breaches the levee system in New Orleans, causing massive flooding and destruction of property. The Bush administration is harshly criticized for an inadequate response by the federal government to the storm’s destruction.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Hurricane Katrina strikes

events.title: Roberts appointed Chief Justice
events.description: Roberts is confirmed as Chief Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court. Roberts replaces William Rehnquist, who died in office, and is President Bush’s first nominee to the Court.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Roberts appointed Chief Justice

events.title: Alito appointed to Supreme Court
events.description: The Senate Judiciary Committee approves President Bush’s nomination of Samuel A. Alito, Jr., to the U.S. Supreme Court by a vote of 10-8. Previously, concerns were voiced over the disruption of the court’s ideological balance that would result from conservative Alito’s replacement of moderate Sandra Day O’Connor.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Alito appointed to Supreme Court

events.title: Remaining in Iraq
events.description: In a White House news conference, President Bush admits for the first time that the complete removal of U.S. troops from Iraq during the remainder of his term is improbable. He continues to assert the fact that progress is being made in the establishment of Iraqi democracy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Remaining in Iraq

events.title: Bird flu scare
events.description: After several cases of avian influenza are reported in Central and Southeast Asia, the Bush administration proposes a plan to minimize losses in the case of a deadly pandemic. The plan includes coordination with the World Health Organization, reorganization of international travel, and the authorization of military assistance in the case of public unrest.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bird flu scare

events.title: Moussaoui convicted
events.description: District Court in Alexandria, Virginia, sentences Zacarias Moussaoui to life in prison without parole for his role in the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks. Moussaoui was the first person to stand trial for the attacks.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Moussaoui convicted

events.title: Senate halts debate on same-sex marriage ban
events.description: The Senate votes 49-48 to conclude debate on a constitutional amendment banning same sex marriages in the United States, thereby preventing a vote on the actual passage of the amendment. President Bush had previously expressed support of the proposed amendment.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Senate halts debate on same-sex marriage ban

events.title: Bush vetoes stem cell research
events.description: President Bush vetoes a bill to lift constraints on federal funding of embryonic stem cell research, and subsequently, the House unsuccessfully attempts to override the veto. This is the first veto Bush issues during his administration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush vetoes stem cell research

events.title: Bush approves border fence
events.description: President Bush signs a bill providing for the construction of a 700-mile fence along the United States-Mexico border, in an effort to increase border security and stem illegal immigration.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush approves border fence

events.title: Democrats gain seats
events.description: Democrats recapture control of the U.S. House and Senate in the midterm elections.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats gain seats

events.title: Saddam Hussein hanged
events.description: Former Iraqi President Saddam Hussein is hanged in Baghdad, Iraq, after being convicted of crimes against humanity dating back to 1982.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Saddam Hussein hanged

events.title: Pelosi becomes first woman Speaker
events.description: Nancy Pelosi, a Democrat from California, takes office as the first woman Speaker of the House. Democrats assume control of both the House of Representatives and the Senate.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Pelosi becomes first woman Speaker

events.title: U.S. attacks al-Qaeda in Somalia
events.description: Air Force launches an air attack on extremist militias and suspected al-Qaeda operatives in Somalia.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. attacks al-Qaeda in Somalia

events.title: Increasing troops in Iraq
events.description: Bush announces what would be termed a “troop surge” in Iraq in an attempt to increase security in the capital of Baghdad and smother insurgency centers throughout the country.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Increasing troops in Iraq

events.title: General David Petraeus takes over
events.description: General David Petraeus takes over command of the multinational forces in Iraq to oversee the surge.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: General David Petraeus takes over

events.title: Scooter Libby convicted
events.description: Scooter Libby, Vice President Cheney’s Chief of Staff, is convicted of perjury and obstruction of justice in the case of CIA operative, Valerie Plame Wilson, whose covert identity was exposed. Bush later commutes Libby’s sentence.
events.impact: Undermining the legal system

events.title: Virginia Tech massacre
events.description: Seung-Hui Cho kills himself and 32 fellow students at Virginia Tech in the deadliest campus gun rampage in U.S. President Bush and the First Lady attend the memorial.
events.impact: Mass killing of people

events.title: Bush vetoes troop removal
events.description: Bush vetoes a war spending bill passed by Congress, which set a timetable for troop withdrawal from Iraq. Within days Bush reaches a record low approval rating.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Bush vetoes troop removal

events.title: Supreme Court to hear Guantanamo appeals
events.description: The Supreme Court reverses an April decision and agrees to hear appeals from Guantanamo Bay detainees who have not had access to the federal courts.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Supreme Court to hear Guantanamo appeals

events.title: Antiterrorism Bill
events.description: Congress passes the Antiterrorism Bill, which will allow for the screening of air and sea cargo and will give more money in government antiterrorism grants to states with the greatest risk for terrorist attacks.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Antiterrorism Bill

events.title: Gonzalez resigns, Mukasey appointed AG
events.description: President Bush names Michael Mukasey as Attorney General after Alberto Gonzalez announces his resignation.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Gonzalez resigns, Mukasey appointed AG

events.title: Dow Jones all-time high
events.description: The Dow Jones industrial average closes at 14,164, its all-time high. Soon after, it begins a steep decline.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Dow Jones all-time high

events.title: Middle East Peace Conference
events.description: President Bush hosts a Middle East Peace Conference in Annapolis, Maryland, with Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel and Palestinian president Mahmoud Abbas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Middle East Peace Conference

events.title: Increasing automobile fuel efficiency
events.description: Congress passes new energy legislation to increase automobile fuel efficiency standards and mandates increases in biofuel production. The bill passes the House and Senate, and President Bush signs it into law.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Increasing automobile fuel efficiency

events.title: Bush proposes stimulus package
events.description: President Bush proposes a $145 billion stimulus package in response to a housing crisis and rapidly increasing oil prices. The package gives individuals several hundred dollars to facilitate spending, as well as rebates for families with children and tax deductions for businesses in order to jump-start the slowing economy.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush proposes stimulus package

events.title: U.S. strike kills al-Libi
events.description: missile strikes in Pakistan kill a top al-Qaeda leader, Abu Laith al-Libi, who trained terror operatives in the region.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: U.S. strike kills al-Libi

events.title: Labor Statistics reports drastic job loss
events.description: The Bureau of Labor Statistics reports that the U.S. economy lost more than 15,000 jobs during the previous month. Such an elimination of jobs from the economy had not occurred for more than four years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Labor Statistics reports drastic job loss

events.title: Senate passes stimulus package
events.description: The Senate passes a $170-billion stimulus package to give many Americans tax rebates as large as $600 or more, and to implement tax breaks for certain businesses in an effort to head-off impending economic slowdown.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Senate passes stimulus package

events.title: Six detainees charged for September 11
events.description: Six detainees at Guantanamo Bay who were thought to have had roles in orchestrating the September 11 terrorist attacks are charged with conspiracy, murder in violation of the law of war, terrorism, and other charges. All six face the death penalty in military tribunals.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Six detainees charged for September 11

events.title: Roadside bomb ups U.S. death toll in Iraq
events.description: After a roadside bomb exploded in Baghdad, the U.S. death toll for the war in Iraq reached 4,000.
events.impact: Moderately negative impact: Roadside bomb ups U.S. death toll in Iraq

events.title: State Department renews controversial defense
events.description: The State Department renews a deal with Blackwater Worldwide, the private defense contractor whose guards killed 17 civilians in 2007, to provide defense for U.S. diplomats in the Middle East.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: State Department renews controversial defense

events.title: Congress overrides veto, pushes Farm Bill
events.description: The House and Senate override President Bush’s veto of the Farm Bill, a $307 billion bill which will provide subsidies to farmers. More than $10 billion of the funds will go to expanding nutritional programs such as food stamps. Bush originally vetoed the bill, which he felt to be excessive.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Congress overrides veto, pushes Farm Bill

events.title: Democrats nominate Obama
events.description: Democratic candidate Barack Obama secures the party’s nomination for the presidency.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Democrats nominate Obama

events.title: Intelligence Committee finds exaggerated evidence
events.description: The Senate Select Committee on Intelligence finds, after a five-year study, that President Bush and other officials greatly exaggerated the evidence showing that Saddam Hussein held weapons of mass destruction.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Intelligence Committee finds exaggerated evidence

events.title: Army admits inadequacies in Iraq
events.description: In a new report issued on the situation in Iraq, the U.S. Army admits that while it was able to adequately topple Hussein’s regime, it did not have the capability to rebuild Iraq into a fully-functioning new country.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Army admits inadequacies in Iraq

events.title: Iraqi military regains control
events.description: forces hand over control of Anbar Province to the Iraqi military and police, who will now be responsible for maintaining order there.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Iraqi military regains control

events.title: Federal takeover of Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac
events.description: government places federal mortgage lenders Fannie Mae (Federal National Mortgage Association) and Freddie Mac (Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation) under control of the U.S. Treasury to prevent the institutions from going under and endangering more than half of the country’s mortgages.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Federal takeover of Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac

events.title: Senate approves nuclear trade with India
events.description: Senate approves an end to the long-standing ban on trading nuclear fuels with India, who will be able to purchase fuel on the market as long as it is for civilian purposes.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Senate approves nuclear trade with India

events.title: Bush signs largest bailout in history
events.description: At the onset of financial crisis, President Bush signs a $700 billion bailout plan for failing bank assets, the largest in U.S
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush signs largest bailout in history

events.title: GDP drops
events.description: gross domestic product drops by 0.3 percent, the first time GDP has shrunk in 17 years.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: GDP drops

events.title: Petraeus takes over Central Command
events.description: General David Petraeus takes over as Head of Central Command, overseeing all U.S. military operations in Iraq, Afghanistan, Pakistan, Syria, and Iran.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Petraeus takes over Central Command

events.title: Barack Obama elected
events.description: Barack Obama is elected the next President of the United States in an historic election in which Democrats win in several traditionally Republican states and pick up seats in the House and Senate. Obama is the first black American elected President.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Barack Obama elected

events.title: Treasury, Federal Reserve agree to buy debt
events.description: The Treasury Department and the Federal Reserve agree to provide another $800 billion in lending programs to buy debt insured by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac and to provide more small loans to consumers.
events.impact: Stabilized the banking system

events.title: Interest rates set at 0%
events.description: Federal Reserve cuts interest rates to an all-time low of zero percent, down from 1 percent and 0.25 percent earlier in the year as part of a plan to stimulate the economy.
events.impact: Stabilized the banking system

events.title: Bush bails out GM, Chrysler
events.description: President Bush issues a $17.4-billion auto bailout to General Motors and Chrysler to keep the two American automotive giants from going bankrupt.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Bush bails out GM, Chrysler

events.title: Barack Obama inaugurated
events.description: Barack Obama is inaugurated the 44th President of the United States. President Bush leaves Washington, D.C. for Dallas, Texas.
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Barack Obama inaugurated

events.title: Obama wins election
events.description: Barack Obama is elected president by defeating Senator John McCain with nearly 53 percent of the popular vote and 365 electoral votes (out of 538).
events.impact: Mixed or neutral impact: Obama wins election

events.title: Inauguration
events.description: On January 20, 2009, Barack Obama was inaugurated as the 44th President of the United States. He was the first African American President in U.S. Obama emerged as an unlikely candidate during the 2008 presidential campaign. He was born and raised in Hawaii. His mother, a white American from Kansas, and his father, a black Kenyan studying in the United States, met as students at the University of Hawaii. After attending school in Hawaii, Obama moved to the mainland for college—first attending Occidental College in Los Angeles, California, and then graduating from Columbia University in New York City.
